# Maamarim Melukatim

## Part 3

The concept of *Tzimtzum* is that it is seemingly a paradox, yet in truth there is no contradiction. For while the *Ohr Ein Sof* is truly infinite, and *Tzimtzum* implies a removal or concealment of that light, the *Zohar* teaches that "there is no place void of Him." This is the fundamental *avodah* of the *Chossid*: to recognize that even within the *hester* of this world, the *Atzmus* of Hashem remains present. Through *tefillah* and *avodah*, one reveals this truth, transforming the *hester* into a vessel for *Elokus*. This is the ultimate purpose of *Seder Hishtalshelus*—that the lowest level of *Asiyah* should become a *dirah b'tachtonim* for the *Ribono shel Olam*.

## B’s”D. Shabbos Parshas Mishpatim, Parshas Shekalim, Mevarchim HaChodesh Adar 5731

**1.** "When you take the sum (Ki Sisa) of the heads of the Children of Israel according to their numbers, then each shall give a ransom for his soul..." It is stated in the Gemara that Moshe said before the Ribono shel Olam, "Master of the World, with what will the horn of Israel be exalted?" He replied, "With *Ki Sisa*." Rashi explains: "If you wish to raise their heads in exaltation, take from them a ransom for tzedakah." 

Seemingly, the connection between *Ki Sisa* and the exaltation of the horn of Israel is that *Ki Sisa* also connotes elevation and raising. Yet, we must understand: if Moshe asked the Ribono shel Olam *with what* the horn of Israel would be exalted, the answer should have been "by giving a ransom for tzedakah." What is the meaning of the answer "with *Ki Sisa*" (implying that the exaltation of the horn of Israel is achieved *through* *Ki Sisa*), when "raising the heads of the Children of Israel" and "exalting their horn" are one and the same? 

Furthermore, the verse states, "they shall *give* (v'nasnu) each a ransom for his soul," implying that the raising (the lifting and exaltation) of the head of Israel is through the *giving* of a ransom. Yet Rashi says, "If you wish to raise their heads in exaltation, *take* (kach) from them a ransom for tzedakah." 

Finally, we must understand why the verse says "take the *head* (rosh) of the Children of Israel," while Moshe’s question was "with what will the *horn* (keren) of Israel be exalted?"

**2.** The concept is explained in the *drushim* (Maamarim on *Ki Sisa*): The "head" (*rosh*) is the natural love that transcends intellect. It is written, "For my iniquities have passed over my head," meaning that iniquities conceal the level of the "head" of the *neshamah* (the natural love that transcends intellect) so that it does not shine in revelation within the internal faculties. 

"Take the head..." means that although there is no flaw in the level of the "head" itself, the very fact that the "head" of the *neshamah* cannot shine in revelation within the internal faculties indicates that it has undergone a descent and degradation due to iniquities (though this descent and degradation is not in the "head" itself, but only in its revelation). Therefore, one must "take" (lift) the head, to raise it from its degradation.

**Note:** In *Likkutei Torah* (on *Shuvah Yisrael*), it is explained that this is like a person who stumbles, causing his head to fall downward. Accordingly, one could say that "take the head of the Children of Israel" means to lift the head from its downward fall. The reason the aforementioned *drushim* do not interpret it this way is that this *parshah* was said after Yom Kippur, after the Ribono shel Olam had been appeased by Israel with joy and a whole heart, saying to Moshe, "I have forgiven according to your word." The impression of the sin that remained after Yom Kippur was (primarily) only that the will transcending intellect did not shine in revelation within the internal faculties; therefore, it says "take the head of the Children of Israel."

**3.** It is written, "He has cut down in fierce anger every horn of Israel," meaning that through iniquities, a descent occurs even in the level of the "horn" (*keren*) that is above the "head." Therefore, Moshe asked, "With what will the horn of Israel be exalted?"—meaning that the "horn" above the "head" must be raised and exalted. 

It is explained in *Likkutei Torah* that the "horn" is the level of *Keter*. We must clarify: if the "horn" is above the "head," it is (not only above the intellect in the head but) even above the skull. According to the known principle that the *galgalta* (the skull covering the brain) is the level of *Keter*, what is the superiority of the "horn" over the "head"? 

The explanation is that the *galgalta* is a *makif* (encompassing light) that relates to the *pnimi* (internal), similar to the skull in a human which encompasses the brain but relates to it. The "horn," however, is a *makif* that is above any relation to the *pnimi*. As explained in many places regarding the verse "And He shall exalt the horn of His people," this refers to the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above the *sovev*. Similarly, regarding the *neshamah*, the "horn" of the *neshamah* is the *etzem* (essence) of the *neshamah*.

We must understand Moshe’s question, "With what will the horn of Israel be exalted?" Seemingly, how can there be a descent in the *etzem* of the *neshamah*? The explanation is that the descent of the *neshamah* below is in order to act within the body. Therefore, when the purpose is not fulfilled, a descent occurs in all its levels, including its *etzem* (which descended below). 

Accordingly, the descent caused by sin in the level of the "head" of the *neshamah* occurs because, relative to this level, the revealed faculties have value (since it is a *makif* that relates to the *pnimi*). Thus, the matters of the revealed faculties cause a change in it (though the change is only regarding its revelation, as mentioned in section 2). The descent caused by sin in the *etzem* of the *neshamah* is not that the matters of the revealed faculties cause a change in it, but rather that the purpose for which it descended below was not fulfilled. 

This is the meaning of "He has *cut down*... the horn of Israel." Just as with a literal horn, the change that occurs when it is cut from the head (that it lacks the strength it had initially) is not that the *head* causes a change in the horn, but rather that it is *cut off* from the head—so too with the "horn" of the *neshamah*: the change caused by sin is because it is not acting within the revealed faculties (and is not fulfilling the purpose of its descent below).

**4.** Accordingly, we can say that when the Ribono shel Olam said to Moshe "take the head of the Children of Israel," there are two matters: that he should take and lift the *head* of the Children of Israel, as explicitly stated in the verse. And another matter (which is not explicit in the Written Torah but was revealed in the Oral Torah): that through "take the head of the Children of Israel," there will also be an exaltation of the "horn" of Israel (as the Gemara says, "He said to him *with* Ki Sisa," meaning that *through* Ki Sisa there will be an additional matter). 

This explains why Rashi says, "If you wish to raise their heads in exaltation, *take* from them a ransom for tzedakah," and not "they shall *give* a ransom for tzedakah," as the verse says "they shall *give* each a ransom for his soul." The raising and lifting of the *head* of the Children of Israel is through the *giving* of a ransom—"they shall give each a ransom for his soul." But for the raising of the head to lead to the exaltation of the *horn* of Israel, the raising and lifting of the head must be through *Moshe taking* the ransom from them—"If you wish to raise their heads in exaltation, *take* from them a ransom for tzedakah."

**5.** This can be understood by prefacing what is written (in our *parshah*): "If you lend money to My people..." The Midrash says: "The Ribono shel Olam said: 'If you lend money to My people' (*ammi*—with a *chirik* under the *ayin*)." It is explained in the *drushim* that the reason the merit of *gemilus chasadim* (acts of kindness) is so great that through it you are "My people" (*ammi*), "My people in My company," is as it is written, "For I desire kindness and not sacrifice." 

Although the sacrifices (*zevach*) are also His will (as is understood from the many commandments to offer sacrifices), the purpose of sacrifices is an *ala'ah* (ascent) from below to above. The ultimate intent, however, is that there be an *hemshech* (drawing down) of the revelation of *Elokus* below, for the Ribono shel Olam desired to have a dwelling place in the lower worlds. Therefore, the desire for sacrifices is (not for the sacrifices themselves, but) for the *hemshech* that will follow through the *nesachim* (libations). (As the Gemara says, "Whoever recites the *Shema* without *tefillin* is as if he offered a burnt offering without a meal offering, and a sacrifice without libations," which implies that when one offers a sacrifice without libations, his sacrifice is nothing. *Likkutei Torah* explains that this is because the *ala'ah* of the sacrifices is for the sake of the *hemshech* that follows through the libations.) 

Thus, the desire for sacrifices is (like) the external aspect of the will (similar to a person who wants to travel to a place to buy merchandise; his desire in the trip is only for the profit he will gain from it), while the internal aspect of the will is in the *hemshech* below. This is "For I desire kindness and not sacrifice"—the *chefetz* (desire), the internal aspect of the will, is that there be kindness, an *hemshech* below.

**To add:** The plain meaning of the verse "For I desire kindness and not sacrifice" implies that it speaks even of a sacrifice with libations, yet it says "I desire kindness and not sacrifice." This is because since the libations are secondary to the sacrifice, even the *hemshech* through the libations is not like the *hemshech* through kindness. This is also proven by the Sages (quoted in the *drushim*) on the verse "Can the iniquity of the house of Eli be atoned for by sacrifice and meal offering? By sacrifice and meal offering it is not atoned, but it is atoned for by Torah and *gemilus chasadim*." This means that even the atonement of the meal offering (and similarly libations) is not like the atonement through Torah and *gemilus chasadim*, because the general concept of sacrifices is *ala'ah*. While within the sacrifices themselves, the meal offering and libations are an *hemshech* below, the *hemshech* through them is not like the *hemshech* through Torah and *gemilus chasadim*. 

Furthermore, even the *hemshech* of Torah, which is called "Torah of kindness" because its concept is an *hemshech* below, is not like the *hemshech* through kindness (*gemilus chasadim*). As the Sages conclude: "Rabbah, who occupied himself with Torah, lived forty years; Abaye, who occupied himself with Torah and *gemilus chasadim*, lived sixty years." Note that this concept—that the *hemshech* through *gemilus chasadim* is an *hemshech* further below (than the *hemshech* through meal offerings and libations, and even than the *hemshech* through Torah)—applies generally to all *mitzvos*, for *gemilus chasadim* is the totality of all *mitzvos*. Nevertheless, the primary merit of the *hemshech* below is in *gemilus chasadim* in its literal sense. This is also understood from the continuation of the *drushim*, that "I desire kindness and not sacrifice" explains the merit of "If you lend money," *gemilus chasadim* in its literal sense. This is also understood from what is explained in the *drushim* regarding why *gemilus chasadim* is one of the things whose fruits a person eats in this world, because the *hemshech* through *gemilus chasadim* is drawn down to the lowest level, even in this world.

**6.** The *Yalkut* says on the verse "For I desire kindness and not sacrifice": "Once, Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai was walking in Jerusalem, and Rabbi Yehoshua was walking behind him. He saw the Beis Hamikdash in ruins. He said, 'Woe to us that the place where our iniquities were atoned for is destroyed!' He said to him, 'My son, do not be distressed, for we have another atonement that is like it. And what is it? *Gemilus chasadim*.' Therefore it is said, 'For I desire kindness and not sacrifice,' and it says, 'I have said, the world shall be built by kindness.'" 

The *drushim* point out: *Gemilus chasadim* existed even when the Beis Hamikdash was standing! The expression "we *have* another atonement" implies that this atonement was innovated during the exile. It explains there, based on the Gemara: "Rabbi Yochanan said, 'There are six things whose fruits a person eats in this world, and the principal remains for him in the World to Come' (and *iyun tefillah*—devotion in prayer—is one of the six)." The Gemara asks, "But we learned [in a *Mishnah*] that these are the things... and it lists only three things!" It answers, "These are also included in those." Rashi explains that *iyun tefillah* is included in *gemilus chasadim*, as it is written, "A man of kindness (*ish chesed*) rewards his own soul (*gomeil nafsho*)" (and "soul" means prayer, as it is written regarding Chana, "And I poured out my soul before Hashem"). 

The *drushim* explain that when Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai said "we have another atonement... *gemilus chasadim*," he meant *iyun tefillah*, which is included in *gemilus chasadim* (for the essence of prayer—the three daily prayers and the text of the prayer—was instituted during the time of the exile). Although prayers are in place of sacrifices, and sacrifices are an *ala'ah* from below to above, the *drushim* explain that "prayers were instituted corresponding to the *temidin* and *musafin*." The *temidin* and *musafin*, the communal sacrifices, are a level of *hemshech*. As the Midrash says, the reason Israel is called "My beloved" (*ra'yasi*) is because they would offer two *temidin* on the altar, and through this they are "My beloved," "My provider" (*parnasati*), for Israel provides for their Father in Heaven, as they draw down the *Ohr Ein Sof* into the *Sefiros*. 

The verse "For I desire kindness and not sacrifice" refers to individual sacrifices, which are a level of *ala'ah*, but communal sacrifices, *temidin* and *musafin*, are a level of *hemshech*. As the *Shach* writes in his commentary on the Torah on the verse "besides the morning burnt offering," that "besides" (*milvad*) is an acronym for "lending to a poor person in his time of distress" (*milveh le'ani b'sha'as dachko*). The meaning of "besides the morning burnt offering" is that lending to a poor person in his time of distress is like the morning burnt offering, the *tamid* sacrifice, which is an *hemshech* from above to below. From the fact that prayers were instituted corresponding to the *temidin*, it is understood that prayers are an *hemshech* from above to below.

**7.** From the fact that *gemilus chasadim* (in its literal sense) existed even during the time of the Beis Hamikdash, whereas the *gemilus chasadim* of prayer (*gomeil nafsho ish chesed*) was instituted specifically after the destruction of the Beis Hamikdash, so that there would now be an atonement similar to the atonement that was through the Beis Hamikdash—the place where our iniquities are atoned for—it is understood that there is an advantage in the atonement achieved through the *gemilus chasadim* of prayer over the atonement through *gemilus chasadim* in its literal sense. 

The concept is that prayer (in general) is an *ala'ah* from below to above, as is also understood from "And I poured out my soul before Hashem" (for this verse is used as proof that prayer is an *hemshech*). The pouring out of the soul is an *ala'ah*, but the request of this *ala'ah* is that there be an *hemshech* below. (To add: the *drushim* explain that in "A man of kindness rewards (*gomeil*) his own soul," there are two interpretations: *gomeil* from the root of separation—as in "the child grew and was weaned" (*vayigamel*)—and *gomeil* from the root of influence/bestowal, as in *gemilus chasadim*. *Gomeil nafsho* from the root of influence is in the *avodah* of prayer that comes from the *Nefesh Elokis*, where the *ala'ah* (pouring out of the soul) is for the sake of the *hemshech*. *Gomeil nafsho* from the root of separation is in the offering of the *Nefesh HaBahamis* in prayer, to separate and exit from the matters of the world and to be in a state of *bitul*—an *ala'ah*. In the *avodah* that comes from the *Nefesh HaBahamis*, the *ala'ah* is not for the sake of an *hemshech*, but that this itself is its request. It can be said that the fact that the prayer of the *Nefesh Elokis* is called *gomeil nafsho*, where *gomeil* is also a term for separation, indicates that even in the prayer of the *Nefesh Elokis* there is an *ala'ah* similar to the *ala'ah* of the *Nefesh HaBahamis*, where the request is the *ala'ah* itself.)

It is known that in forgiveness and atonement there are two matters: 1) That through the *hemshech* from the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, the Master of the Will who is not compelled by the will, the sins and flaws are nullified. 2) Another matter in forgiveness and atonement: that through the *hemshech* from the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, the Master of the Will, a new *will* is drawn down (which is above the will that was removed by the sin), and through this, one becomes "acceptable" before Him, may He be blessed, as before the sin. 

The difference between these two matters is: the fact that through the *hemshech* from the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* the flaws are nullified is because He is not compelled by the will, He is *distinct* from the will. The fact that a new will is drawn down from Him is (not because He is *distinct* from the will, but on the contrary) because He is the *Master* of the Will. At the level of the will, there is a limitation in the manner of its *hemshech*, and when one does the opposite of the will, it is removed. Not so with the *Master* of the Will; He is not limited in the manner of the *hemshech*, and therefore, even after one has done the opposite of the will, which caused the will to be removed, it is possible for a new will to be drawn down from Him.

It can be said that what was brought above (section 5)—that the essence of atonement is *gemilus chasadim* because the internal aspect of the will above is in the *hemshech* below, "I desire kindness"—is primarily regarding becoming "acceptable" before Him, may He be blessed, and also regarding the *repair* of the flaws. But regarding the *nullification* of the flaws (for this matter is because the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, the Master of the Will, is distinct from the will), there is an advantage in the *hemshech* that relates to *ala'ah*. Through *ala'ah* (exiting from one's existence, *bitul*), one reaches this matter of the Master of the Will being distinct from the will. And through the *hemshech* that follows the *ala'ah*, this matter (that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is distinct from the will) is drawn down below, and through this, the flaws are nullified.

**8.** This is "Take the head of the Children of Israel... and it is stated in the Gemara that Moshe said before the Ribono shel Olam, 'Master of the World, with what will the horn of Israel be exalted?' He replied, 'With *Ki Sisa*.'" It can be said that the two matters in *Ki Sisa*—the taking and lifting of the "head" of the Children of Israel and the exaltation of the "horn" of Israel—are similar to the two matters of *gemilus chasadim*: *gemilus chasadim* in its literal sense and the *gemilus chasadim* of prayer. 

The weakness (descent and degradation) caused by sin in the "head" of the *neshamah*, that it is unable to shine in the internal faculties, is similar to the removal of the light caused by the flaw, where the light is removed to its source and does not shine in the vessels. The matter of taking and lifting the "head" (that the power of revelation of the level of the "head" in the *neshamah* is as strong as it was initially, to shine in the internal faculties) through giving a ransom for tzedakah is similar to the atonement (filling the flaws and returning the will) through *gemilus chasadim* in its literal sense. 

Moshe’s wonder was, "With what will the *horn* of Israel be exalted?" Since the descent in the level of the "horn" is (not that the sin caused a descent in it, but) that the purpose for which it descended below was not fulfilled, Moshe wondered how it is possible to repair this. It can be said that Moshe saying "before the Ribono shel Olam" "with what will the horn of Israel be exalted" is because to repair the fact that the purpose was not fulfilled is through the *hemshech* from the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, the Master of the Will who is *distinct* from the will. Therefore, Moshe said this "before the Holy One, blessed be He," who is holy and distinct, and furthermore, "before" the Ribono shel Olam, in order to draw down from there. He replied to him, "With *Ki Sisa*," that through *Moshe* taking their heads in exaltation—for even the *hemshech* of the level of "before the Ribono shel Olam" must be through the level of *Chochma* (the concept of Moshe), similar to the known principle that even the revelation of the *Sovev* is through the *Memale*. Although the action of Moshe (from his own perspective) is "take the head," the power of the level of "before the Ribono shel Olam" is also drawn down in this, through which the exaltation of the "horn" of Israel is achieved.

For this matter to be drawn down to Israel, it is through their head being taken and lifted by *Moshe taking* a ransom from them, as Rashi says, "*Take* from them a ransom for tzedakah"—the matter of the *ala'ah* in tzedakah. Similar to what was explained above (section 7), that through *gemilus chasadim* in its literal sense is the *hemshech* of the *will* from the Master of the Will, and to reach this matter of the Master of the Will being *distinct* from the will is through the *gemilus chasadim* of prayer, an *hemshech* that relates to *ala'ah*—so too regarding the ransom for his soul: through the *giving* of the ransom for tzedakah (an *hemshech*) is the *hemshech* of the will, and through this, the taking and lifting of the head is achieved. And through *Moshe taking* from them a ransom for tzedakah (the matter of the *ala'ah* in tzedakah) is the *hemshech* of the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is distinct from the will, and through this, the horn of Israel will be exalted, in the manner of "and his horn shall be exalted in honor."

## Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Mishpatim, Mevarchim HaChodesh Adar Rishon – In his room – 5738

“And these are the ordinances which you shall set before them.” The Alter Rebbe notes in Torah Or at the beginning of our Parsha that the word “before them” (*lifnehem*) implies the plural, yet “you shall set” (*tasim*) is singular; it should have said “you shall set” (*tasimu*). Furthermore, the well-known question is asked: why does the entire section of ordinances begin specifically with the law of the Hebrew slave? (This is also the intent of the Midrash regarding the verse, “For I bought you in Egypt, etc.,” to explain the reason for beginning “And these are the ordinances” with the law of the Hebrew slave). There are several other points of precision regarding this verse, as explained in the discourses of our Rebbes, his successors.

The matter is as follows: The phrase “And these are the ordinances” is a general concept, as is understood from the words of our Sages that it refers to the entire subject of Torah study. This is also the meaning of “you shall set” (singular), which refers to Moshe, for the essence of Moshe is the concept of Torah. Subsequently, it says “before them” (plural), meaning that through him, the Torah was drawn down to every single Jew until the end of all generations, as it is written, “Remember the Torah of Moshe My servant,” reaching even the child beginning to speak, whom his father teaches Torah. This is the verse, “The Torah that Moshe commanded us.” Likewise, there is a general instruction in this verse regarding the manner of Torah study; it must be in a specific way, as our Sages state regarding “you shall set before them”: that the teacher who has taught must repeat the matter even several times until they understand the depth of the *halacha* and its reasoning well. The Alter Rebbe adds in the conclusion of his *Hilchos Talmud Torah* that you must set before them the reasoning that settles their study and places it in their hearts. (It can be said that when the Alter Rebbe says “places it in their hearts,” he is interpreting “before them” as “before their inwardness,” for that occurs when one places it in the heart.) This is a general concept in Torah study for every Jew (since Torah study is a *mitzvah* of the body, which everyone is obligated in). As it is written, “And you shall teach them to your children,” and our Sages state that the words of Torah should be sharp and honed in your mouth (which is like the aforementioned concept that the *halacha* must be clear and organized, etc.). This is an obligation upon every Jew (for in addition to the obligation of “teaching your children,” there must be the sharpening of words of Torah in the study of the father (and teacher) himself; on the contrary, specifically through his own sharpening of the words of Torah can he then teach his children). Based on this, we can understand why “And these are the ordinances which you shall set before them” appears immediately after the Parsha of the Giving of the Torah, for in this verse there is a general instruction regarding the manner of Torah study for everyone (both in one’s own study, and in “teaching your children” literally, and “your children” refers to students, as our Sages state regarding the verse) according to the aforementioned words of our Sages, and primarily regarding the manner of a person’s *avodah* to his Creator.

The text continues to explain the details of the *avodah* in order from the easier to the more difficult, for the *avodah* must be in the manner of “little by little I will drive them out.” The beginning of the *avodah* is with the verse, “If you buy a Hebrew slave, etc.,” for this includes two categories of *avodah*: the level of a Canaanite slave and the level of a Hebrew slave. This is understood from what is found in the *Mechilta* and cited in Rashi on the Torah (from which it is understood that this is also according to the literal meaning) that a “Hebrew slave” means a slave who is a Hebrew; or perhaps it is only the slave of a Hebrew (a Canaanite slave whom he bought from a Jew)? The verse teaches, etc. The conclusion is that it refers to a slave who is a Hebrew. Nevertheless, there is an initial assumption (even in the literal meaning, as mentioned) that it refers to a Canaanite slave, and a special teaching is required to exclude this premise. From this, it is understood that there is room to say (as an initial premise) that it also refers to a Canaanite slave. In this (the level of a Canaanite slave) is the beginning of the *avodah*, and afterwards one ascends in his *avodah* and reaches the level of a Hebrew slave, and until he reaches the level of a Hebrew maidservant, as follows.

The matter is as explained in the discourses at length (especially in the discourse that the Tzemach Tzedek writes in *Ohr HaTorah* that this discourse is from our great Rebbe, and it is a very esteemed discourse, and it concerns the levels of *tzaddik*, *rasha*, and *beinoni* mentioned in *Tanya*): there are three levels in the human soul: Canaanite slave, Hebrew slave, and Hebrew maidservant. Their essence is the three levels of *nefesh, ruach, neshamah*. It is explained there that they correspond to *tzaddik*, *rasha*, and *beinoni*. This is the order of *avodah*: first is the *avodah* of the level of a Canaanite slave, then a Hebrew slave, and a Hebrew maidservant. As it is found in the *Zohar* in *Saba D’Mishpatim*: when a person is born, they give him a *nefesh*, etc.; if he merits more, they give him a *ruach*, etc.; if he merits more, they give him a *neshamah*. Immediately at the time of birth, the level of *nefesh* (among the levels of the soul) is drawn to everyone, and from the perspective of this level, the *avodah* is in the category of *nefesh*. Afterwards, through his *avodah*, he ascends to the level of *ruach*, which is more elevated, until he reaches the level of *neshamah* (and the *avodah* that corresponds to these levels), as the details of the differences in the *avodah* of *nefesh, ruach, neshamah* are explained in *Kuntres HaAvodah*.

Regarding the explanation of the Canaanite slave, there is a discussion in the *Hagahos V’He'aros* and *Biurim* of the Tzemach Tzedek in *Ohr HaTorah* whether this is the level of *rasha v’tov lo* or the *beinoni* mentioned in *Tanya*. He concludes there that both are true. Accordingly, the level of a Canaanite slave refers to both a *rasha* and a *beinoni*. Seemingly, this requires clarification: how is it possible that even one who is at the level of a slave—for “a slave prefers licentiousness” (his primary desire and pleasure is in licentiousness, etc., and he follows the stubbornness of his heart, and what he does and works is only against his will due to the fear of judgment, lest he be struck with the rod, as explained at length in the discourses)—can be at the level of a *beinoni* (and fulfill the oath to be a *beinoni*)? For the *beinoni* has never committed a sin and never will, as explained in *Tanya*, and as is known the saying of the early Chassidim, “If only one could be a *beinoni*.” However, the matter is that being a *beinoni* is within the capacity of everyone (including one who “prefers licentiousness”), as is understood from the fact that on the title page of *Sefer Shel Beinonim* (which contains the essence of the book), the Alter Rebbe writes (and it is known that the Alter Rebbe also wrote the title page) that “it is *very* close to you, in your mouth and in your heart to do it.” Inside the *Tanya*, he writes further that the measure of the *beinoni* is the measure of *every person* (meaning that even from the perspective of measurement and limitation—for the concept of a “measure” is only applicable when there is a boundary—the level of the *beinoni* is according to the capacity and measure of *every person*, for whoever he may be among the Jewish people (every person), he is included in “You are called *adam*,” which refers to “I will be like the Most High”). Furthermore (as he continues in *Tanya* there), “and after every person he will draw” (in this word “draw,” there are two interpretations—as in “you shall love”—a command and a promise for the future). The reason for this is that even that which he “prefers licentiousness” (as mentioned) is only in the externality, but his inwardness—the measure of every person and “after every person he will draw”—is that he is literally a part of *Elokus* from above. As the well-known ruling of the Rambam states: when they coerce him (because he prefers another matter) and he says “I want to,” this is his true will (for what he “prefers in licentiousness” is not *him*, but his *yetzer* forcing him). Certainly, his inwardness will come into actuality, as mentioned (that “he will draw” is a future tense and a promise), “in your mouth and in your heart to do it,” in thought, speech, and action, including the fulfillment of the oath that they adjure him (at least “do not be a *rasha*” (but a *beinoni*)), as in *Tanya* (for this is also the meaning of “after every person he will draw,” that the person (from the perspective of his soul) is drawn to be a *beinoni*, which takes him out of whatever state or situation he may be in his *avodah*). For certainly (even if he needs to do *teshuvah*), he will eventually do *teshuvah*, as the promise, “that no one cast away be banished from Him” (on the contrary, through *teshuvah* he will come to a more elevated level, as the saying, “In the place where *baalei teshuvah* stand, even perfect *tzaddikim* cannot stand”). Thus, from the perspective of his inwardness, he is always *very* close to this (to be in your mouth and in your heart to do it, in thought, speech, and action—as a *beinoni*). Based on this, it is understood in our case that even though a Canaanite slave “prefers licentiousness,” nevertheless his level is that of a *beinoni*, for ultimately he will certainly say “I want to” (from the perspective of his inwardness), for “no one cast away be banished,” etc.

This possibility of an initial premise in Torah that “perhaps it is only the slave of a Hebrew,” but he himself is a Canaanite slave—meaning that the level of *avodah* is that of a Canaanite slave who “prefers licentiousness”—is only from the perspective of the descent of the soul below, that it descended from a high mountain to a deep pit. (And even then, there is no premise that he is a Canaanite slave entirely, *chas v’shalom*, but rather “the (Canaanite) slave of a Hebrew,” for there is upon him the influence and lordship of a “Hebrew.” For even one who is at the lowest level among the Jewish people (at the level of a Canaanite slave, from the perspective of the descent of the soul below), nevertheless, because he is a son of Avraham Avinu (for “Avraham was one,” Avraham *the Hebrew*), he possesses the hidden love (and fear) as an inheritance, as explained at length in *Tanya*. This love and fear causes everyone (even if it seems to him that he “prefers licentiousness,” that his desire is for other matters, even matters that are the opposite of what is desired, *chas v’shalom*) that he *must* do as the Almighty wills. Therefore, it is impossible that he be a Canaanite entirely, *chas v’shalom*, but rather a Canaanite slave of a Hebrew, who is outwardly at the level of a Canaanite slave, but there is found within him (in concealment) the hidden love inherited from Avraham the Hebrew). The intent of this descent—that there is an initial premise in Torah that “perhaps it is only the slave of a Hebrew”—is only so that through his *avodah* in “the verse teaches”—learning Torah in a way that he learns from it what the will of the Torah is—he will immediately see and reveal his truth that “here too, it is a slave who is a Hebrew,” and that he himself is a Hebrew (and not that he is a Canaanite and only has the lordship of a Hebrew). This means that his place and level is at the level of “the other side of the river,” and also as it is found in the Midrash that Avraham is called Avraham the Hebrew because the entire world was on one side and he was on the other. So too, he acts in his *avodah* to be a Hebrew (slave), that he stands against the entire world, starting from the concealment and hiding (the word *olam* from the word *helem*) of his animal soul, and he transforms it, and goes and ascends from level to level, from the level of a Canaanite slave (whom he bought from a Jew) to the level of a slave who is a Hebrew, and afterwards also to the level of a Hebrew maidservant, until he acts in his *avodah* that he or his son will have it, as explained at length in the aforementioned esteemed discourse of our great Rebbe.

The general concept mentioned above is also present in the *avodah* of everyone every day. The matter is that the level of a Canaanite slave is when Jews are in the state of being clothed in the affairs of the world in order to refine them, as it is written, “And you shall do all your work,” that there must be work in the affairs of this physical world and also in the materiality of the world in order to transform it into holiness. From this state of being clothed in the affairs of the world, through the refinement, it is possible that an undesirable matter from the thing being refined will attach itself to the refiner, as it is found in *Tanya*, and through this, it is possible that it will appear at the beginning of the *avodah* as the level of a Canaanite slave. However, this descent is for the sake of an ascent, and therefore it is only for a moment, and immediately afterwards is the beginning of the ascent. Furthermore, through the descent, an ascent is achieved with greater strength and power, similar to what is explained in *Tanya* regarding the matter that distracts a person in his prayer: that from the distraction itself, one must be aroused to pray with more *kavanah* from the depths of the heart. This is when he contemplates that since the Almighty is the one who gives life to the matter that distracts him, it is certain that the fact that they showed him (from above) this distraction, and the distraction is found within his four cubits, and even distracts him in his prayer, is (not because it is an independent matter, *chas v’shalom*, but) only so that the person, through his *avodah*, will make from the distraction “reasons” (plural), first through the *avodah* of *iskafya* (the general *avodah* of the *beinonim*), until he reaches also the level of *isahpcha* (the general *avodah* of the *tzaddikim*), as explained in *Tanya*. Similarly, in the aforementioned matter, from the descent itself, one must be aroused with greater strength to perform his *avodah* at the level of a Canaanite slave, Hebrew slave, and Hebrew maidservant, until he reaches the point that he or his son will have it, until “she shall go out free without money” (for all this is the general *avodah* of *iskafya* and *isahpcha* mentioned above), as explained at length in the aforementioned discourse.

Through the aforementioned *avodah* of “six years he shall serve”—where “serve” refers to the general *avodah* of a person to his Creator—one reaches the seventh (he shall go out free without money), which refers to the most elevated perfection that will be in the future, a day that is entirely Shabbos and rest for eternal life, when there will be a revelation of *Elokus* below, a dwelling place for Him, blessed be He, in the lower realms (and a taste of this revelation shines even now on Shabbos, and especially during the time of *Ratzon Kol Ratzon*).

This can be connected to the subject of the *Mishkan* explained in Parshas Terumah (the Parsha of the coming week, which we read on Shabbos during the time of *Mincha*, for then it is already the beginning of the connection to *Motzaei Shabbos*), whose purpose is that through it, “I will dwell among them.” Primarily, this indwelling was through the Ark (which is why the command for the making of the Ark precedes all other matters of the *Mishkan*, for the inwardness and ultimate intent of the entire *Mishkan* was (the indwelling of the *Shechinah* in) the Ark, as explained at length in Ramban on the Torah. This is similar to the interpretation of the Midrash cited above, that the reason it began “And these are the ordinances” specifically with the law of the Hebrew slave is because in this *mitzvah* is hinted the inner point in the *avodah* of the Jewish people—that “I was created to serve my Creator,” and as explained in the Midrash there that “I bought you in Egypt,” etc. Similarly, in the actual *avodah*, one first prioritizes the ultimate goal of the *avodah*, which is the drawing down of the *Shechinah* through the Ark), through which it is drawn down to make for Him, blessed be He, a dwelling place in the lower realms. For through the fact that first it is “I will dwell among them,” within everyone, then afterwards it is drawn down to be “I will dwell among them” literally, in the *Mishkan* and in the *Beis HaMikdash*, and eventually in the entire world (and this will be in perfection in the seventh, in the seventh millennium). The *avodah* to effect the drawing down of the *Shechinah* is the making of the Ark, which is the concept of Torah, through which the *Shechinah* is afterwards drawn down also into the world, for “a *mitzvah* is a lamp and the Torah is light,” for the Torah illuminates and shows the path of every Jew to know the deed that they shall do and those that they shall not do. First, one draws down the indwelling of the *Shechinah* into the gold, silver, and copper, etc., of the *Mishkan*, and from the *Mishkan* and the *Mikdash*, light goes out to the world, until the world is filled with this light in a revealed manner, as the saying, “In the future, Jerusalem will expand throughout the entire Land of Israel, and in the future, the Land of Israel will expand throughout all the lands in the true and complete Redemption.” This Redemption depends on our deeds and our *avodah* during the six thousand years of the world’s existence, and especially in the *avodah* during the time of exile, and particularly in the *avodah* at the conclusion and seal of the time of exile in the footsteps of *Mashiach* (and everything follows the seal), through which we bring the Redemption, when the *Beis HaMikdash* will be built in its place in a revealed manner and literally, and with the elevation that will be in the Third *Beis HaMikdash*, “the Sanctuary, O Lord, which Your hands have established,” which is higher even than the First and Second *Beis HaMikdash* and also higher than the elevation that was in the *Mishkan*. For although the *Mishkan* was the work of the hands of Moshe, and the work of the hands of Moshe is eternal, as it is found in the Gemara, and because of this the *Mishkan* is also eternal, nevertheless, through the *avodah* of the Jewish people during the time of exile, there will be (in the Third *Beis HaMikdash*) an elevation that is higher even than the level of the *Mishkan* (similar to the elevation of *Mashiach* over Moshe Rabbeinu), which will be the elevation of the *avodah* of *teshuvah*, that from the concealment and hiding of the world itself, one makes a dwelling place for Him, blessed be He, in the lower realms. As is known, the essence of *Mashiach* is the concept of *teshuvah*, as the saying, “*Mashiach* comes to bring the *tzaddikim* to *teshuvah*.” “And a shoot shall come forth from the stem of Yishai, and a branch shall grow from his roots,” until “the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea,” in the true and complete Redemption.

Through the aforementioned *avodah*, we hasten, urge, and speed up its coming even more, that it should come with the greatest possible haste in our days literally, below ten *tefachim*. It will be seen in a revealed manner that “nations shall walk to your light,” and “kings shall be your nursing fathers and their queens your nursing mothers.” Likewise, this will be for us even in the final days during the time of exile; for even though “we are still servants of Achashverosh,” nevertheless even then “the fear of the Jews fell upon them,” until Achashverosh himself gave the house of Haman to Queen Esther. This is a close preparation for the fulfillment of the prophecy that “then I will turn to the peoples a clear language, etc.,” in the true and complete Redemption through *Mashiach Tzidkeinu*.

## Shabbos Parshas Mishpatim, Parshas Shekalim, Mevarchim HaChodesh Adar, 5740

"This shall they give, everyone who passes among those who are counted: a half-shekel by the holy shekel—the shekel is twenty gerah—a half-shekel as a contribution to Hashem," and it concludes, "to make atonement for your souls." In the maamar of Shabbos Parshas Mishpatim and Parshas Shekalim 5780 (Parshas Terumah), it is noted that the concept of "half" is repeated: it says "a half-shekel by the holy shekel," and then repeats "a half-shekel as a contribution to Hashem." Furthermore, the concept of "the shekel is twenty gerah" requires explanation, for one is only required to give a half-shekel. Also requiring explanation is the statement "to make atonement for your souls." As is further explained there, the concept of the half-shekel is to atone for the sin of the Golden Calf, and it is difficult to understand what the virtue of the half-shekel is that it can atone for such a sin. It should be added (as a note in Chassidic thought) that the sin of the Golden Calf was a general sin, and it is the source and root of all sins, iniquities, and transgressions. As it is stated in the Gemara, at the time of the Giving of the Torah, their filth (which the serpent injected into Eve) ceased, and after the sin of the Golden Calf, it returned. And even though it did not return entirely to the state it was in before, for even after the sin of the Golden Calf, the concept of choice remained with the Jewish people—"And in you He chose"—in the material body, which in its materiality resembles the bodies of the nations of the world (and see the Gemara there regarding why the nations of the world are filthy, etc.), nevertheless, the sin of the Golden Calf is the general sin, and concerning it, it is written, "And on the day I make an accounting (for every sin), I will make an accounting, etc." Accordingly, it requires further explanation: what is the virtue of the half-shekel that it atones for this sin? Now, from this contribution (of the half-shekel), the communal offerings were purchased throughout the year. The concept of the offerings now is the concept of tefillah, as our Sages say, "They instituted tefillos in place of the offerings." From this, it is understood that the concept of the half-shekel also exists in the avodah of tefillah.

2) To understand all of this, the maamar proceeds to explain the concept of tefillah, and brings the statement of our Sages, "One should only stand to pray with a heavy head (koved rosh)." It should be added to this, based on what is written in Tosafos in Maseches Menachos, that the phrasing "One should only... unless..." comes to exclude that it is invalid after the fact (b'dieved). And this is the simple meaning of the matter: "One should not stand to pray" means that the concept of tefillah is not relevant, except with a heavy head. It brings the interpretation of the Maggid of Mezeritch that the tefillah should be about the "heaviness" in the "Head." For when one prays for a lack, one should not pray for his own lack, but rather for the lack Above, because there is no lack below that does not have a corresponding lack Above. It explains that the concept of the lack Above is as our Sages say, "When a person suffers, what does the Shechinah say, as it were? 'I am lighter than My head, I am lighter than My arm.'" For the Shechinah is the level of Malchus, which has ascents and descents, etc. This is the concept of the "heavy head" of the Shechinah, and this is "One should only stand to pray with a heavy head"—meaning, about the heaviness and the lack in the "Head." Afterwards, it brings the interpretation of our Alter Rebbe (and so it is also in the maamar "Ein Omdin L'hitpallel" by the Rebbe Rayatz) that "Head" refers to the souls of Israel. "Israel is My head." In this, there is the concept of the heavy head that comes through sins, as it is written, "For my iniquities have passed over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me."

This is "One should only stand to pray with a heavy head." Through tefillah, not only does one nullify the heavy head, but furthermore, it becomes "Take a census (lit. lift the head) of the congregation of the children of Israel... by their skulls," (as explained in Likkutei Torah, beginning of Parshas Bamidbar), that one elevates the "head" to the level of their "skulls," which is above the "head." This is through the avodah of refining the nefesh habahamit, through which one elevates the "head" of the nefesh elokit, for the root of the nefesh habahamit is from the level of Tohu that preceded Tikun, for "Esau is the elder son," and the abundant lights of Tohu elevate the lights of Tikun.

3) This is the concept of the half-shekel: the giving from below is only a "half," and according to the teaching of the Maggid of Mezeritch, "Make for yourself two trumpets," two "half-forms." The Holy One, Blessed be He, and the Community of Israel are two half-forms that complete each other, as it were, as our Sages say, "My perfect one, my twin (te'omati/te'om-ati - my twin/my half)." That is, through his avodah with his ten faculties, he acts upon the Above as well. And within his avodah itself, the concept of the half-shekel is that he elevates the ten faculties of the nefesh habahamit, and thereby acts upon the nefesh elokit, as mentioned above, that the abundant lights of Tohu elevate the lights of Tikun.

It can be said that this is also the connection between the two interpretations of "heavy head"—the "Head" Above and the "head" of the soul (nefesh elokit). For just as through the refinement of the ten faculties of the nefesh habahamit—the twenty gerah—one elevates the upper half, the ten faculties of the nefesh elokit, so too is it for the interpretation that refers to the "Head" Above. For through the fact that (the Holy One, Blessed be He, helps him and) he elevates the ten faculties of his soul—for the soul descended below, and the authority and ability is given to every person, etc.—thereby he acts upon the Shechinah, as it were, etc.

4) With this, one can understand why it concludes "to make atonement for your souls," for through this, one effects a rectification of sins, even up to the sin of the Golden Calf. For since through these refinements one ascends to a most elevated place, the level of Tohu that preceded Tikun, and above that, to the place where Jacob is the firstborn (like the parable of the tube, where the drop that enters first will exit last), and even above that, to the place where both are equal—"Was not Esau a brother to Jacob?"—up to the level of simple unity, it is plain that from there is drawn the rectification of all lacks and blemishes, etc. This is the concept of the "twenty gerah" mentioned above. It is stated in Sefer HaBahir, "The ten Sefiros from below to above, we do not know; from above to below, we know," for through the avodah on the level of the ten Sefiros from below to above, one draws from the level of "we do not know," which is entirely above the concept of knowledge, etc.

This is also why this was the rectification for the sin of the Golden Calf. The sin of the Golden Calf occurred when "Moses delayed to descend," and our Sages say, "Do not read 'delayed' (boshesh), but 'the sixth hour has come' (bau shesh)," that the sixth hour arrived—half of the day—and he did not come. The rectification for this is specifically through the avodah of the half-shekel.

5) Now, just as through the giving of the half-shekel they offered sacrifices throughout the year, so too in avodah: this avodah must be performed throughout the year, and in a manner of joy and goodness of heart, "from the abundance of all," meaning that in place of what was a lack of all, there will be an abundance of all. And above that, for "one hour of teshuvah and good deeds in this world is better than all the life of the World to Come." However, for a reason known to their Creator, these matters are not currently in the open. But very soon, "The glory of Hashem shall be revealed, and all flesh together shall see, etc." May it be His will that very soon we will fulfill the mitzvah of the half-shekel in the Third Beis Hamikdash, souls in bodies, in good health and with abundance, and "Those who dwell in the dust will awake and sing," and Aaron and Moses with them, and a great congregation will return here, very soon indeed.

## B’s”D. Shabbos Parshas Mishpatim, Mevarchim HaChodesh Adar Rishon, 5741

### B’s”D. Shabbos Parshas Mishpatim, Mevarchim HaChodesh Adar Rishon, 5741

"And these are the *Mishpatim* (ordinances) which you shall set before them: If you buy a Hebrew slave, etc.". It is well known that there is a precise distinction here: "before them" (*lifneihem*) is in the plural and third person, while "you shall set" (*tasim*) and "you shall buy" (*tikneh*) are in the singular and second person. We must also understand the teaching in the Yerushalmi regarding the verse "And these are the *Mishpatim* which you shall set (*tasim*) before them," that *tasim* comes from the word *simah* (a treasury/storehouse) which is not revealed, i.e., the mysteries of Torah (*Razei Torah*). We must understand: *Mishpatim* are laws that are understood by human intellect [and even by the intellect of the nations of the world]. What, then, is the connection between *Mishpatim* and *tasim*, which implies *simah*—the *mysteries* of Torah? Furthermore, we must understand why the *Mishpatim* begin with the law of the Hebrew slave.

2) It is explained in *Ohr HaTorah* that the concept of a Hebrew slave in spirituality is that one’s service of Hashem is through *kabbalas ol* (acceptance of the yoke), the service of a slave. Based on this, we can say that the fact that the *Mishpatim* begin with the law of the Hebrew slave is to hint that even the fulfillment of the *Mishpatim*—which are understood by intellect—must be performed from a place of *kabbalas ol*, in the manner of a slave’s service. For although the fact that these *mitzvos* are called in the Torah of Truth by the name *Mishpatim* implies that they must be fulfilled also by virtue of their rationale, nevertheless, their primary fulfillment must be in the manner of *kabbalas ol*. This is true both from the perspective of the person performing the *mitzvos*, whose performance must be in the manner of a slave’s service, and also from the perspective of the *mitzvos* themselves. For all *mitzvos* (even the *Mishpatim*) are primarily the will of the Ribono shel Olam—a will that transcends rationale—it is only that the Ribono shel Olam decreed that the will of these *mitzvos* should be clothed in a rationale. To add: the fact that the fulfillment of *Mishpatim* must also be in the manner of *kabbalas ol* applies even to the *Mishpatim* aspect within them. That is, the fact that these *mitzvos* must be fulfilled also by virtue of their rationale is because that is the command of the Ribono shel Olam—that the fulfillment of these *mitzvos* should also be by virtue of their rationale. It can be said that this is what our Sages meant regarding the verse "And these are the *Mishpatim*": "And these" adds to the first ones; just as the first ones were from Sinai, so too these are from Sinai. At the Giving of the Torah (Sinai), Yisrael became slaves to the Ribono shel Olam. The hint "so too these are from Sinai" is that even the fulfillment of the *Mishpatim*—including the aspect that they must be fulfilled by virtue of their rationale [“so too these,” the *Mishpatim*]—must be in the manner of a slave’s service. Therefore, the *Mishpatim* begin with the law of the Hebrew slave, to hint that even for the *Mishpatim*, the primary approach (*der ershter tzugang*) to their study and fulfillment is *kabbalas ol*, the service of a slave. A further explanation: since the primary concept of slavery is the Canaanite slave [for in addition to the fact that a Hebrew slave works only for six years (and even a slave who has his ear pierced goes free in the Yovel), whereas regarding a Canaanite slave it is written "you shall work them forever," the servitude (slavery) of a Hebrew slave (even while he is a slave) is not like the servitude (slavery) of a Canaanite slave], then in order to hint at the aforementioned concept (that the fulfillment of *Mishpatim* must be in the manner of a slave’s service), it would seemingly have been more appropriate to begin the *Mishpatim* with the law of the Canaanite slave.

3) This can be understood by prefacing the teaching of our Sages on the verse "And these are the *Mishpatim* which you shall set before them." From the fact that it says "which you shall set (*tasim*) before them" (and not "which you shall teach them" or the like), we learn that it is not enough for the teacher to repeat the laws to his students until they are fluent; rather, he must also "set before them" the rationale that settles their study. Based on this, we can say that the fact that it subsequently says "If you buy" (*ki tikneh*) in the singular is to hint that *ki tikneh* refers (also) to "which you shall set", meaning that the teacher must teach his students in such a way that the subject matter becomes their own acquisition (*kinyan*). As our Sages said regarding the verse "In the Torah of Hashem is his desire, and in His Torah he meditates day and night": at first, the Torah is called by the name of the Ribono shel Olam ("Torah of Hashem"), and afterwards it is called by his name ("his Torah"—*Toraso*), it becomes his own Torah. And the fact that the Torah becomes the acquisition of the person engaged in Torah (*Toraso*—his own Torah) happens specifically when he knows the *reasons* for the laws. This is "which you shall set before them, if you buy"—that through the study in the manner of "setting before them," the Torah becomes the acquisition of the student, "if you buy."

It can also be said that the reason "If you buy" follows "which you shall set before them" is because "If you buy" is an additional command to the command of "setting before them." This can be understood by prefacing that, on the face of it, it is difficult to understand: the fact that Torah is clothed in the intellect of the student until it becomes *his* Torah seems to be a descent compared to the Torah as it is the "Torah of Hashem." Yet, from the aforementioned teaching of our Sages (that afterwards it is called by his name), it is implied that the Torah becoming *his* Torah (the student’s) is an elevation. The core of the explanation is that the descent and drawing down of Torah below is in such a way that even after it has descended and been clothed in human intellect, it remains the wisdom of the Ribono shel Olam, which transcends the intellect of created beings. The fact that it is understood by human intellect is because its root is in the *Ohr Ein Sof* (the Infinite Light), which is not limited by the definitions of "above" and "below," and therefore it has the capacity to exist below as well. This means that in addition to the fact that even in its descent below it remains in its essence the wisdom of the Ribono shel Olam, on the contrary, through its descent below, an advantage is created in it compared to the level of Torah as it was above before its descent. For through its descent below, its root in the *Ohr Ein Sof* is revealed within it. The same applies to the fact that through the study of Torah with understanding and comprehension, it becomes the student’s own Torah (*Toraso*): even after it becomes the student’s Torah, it remains in its essence the "Torah of Hashem." Furthermore, through the "Torah of Hashem" becoming the student’s Torah, an advantage is created in it compared to the level of Torah as it is in itself, the "Torah of Hashem". For the fact that the "Torah of Hashem" becomes the student’s Torah [in such a way that even after it becomes the student’s Torah it remains in its essence the "Torah of Hashem," and nevertheless it is the student’s Torah], is through the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* (which transcends the "Torah of Hashem"), which connects and unites the person with the "Torah of Hashem"—Yisrael and the *Kudsha Brich Hu* are all one.

4) The two aforementioned aspects of Torah—as it is the "Torah of Hashem" and as it is "his Torah" (the student’s)—are (generally) the Torah as it is in *Atzilus* and as it is in *Beriah*. As is brought in *Likutei Torah* of the Arizal, the reason Torah begins with the letter *Beis* is because the *Aleph* of Torah is the Torah in *Atzilus*, and the Torah we study with understanding and comprehension is the Torah as it is in *Beriah*, the *Beis* of Torah. It is explained in Chassidus that this is the meaning of "and regarding Your Torah that You have taught us"—Your Torah in *Atzilus* that You have taught us in *Beriah*. It is known that *Atzilus* is called by the name *Kinyan* (acquisition). As it is written, "Blessed is He who makes you, blessed is He who forms you, blessed is He who creates you, blessed is He who acquires you (*Konecha*)." "Makes you," "forms you," and "creates you" are the three worlds of *Asiyah*, *Yetzirah*, and *Beriah*, and "*Konecha*" is *Atzilus*. Just as an acquisition is not the creation of a new thing but the revelation of something hidden [for even before the acquisition the thing existed, but before the acquisition, when it was in the domain of the seller, it was hidden from the buyer, and through the acquisition the thing comes to him in revelation], so too *Atzilus* is the revelation of the hidden. This is why after saying "which you shall set before them," it adds "if you buy" (*ki tikneh*). Even though the study of Torah must be with understanding and comprehension ("which you shall set before them"), to the point that it is called by his name, *his* Torah (the student’s), nevertheless, the study must be in such a way that he feels that the Torah he is studying (even after it has become his wisdom) is the "Torah of Hashem," Your Torah that is in *Atzilus*—*tikneh*. Based on this, we can explain the teaching in the Yerushalmi that *tasim* comes from the word *simah* (a treasury) which is not revealed, the mysteries of Torah. For in order for the Torah that a person studies and understands with his intellect (human intellect) to be felt as the "Torah of Hashem," Your Torah that is in *Atzilus* (*ki tikneh*), it is through the revelation of the *neshamah* as it is in *Atzilus*, the level of *Tehorah* (pure) (which transcends the level of "You created it"). The revelation of this level of the *neshamah* (*satim*—the hidden of the *neshamah*) is through the study of the *Pnimiyus HaTorah* (the inner dimension of Torah), the *satim* of Torah. This is "which you shall set before them, if you buy"—that through *tasim* (from the word *simah*), the study of the inner dimension of Torah, the revelation of the inner dimension of the *neshamah* is achieved—"before them" (*lifneihem*), before their inner dimension (*lifnimiyusam*). Through this, even in the Torah that he studies in the manner of "setting before them" [understanding and explanation, the rationale that settles his study], the "Your Torah" that is in *Atzilus* is felt—*ki tikneh*.

5) The simple meaning of "And these are the *Mishpatim* which you shall set before them" is that the Ribono shel Olam told Moshe Rabbeinu that he (Moshe) should set the *Mishpatim* before Yisrael. From this, it is also understood regarding *ki tikneh* [that *ki tikneh* is in the singular and refers to "which you shall set," as mentioned in section 3], that it refers to Moshe. The concept is that it is brought in the Zohar that in every place where a name is mentioned twice, there is a pause in the meaning between them, such as "Avraham, Avraham," etc., except for "Moshe, Moshe," where there is no pause in the meaning between them. It is explained in Chassidus that Avraham, because his *neshamah* descended below, underwent a change compared to its root in *Atzilus*. This is not the case with Moshe; even while he was below, his source as it is in *Atzilus* shone in revelation, to the point that there was no partition or separation between them—"no pause in the meaning." The reason for this—that even while he was below he was as he is in *Atzilus*—is because the root of Moshe’s *neshamah* is from the level of *Iggulim* (circles) which transcends the *Seder Hishtalshelus* (the order of progression). Relative to what is above the *Seder Hishtalshelus*, *Atzilus* and *Asiyah* are equal. This is why it was said to Moshe, "which you shall set before them, if you buy," because the connection of the two levels in Torah [as it is clothed in human intellect ("set before them") and as it is "Your Torah" in *Atzilus* (acquisition)] is through the revelation of what is above the *Seder Hishtalshelus*, which is drawn down through Moshe. Just as it was explained above (section 3) that the connection of *his* Torah (the student’s) with the "Torah of Hashem" is through the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof*.

6) The aforementioned interpretation of *ki tikneh*, that it refers to "which you shall set before them," can be connected to the simple interpretation that *ki tikneh* refers to what follows: "If you buy a slave." For when his Torah study is in the manner of *ki tikneh*—that he feels that the Torah he is studying is the "Torah of Hashem"—his study is in a state of absolute *bitul* (nullification), like the *bitul* of a slave to his master. Even though the nature of study is (not that a person should nullify his intellect to the Torah, but on the contrary) that he should understand the Torah with his intellect—which is (seemingly) connected with his *existence*—and all the more so when his study is in such a way that the Torah is *his* Torah (the student’s), nevertheless, through the fact that he feels that the Torah (even after it has descended below and been clothed in human intellect) is the "Torah of Hashem," the wisdom of the Ribono shel Olam that transcends the intellect of created beings [and the fact that a person understands the Torah with his intellect is because the Ribono shel Olam is not limited by any limitations (not even by the definition of the Infinite), and therefore it is in His power to connect the opposites of the Infinite and the finite, so that the (limited) human intellect can grasp the (unlimited) wisdom of the Ribono shel Olam], the fact that he understands the Torah with his intellect does not contradict the concept of *bitul*. For the fact that he understands the Torah with his intellect [and also the fact that it is *his* Torah] is not from the perspective of his own existence, but from the perspective that the Ribono shel Olam is all-powerful.

7) Based on this, we can understand the expression of our Sages, "the yoke (*ol*) of Torah." On the face of it, since the *mitzvah* of Torah study is (not that a person should nullify his intellect to the Torah, but on the contrary) that he should understand the Torah with his intellect specifically [and also the halachic ruling is that the person engaged in Torah must rule according to his own understanding, for "Torah is not in heaven," and the *Kudsha Brich Hu* smiles and says, "My children have defeated Me, My children have defeated Me"], what is the concept of the *yoke* of Torah? For *yoke* is the concept of *bitul*. The explanation is (as above) that through the knowledge and feeling that the Torah is the "Torah of Hashem" [and the fact that it was given into the domain of man, "My children have defeated Me," is because He is all-powerful, and therefore it is in His power to give *His* Torah (the "Torah of Hashem") into the domain of man], even the fact that he understands the Torah with his intellect is in the manner of the "yoke of Torah"—that the Torah is his master and he is nullified to the Torah like a slave upon whom the yoke of the master is placed. And the fact that the Torah was given into his domain ("My children have defeated Me") is because that is the will of the "Master," the Giver of the Torah.

This can be connected to what our Sages said regarding the verse "And these are the *Mishpatim*": "And these" adds to the first ones; just as the first ones were from Sinai, so too these are from Sinai. The power for the connection of the two opposites in the *Mishpatim* [that they are understood by intellect, and the study (of them) must be in the manner of "which you shall set before them," a rationale that settles his study, through which the Torah becomes the student’s own Torah; and together with this, the study of them must be in the manner of *ki tikneh*, that he feels it is the "Torah of Hashem" (Your Torah in *Atzilus*, acquisition); and furthermore, that their study must be in the manner of a slave, the *yoke* of Torah] was given from Sinai, which also contains the connection of the two opposites: existence and *bitul*. As is explained in *Likutei Torah*, the fact that the Torah was given on Mount Sinai, which is the lowest of all mountains (*mechich*), is because on Mount Sinai there are the two opposites: *Har* (mountain)—elevation, and *mechich*—*bitul*.

8) Our Sages said, "Great is study, for it leads to action." It can be said that through the connection of the two opposites (existence and *bitul*) in Torah study, the same is drawn down into the action of the *mitzvos*. However, in Torah study, the innovation is the *bitul*. Even though Torah study is with understanding and comprehension (existence), nevertheless, the study must be in *bitul*. And in the fulfillment of *mitzvos*, the innovation is the existence. Even though the fulfillment of *mitzvos* must be by virtue of the *command* of the Ribono shel Olam [as is also understood from the fact that *mitzvah* is a term for command], in the manner of a slave’s service, nevertheless, his *bitul* must be in such a way that the *bitul* does not negate his existence, but on the contrary, this itself is his existence. That is, the fact that he is a slave of the Ribono shel Olam is [not only by necessity, but] that he wants to be a slave of the Ribono shel Olam and he has vitality and *taanug* (pleasure) in this.

It can be said that one of the reasons why the beginning of the commands of Parshas Mishpatim is the verse "And these are the *Mishpatim* which you shall set before them," which speaks of Torah study as per the teaching of our Sages, was brought above in section 3 [unlike most of the commands in the Torah, which begin with "Speak to the Children of Yisrael," "Command the Children of Yisrael," and do not mention the matter of study], is because the fact that the beginning of the commands of Parshas Mishpatim is with the laws of a slave hints that the fulfillment of all *mitzvos* must be in the manner of a slave’s service (as above, section 2). And since the *bitul* of *kabbalas ol* (slave’s service) must also be with vitality and *taanug* (as above), therefore, the beginning of the Parsha is with the matter of Torah study, for from the *taanug* and vitality in Torah study, a semblance of the *taanug* and vitality of Torah is drawn down into the service of *kabbalas ol*.

9) Based on this, we can understand why the beginning of the Parsha is with the law of the Hebrew slave, even though the primary concept of slavery is the Canaanite slave (as above, section 2). For the perfection of the concept of slavery (*kabbalas ol*) is that the *bitul* of slavery should be with *taanug* and vitality, and this concept exists specifically in a Hebrew slave. This can be understood according to what the Alter Rebbe writes in the *Maamar* beginning "And if a man sells his daughter to be a maidservant" [and the Tzemach Tzedek writes about this *Maamar* that it is a very important *drush* that speaks of the subject of the *tzaddik*, *rasha*, and *beinoni*], that the three types of slaves in Parshas Mishpatim—Canaanite slave, Hebrew slave, and Hebrew maidservant—are three levels in the service of the "Torah of Hashem." A Canaanite slave is one whose desire and *taanug* are in casting off the yoke, as our Sages said, "A slave is comfortable with lawlessness," meaning his pleasure (*nichah*) is in lawlessness, casting off the yoke, and his service of Hashem is only by necessity, because of the fear of the "striking of the rod." The "striking of the rod" in spirituality is that his *Nefesh Elokis* (Divine Soul) shines within him (it is just that the revelation of the *Nefesh Elokis* is only regarding action in practice). [He explains there that the "slave" (Canaanite) also includes the *rasha v'tov lo* (wicked person who is good). For just as with a slave in the literal sense, it is possible that the slave may sin against his master, and yet when his master strikes him with the rod he returns to his service, so too it is in the service of Hashem: even one who, by virtue of his *taanug* in casting off the yoke, transgresses the commands of Hashem, nevertheless, since afterwards, when the revelation of his *Nefesh Elokis* shines within him (the striking of the rod), he regrets his sins, he has not left the category of "slave." On the other hand, even the *beinoni* (the lowest level of the *beinoni*) is included in the Canaanite slave. For even though he has never committed a sin in his life, nevertheless, since his desire and *taanug* are in the cravings of this world, and his service of Hashem is only by necessity (it is just that the revelation of the *Nefesh Elokis* *forces* him to turn from evil and do good throughout the entire day), he has not left the category of Canaanite slave]. A Hebrew slave is one whose desire and *taanug* are (primarily) in the service of Hashem, and the matters of casting off the yoke (including the *taanug* in the matters of this world) are for him great suffering and pain. And even though when matters of this world come to him he enjoys them, his *desire* is not to be drawn after them. And he is bitter about the fact that he enjoyed the matters of this world. [And he is the *primary* level of the *beinoni*]. And the Hebrew maidservant is the level of the *tzaddik*, whose *middos* (attributes) have been *transformed* into holiness (*at-hafocha*—transformation, which transcends *at-kafya*—suppression), as is explained there at length.

This is why Parshas Mishpatim speaks of the acquisition of a Hebrew slave and a Hebrew maidservant ["If you buy a Hebrew slave, etc." and "If a man sells his daughter to be a maidservant, etc."], and the matter of the Canaanite slave (in the sections that speak of the *acquisitions* of a slave), "she shall not go out as the slaves go out," is only as if in passing (and in a negative way). For the fact that the beginning of Parshas Mishpatim is with the laws of a slave is because in this Parsha [which is in continuation of the Giving of the Torah, "just as the first ones were from Sinai, so too these are from Sinai"] it speaks of the general concept of the service of Hashem, which must be in the manner of a slave’s service. And since the slavery must be with *taanug* and vitality, therefore it speaks of the laws of a Hebrew slave and a Hebrew maidservant. At first, it speaks of a Hebrew slave, the level of the *beinoni*, because the level of the *beinoni* is the level of every person, and "after every person he will draw". And afterwards, it speaks of the Hebrew maidservant, the level of the *tzaddik*, for this service also belongs to everyone, as is explained in *Tanya*, that even when he knows in his soul that he will not reach this level in truth, nevertheless, he will do his part to fulfill the oath that they make him swear: "Be a *tzaddik*." It can be said that the fact that it speaks [even if only in passing] of the Canaanite slave is because of the advantage of *at-kafya*. As the Alter Rebbe writes in the aforementioned *drush*, that even in a complete *tzaddik* there must be the service of the Canaanite slave, because of the advantage that is in *at-kafya*. This is what our Sages said, "A person should not say 'I do not want,' etc., but rather 'I want, but what can I do, for my Father in heaven has decreed upon me?'" The fact that he must say "I want, etc." is so that he will have the service of *at-kafya*.

10) **It should be noted** that in this *at-kafya*, it is only the advantage of *at-kafya*. For even though in saying "I want" he is very far from G-dliness, the fact that he says "I want" is by virtue of the command of the Torah, and his intention in saying "I want" and awakening a desire for it is so that he will have the service of *at-kafya* (that he will act *against* his desire). Based on this, it is understood that the fact that the service of *tzaddikim* is not by necessity but is their *taanug* applies also to their service of *at-kafya*. For even though it is *at-kafya* in truth—because in saying "I want" they are awakening their *desire* for it, but they force themselves and act *against* their desire—this itself is their *taanug*, since the intention in their desire is so that they will act against their desire.

It is known that the drawing down achieved through the service of *at-kafya* is a higher drawing down than the drawing down achieved through the service of *at-hafocha*. As the saying goes, "When the *Sitra Achra* is suppressed (*at-kafya*), the glory of the *Kudsha Brich Hu* is exalted in all worlds"—that through the service of *at-kafya* is the drawing down of the light that is in the level of transcendence (*romemus*), "exalted." It can be said that the drawing down through the *at-kafya* of *tzaddikim* (where the *at-kafya* itself is their *taanug*) is an even higher drawing down. The concept is that one of the reasons why this drawing down is specifically through *at-kafya* is that through a person forcing himself to act against his desire and nature, the drawing down of the revelations from above is not according to their nature. Even the light that is in the level of transcendence, which by its "nature" is above revelation, is drawn down in revelation. It can be said that when a person’s *at-kafya* is by way of necessity, the opposite of his *taanug*, the fact that the light that is above revelation is drawn down in revelation (the opposite of its nature) is as if by way of necessity, so to speak. That is, the drawing down is from the level of the *external* dimension of the will. And through the person’s *at-kafya* being in such a way that it is his *taanug*, the drawing down is from the level of the *internal* dimension of the will and *taanug*.

11) This is "And these are the *Mishpatim* which you shall set before them," and our Sages said "And these" *adds* to the first ones. The descent and drawing down of Torah into *Mishpatim* that are understood even by human intellect, and in particular that through this the power is given to man to be able to study Torah in the manner of "which you shall set before them" (with understanding and explanation, to the point that the Torah is called by his name), is an addition to the first ones. For through this descent, the root of the Torah in the *Ohr Ein Sof* is revealed (as above, section 3). To add: through a person’s Torah study in such a way that the Torah is called by his name, even more is added to the Torah. For in the descent and drawing down of Torah into *Mishpatim*, what is drawn down in revelation below is the *wisdom* of the Ribono shel Olam (*Chochmah* of *Atzilus*). It is just that the connection of His wisdom, which transcends the intellect of created beings, with human intellect is through the drawing down from the *Ohr Ein Sof* which transcends *Chochmah* [meaning that the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* is only the giving of power to the level of *Chochmah* of *Atzilus* to connect with human intellect, and not that the *Ohr Ein Sof* itself is revealed]. And through the person’s engagement in Torah in such a way that the Torah is *his* Torah, the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* itself is drawn into the Torah. As is explained in the *drushim*, the fact that the Torah is called *his* Torah (the student’s) is because through engagement in Torah *lishmah* (for the sake of the Torah), he draws into it the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, and therefore the Torah is called by his name, because he drew into it this revelation. It can be said that this is why "And these" (*Ve'eleh*) is *Vav* (and) *Eleh* (these). The *Vav* that adds is the addition that is made to the Torah, *Eleh* is a term for revelation, and the meaning of *Ve'eleh* (*Vav* *Eleh*) is that the addition to the Torah (that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is drawn into it) is in revelation.
