That is, the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is drawn into it is not only in the manner of giving power, but the light itself shines in revelation.

12) The verse continues "which you shall set before them." It can be said that *asher* (which) is (also) from the word *osher* (happiness) and *taanug* (pleasure). That is, the matter of *Ve'eleh* (*Vav* *Eleh*)—the addition that is drawn into the Torah in the manner of revelation from the Essence of the *Ohr Ein Sof*—is from the level of *taanug*. For the fact that his Torah study is in the manner of the "yoke" of Torah, the *bitul* of a "slave" (as above, section 6-7), is like the matter of *at-kafya*. And the fact that his *bitul* is in such a way (that the *bitul* does not negate his existence, but on the contrary) that from the *bitul* itself he feels that the Torah is *his* Torah (as above, section 7) is like the *at-kafya* of *tzaddik

## [B's"d. Shabbos Parshas Vayakhel, Parshas Shekalim, Mevarchim HaChodesh Adar Sheni, 5711].

"When you take the sum of the children of Israel"—Moshe Rabbeinu, specifically the *Ra’aya Meheimna* (the Faithful Shepherd), who shepherds the *emunah* (faith) of the children of Israel. This *emunah* must be a *makif* (encompassing light) that becomes *pnimiyus* (internalized).

"When you take the sum of the children of Israel according to their numbers, and they shall give..." Seemingly, it should have said, "When you count the children of Israel." Why the expression "take the sum" (*tisa es rosh*)? To understand this, we must note that the parsha contains two distinct matters: 1) Moshe was commanded to take the half-shekel from every individual for the service of the *Ohel Moed*. 2) The commandment for all generations to give a half-shekel annually to the Temple treasury, from which the communal offerings were brought, as explained in Maseches Shekalim.

Regarding the first matter, it was not a permanent obligation, as Moshe was commanded to count the children of Israel specifically for that time; it was not a standing commandment for all generations. On the contrary, we see that Yoav sought to avoid counting the children of Israel. The second matter—that the half-shekels were taken for the sockets—was a one-time event. The third obligation, which our Sages learned—and which is also proven from Divrei Hayamim II 24:9—is the permanent annual obligation to give the half-shekel so that communal offerings could be brought from it. In this context, counting the children of Israel is entirely irrelevant to knowing their sum, and it is implied in many places that they were not counted at all. If so, a difficulty arises: the literal phrasing suggests that the introduction "When you take the sum of the children of Israel" applies to all the matters in the parsha.

The general concept in *avodah* is that in order to offer sacrifices upon the Altar—where the fire from above would consume the offerings, and through this, the *nefesh habehamis* within every Jew would be diminished and included in *kedushah* until each person bound their intellect and understanding to the greatness of Hashem to be *batel* to Him—there must first be an infusion of strength and assistance from above. This is the concept of Moshe, the *Ra’aya Meheimna*, who shepherds the *emunah* through Torah and *gemilus chasadim*. Through this, the "taking of the sum" and the raising of the head of the children of Israel is achieved—the elevation from below to above through the sacrifices and *tefillah*.

To understand the relevance of this specifically to Moshe, the *Ra’aya Meheimna*, to whom it was said, "When you take the sum... they shall give... a half-shekel"—he is called *Ra’aya Meheimna* because he shepherds the *emunah*, feeding and sustaining it. For *emunah* by itself can remain a *makif*, and "a thief at the mouth of the tunnel calls to Hashem". Even in subtle ways, one can find among those occupied with business that even though they believe "He is the One who gives you strength," they nevertheless involve themselves in schemes that distract them from their Torah study and the fixed *shiurim* even for a businessman —which is a contradiction in terms. Similarly, among those who "dwell in the tent" (full-time learners), one believes that this is the wisdom and will of the Holy One, blessed be He, yet when he succeeds in his studies and achieves greatness, he prides himself on it—and certainly so if he studies with that intent. Consequently, he may sometimes skew the matter just so that he comes out on top. Therefore, one must draw the *emunah* into the *pnimiyus* of his soul’s powers, so that his actions in practice align with his *emunah*. This is achieved specifically through his own personal *hisbonenus* (contemplation), and he must "strike the mind" with strength so that the resulting offspring are not empty imaginings.

What is this *hisbonenus*? It is known that there is a difference between the *emunah* that comes through the wisdom of philosophical inquiry—which is the *emunah* of the pious among the nations of the world—and the *emunah* of the children of Israel. The former relates to *Memalei Kol Almin* (the light that fills all worlds) and *Sovev Kol Almin* (the light that encompasses all worlds). *Memalei* is vitality that is enclothed and particularized into details, and consequently, the world is a reality, only it is *batel* to the vitality enclothed within it—this is the name *Elokim*. *Sovev* is a general *makif* that is above the limitations of nature and division; this is the *emunah* of the children of Israel. This is also the reason why they were not concerned about *shittuf* (associating other powers with Hashem).

## Beis Seudah. Shabbos Parshas Tetzaveh, 16 Adar, 5728.

**For** "When you take" [the census of the Children of Israel], etc. In this parsha the matter of the avodah in the Mishkan and the Beis Hamikdash—which is called a "Mishkan"—is explained, specifically the act of the Ketores. This was one of the primary avodos in the Mishkan. Although the previous parsha, Parshas Tetzaveh, also discusses the Ketores, and the following parsha, Parshas Vayakhel, does as well, those passages speak only in general terms. In this parsha, however, the matter is explained in detail: the preparations for it, the ingredients from which the Ketores was made, the greatness of the matter, the prohibitions associated with it, the severity of those prohibitions, and even the consequence of "the opposite of life" for one who transgresses them. This consequence of "the opposite of life" is explained specifically in this parsha because it is here that the severity of the matter is elucidated. Even though there are many prohibitions regarding korbanos—such as *chulin* in the *azarah* or *shechutei chutz*—they do not reach the level of the Ketores, which is so severe that it carries the consequence of "the opposite of life" for one who performs it in its [unauthorized] composition.

The matter of korbanos and the act of the Ketores constitutes the avodah of the Mishkan. As explained in the maamar for the day of the Hillula, the primary avodah in the Mishkan and the Beis Hamikdash is the avodah of korbanos (which includes the act of the Ketores). This is why the Mishkan was made specifically of *shittim* wood, etc. (as explained there), and through this, "I will dwell among them" was achieved. The general concept of korbanos is the drawing near of all the *kochot*, to the point of the closeness where "the secret of the korban ascends to the secret of the Ein Sof". However, there is a distinction between korbanos in general and the act of the Ketores. Korbanos are called "eating," as it is written, "My offering, My bread for My fire," etc. Therefore, Yisrael are called "My beloved" (*re'isi*), which means "my provider" (*parnasati*) through the two *tamidim*, etc., as it is stated in the Midrash, "Yisrael provide for their Father in Heaven." The act of the Ketores, by contrast, is the matter of "scent." Although, from the perspective of the *hishtalshlus* within holiness, korbanos also involve the element of scent, as it is written, "a pleasing aroma to Hashem," the primary aspect of korbanos is "eating." Therefore, where do we distinguish between the right *pardeshka* and the left *pardeshka*? Specifically regarding the Ketores, because the primary essence of the Ketores is the scent. Consequently, the Ketores specifically has the power to transform the *sitra achra*. Korbanos must come from that which is "permitted in your mouth," whereas in the Ketores there is also the element of *mor* (myrrh), which is a matter of the *sitra achra*, as explained by the Alter Rebbe regarding the "six months with oil of myrrh" and "Mordechai, pure myrrh." And it is not only that *mor* is from the *sitra achra*, but each and every one of the eleven spices of the Ketores is connected to the *sitra achra*. For the Ketores specifically requires eleven spices (and if one is missing, etc.), meaning each of the spices is one of the eleven—and "eleven" is the concept of the *sitra achra*, which is not "ten" (as in holiness) nor "one" (the eleventh). Furthermore, these eleven spices correspond to the eleven chieftains of Esav; each of the spices corresponds to one of the chieftains of Esav. Thus, each of the eleven spices is connected to the *sitra achra*, and the purpose of the Ketores is to transform the *sitra achra* into holiness. The power for this comes from the fact that the Ketores is the "scent," which reaches higher than the korbanos.

The advantage of the scent is that it reaches the *etzem hanefesh* (the essence of the soul). Therefore, the scent—specifically a strong scent—has the power to awaken one from sleep. This is a power unique to scent; it is not a power of "eating." On the contrary, eating causes sleep. Thus, it is stated in Maseches Yoma that the Kohen Gadol must minimize his eating on the eve of Yom Kippur because food brings sleep. Scent, however, awakens one from sleep because it reaches the *etzem hanefesh*. This is why the avodah of the Kohen Gadol on Yom Kippur, "once a year," was specifically with the Ketores, which is the matter of scent. Just as in a person below, the scent has the power to awaken the *etzem hanefesh*, so too in the Beis Hamikdash, the avodah with scent takes place in the *Kodesh Hakodashim*. Although he would enter there also with the blood of the korbanos, the primary avodah in the *Kodesh Hakodashim* was the Ketores. The innovation of the act of the Ketores on Yom Kippur is in the *Kodesh Hakodashim*, and due to the greatness of the Ketores, "no man shall be in the Tent of Meeting," etc.. This applies throughout the entire year as well (in Chassidus, this is explained regarding Yom Kippur—but it is true for the entire year due to the greatness of the Ketores), because it is the matter of scent that reaches the *etzem hanefesh*. Just as in the physical realm, the scent specifically has the power to awaken from sleep, so too in the spiritual realm: awakening from sleep is achieved specifically through the scent. The matter of sleep was explained in the maamar for Purim regarding the verse, "I sleep, but my heart is awake; the voice of my Beloved knocks," etc. This means that even though Knesses Yisrael is "asleep" in *galus*, since "my heart is awake"—meaning the *yechidah* in the soul is in its state of perfection and is awake in every person—therefore, "the voice of my Beloved knocks: Open for Me," so that she may be "My sister, My beloved, My dove, My perfect one." (In that maamar, this verse, "I sleep," and the following verses, "I have taken off my tunic," etc., are explained, and the explanation is that this occurs after "the voice of my Beloved knocks.") To this, Knesses Yisrael replies, "I have taken off my tunic; how shall I put it on?" Admur Maharash and Admur HaTzemach Tzedek in his notes on Shir HaShirim (on the verse, "My Beloved sent His hand through the hole") cite the Midrash Rabbah on Shir HaShirim on the verse "I have taken off my tunic," which states that this refers to the priestly garments and royal garments that were stripped from her in *galus*. Seemingly, the connection of the priestly garments, etc., to this context is not understood. They explain that since "I am asleep" in *galus* and "the voice of my Beloved knocks" to awaken from sleep, the response to this is "I have taken off my tunic"—she lacks the priestly garments, etc. Therefore, it is impossible for there to be the avodah of korbanos, which is called "a pleasing aroma to Hashem," and especially the act of the Ketores, which is the matter of scent, and the scent awakens from sleep. Since "I have taken off" the priestly garments and there is no scent of korbanos and Ketores, "how shall I put it on" to be awakened to the level of "My sister, My beloved," etc.? To this, the *Ruach Hakodesh* replies in the name of the Ribono shel Olam, "I have washed My feet; how shall I defile them?" The Beis Hamikdash is called "the footstool of the feet of the Ribono shel Olam," and in the Beis Hamikdash there was the *hashra'as haShechinah*, as it is written, "Behold, the heavens and the heavens of the heavens cannot contain You, how much less this house." This is a standing question, but after "I have washed My feet"—meaning, I have washed Myself from being enclothed in the Beis Hamikdash—then "how shall I," etc.

Although even in the time of *galus* there is the matter of korbanos, as "the prayers were instituted corresponding to the korbanos", and likewise there is the matter of the Ketores now—which is the matter of the *Mincha* prayer, called "the *Mincha* of Ketores," as explained in the *drushim* that explain the superiority of the *Mincha* prayer over other prayers (and even Eliyahu was answered only during the *Mincha* prayer, as stated in the *Haftorah* of this Shabbos)—the *hemshech* (drawing down of G-dliness) through prayer is only in the *pnimiyus* (internal aspect) of the worlds. It is known that the *hemshech* must also be in the *chitzoniyus* (external aspect) of the worlds, as explained regarding Torah, which must be "in their mouths" specifically, as the Alter Rebbe elaborates in Tanya. Similarly, regarding mitzvos, there must be the *ma'aseh hamitzvos* (the physical act of the mitzvah) specifically. The general concept of Torah and mitzvos is specifically down here in this world, "you have descended to Egypt". With korbanos, it is indeed true that through them there is the revelation of the *hemshech* also in the *chitzoniyus* of the worlds, whereas through the prayers—which were instituted in place of korbanos—the *hemshech* is only in the *pnimiyus* of the worlds. Since through mitzvos the *hemshech* is in the *chitzoniyus* of the worlds, to this Knesses Yisrael replies, "I have taken off my tunic," etc.

To this, the verse continues, "My Beloved sent His hand through the hole." Even though the *Ruach Hakodesh* replies in the name of the Ribono shel Olam, "I have washed My feet; how shall I defile them?", from the perspective of "My Beloved sent His hand," where "My Beloved" is the *ahavah atzmis* (essential love) of the Ribono shel Olam for the souls of Yisrael (as the Alter Rebbe explains in the maamar on the verse "I arose to open to my Beloved," and as explained in the *sicha* of the Rebbe [Rashab]), from the perspective of this *ahavah atzmis*, "He sent His hand through the hole." "Hole" (*chor*) is also related to the word for linen (*chur*) and to the word for freedom (*cherus*). Through this, the "Open for Me" is achieved. And after the "Open for Me" come the four levels of "My sister, My beloved, My dove, My perfect one." These are four levels both in the avodah of love—"for there is no service like the service of love"—and in the avodah of keeping mitzvos. These four levels correspond to the four letters of the *Shem Havayah*. Through this, "the Jews had light, and gladness, and joy, and honor". It can be said that these four things—light, gladness, joy, and honor—correspond to the four letters of the *Shem Havayah*: "Light" is Torah, as it is written, "For the mitzvah is a candle and the Torah is light," which is the concept of the *sefirah* of Chochma. "Gladness" is the festivals (*mo'adim*), which is the concept of the *sefirah* of Binah. "Joy" is *milah* (circumcision), which is the concept of the *sefirah* of Yesod, the essence of *Ze'er Anpin*. "Honor" is *tefillin*, which is the aspect of Malchus, as explained at the end of *Shaarei Orah*, and as it is in its root in the *Atzmus* of the Ein Sof *Baruch Hu*. This is what is written in the *Zohar Chadash* that *tefillin* are "the image of the seal of the Supreme King." "Image" and "seal" are the concept of the *sefirah* of Malchus. This means that through "Open for Me," the four letters of the *Shem Havayah* are drawn down into avodah, and likewise into "light, and gladness," etc.

This is also what is meant by "*For the Jews* there was light," etc., and as it is stated in the Gemara, "Anyone who denies idol worship is called a 'Yehudi' (Jew)." This means that first there is the matter of *mesiras nefesh*, which is what they stood for in the days of Purim. This is the concept of "Open for Me" as the eye of a needle, which is the concept of *mesiras nefesh*. Afterwards come the four levels of "light, and gladness, and joy, and honor." This is also what is written, "There was a Jewish man in Shushan the capital, and his name was Mordechai." Mordechai—where is he found in the Torah? It is said, "pure myrrh" (*mar deror*), and we translate it as *mira dachya* (pure myrrh), as explained in *Torah Or*. And "Yehudi" is the concept of *mesiras nefesh* as mentioned above; "Yehudi" is from the word *hoda'ah* (acknowledgment) that is above *ta'am v'da'as* (reason and intellect). This activated in everyone the matter of *mesiras nefesh*, so that they were all called "Yehudim" specifically, contrary to their previous state where they bowed to the statue, as recounted in the Gemara. Afterwards, from this *mesiras nefesh*, it is drawn down into the four letters of the *Shem Havayah*—light, and gladness, and joy, etc.—all the way to "light, and gladness, and joy, and honor" below ten *tefachim*, according to the literal meaning of the verse.

## With the help of Hashem. Shabbos Parshas Tetzaveh, 10 Adar Rishon, 5741

### With the help of Hashem. Shabbos Parshas Tetzaveh, 10 Adar Rishon, 5741

"And you shall command the Children of Israel, that they bring to you pure olive oil, crushed for the light, to kindle a lamp continually". The well-known nuances in this verse are that in all other commandments in the Torah, it says "Command the Children of Israel" or similar, whereas here it says, "And *you* shall command the Children of Israel." Furthermore, the emphasis of "And *you* shall command" is not only in the wording but also in the content; the phrasing implies that Moshe himself is the one commanding. This requires understanding, for Moshe is merely the messenger to convey the command of the Ribono shel Olam to the Jewish people; why then is it said, "And *you* shall command"? 

We must also understand the phrase "that they bring to *you*" (*eilecha*), implying they bring the oil to Moshe. Seemingly, since the kindling of the lamps was performed by Aharon, why was it necessary to bring the oil to Moshe? We must also understand the phrase "olive oil... *crushed* (katit) for the light," for seemingly it should have said "olive oil... to give light." Finally, we must understand why the subsequent verse says "from evening until morning," while here it says "to kindle a lamp continually."

2) The Rebbe Rayatz explains (in his famous maamar beginning "Vekibel HaYehudim", delivered on Purim Katan 5727) that a command (*tzavah*) is a matter of connection and bonding (*tzavta*). This is the meaning of "And *you* shall command the Children of Israel": Moshe connects and bonds the Children of Israel with the Ohr Ein Sof. Through Moshe’s influence upon the Jewish people (by connecting them with the Ohr Ein Sof), an advantage and addition is created within Moshe himself. [For Moshe and the Jewish people are like the head and the feet, as it is written, "Six hundred thousand footmen of the people among whom I am," meaning all of the Jewish people are the feet of Moshe, and Moshe is their head. Just as in a human being, the feet lead the head to a place the head could not reach on its own, so too it is with Moshe and the Jewish people: through the Jewish people (the feet of Moshe), an elevation is added to Moshe. This is the meaning of "Six hundred thousand footmen of the people among whom I am"—that through the "feet" of the people, the revelation of "I am among them" is drawn into Moshe]. This is the meaning of "And you shall command the Children of Israel, that they bring to you olive oil...": by Moshe commanding and connecting the Jewish people with the Ohr Ein Sof, the Jewish people will bring olive oil to Moshe (*eilecha*), thereby adding further light within Moshe.

3) To explain this, the maamar introduces that Moshe Rabbeinu is called *Ra'aya Meheimna* (the Faithful Shepherd), which has two meanings: that he is the faithful shepherd of the Jewish people, and that he feeds and sustains the Jewish people in the matter of *emunah* (faith). The *emunah* that exists within the Jewish people on their own, for they are "believers, the children of believers", can be in the category of *makif* (encompassing). Moshe’s shepherding and sustaining the Jewish people in *emunah* means that this *emunah* becomes internalized. This is what is stated in the Zohar: "That *emunah* from above is fed and sustained by you (through Moshe)," meaning that Moshe feeds and sustains the *emunah* by drawing it into the internal dimension. The maamar continues that Moshe being *Ra'aya Meheimna* also refers to the "extension of Moshe" in every generation; the leaders of the Jewish people in every generation strengthen the *emunah* of the Jewish people (of their generation) so that their *emunah* will be internalized. 

Like Mordechai, who was the extension of Moshe in his generation, as our Sages say that Mordechai in his generation was like Moshe in his generation. Even during the time of Haman’s decree, when the study of Torah and the performance of mitzvos were linked with *mesiras nefesh*, he gathered congregations in public to strengthen the *emunah* of the Jewish people in Hashem and to stand firm in Torah study and the performance of mitzvos. After explaining at length that Moshe and his extensions in every generation strengthen the *emunah* of the Jewish people, the maamar explains the nuance of the verse "olive oil... *crushed* (katit) for the light" (for the *ma'or*—the source of light—and not merely "to give light"). During the time of exile, when everyone is broken and crushed (*katit*), through this they reach the *ma'or* (the essence) from which the light is found. We must understand the connection between this interpretation of "crushed for the light" and what was explained previously (in the maamar) regarding Moshe [and his extensions in every generation] feeding and sustaining the *emunah* so that it becomes internalized.

4) The explanation of the verse "And you shall command" comes (in the maamar) as a continuation of what was explained at the beginning of the maamar: that the meaning of "And the Jews accepted what they had begun to do" is that they accepted what they had started at the time of the Giving of the Torah. At the Giving of the Torah, there was the beginning ("began to do"), and in the days of Achashverosh (during the time of Haman’s decree), there was the acceptance ("and the Jews accepted"). [Similar to the words of our Sages on the verse "The Jews confirmed and accepted," meaning they confirmed what they had already accepted; for the "we will do" that preceded "we will hear" at the Giving of the Torah was only an acceptance, and in the days of Achashverosh they *confirmed* what they had accepted]. 

The maamar notes that this is seemingly a wonder: at the Giving of the Torah, the Jewish people were at the pinnacle of elevation, and there was a revelation of Elokus at the highest level [in addition to the fact that even before the Giving of the Torah there were extremely high revelations, such as the revelation at the Exodus from Egypt and especially at the splitting of the Red Sea; the revelation at the time of the Giving of the Torah was even higher]. Yet in the days of Achashverosh, the Jewish people were at the nadir of descent. In addition to the concealment and hiding present in every exile [for every exile is like the exile of Egypt, and just as in the Egyptian exile it is written "they did not listen to Moshe because of shortness of breath and hard labor," so too it is in every exile, where there are many trials in the performance of Torah and mitzvos], at that time (during Haman’s decree) the concealment and hiding were even greater. And yet, at the time of the Giving of the Torah, when they were at the pinnacle of elevation, there was only the beginning, while in the days of Haman’s decree, when they were at the nadir of degradation, that was precisely when they accepted what they had begun at the Giving of the Torah. 

The maamar explains that during the time of the decree, their performance of Torah and mitzvos was with *mesiras nefesh*. [In addition to the fact that they had *mesiras nefesh* not to apostatize, G-d forbid [as explained in *Torah Or*, that if they had converted they would have been left alone, for the decree was only against the Jews, yet the thought of apostasy never entered their minds, G-d forbid], they also had *mesiras nefesh* for the performance of Torah and mitzvos, to the point that they gathered congregations in public to study Torah with *mesiras nefesh*]. This arousal of *mesiras nefesh* was through Mordechai the Jew, the Moshe of his generation. This is the meaning of "And the Jews accepted what they had begun to do": at the Giving of the Torah there was only the beginning, and during the time of Haman’s decree there was the acceptance, because through having *mesiras nefesh* in practice for Torah and mitzvos, they were elevated (in this regard) to a higher level than they had been at the time of the Giving of the Torah, and therefore that was when the acceptance occurred—"and the Jews accepted."

Seemingly, one could say that the interpretation of "crushed for the light"—that through being *katit* (broken and crushed) one reaches the *ma'or*—is an explanation of why, during the time of the decree, they specifically arrived at *mesiras nefesh* in such a highly elevated manner. For *mesiras nefesh* comes from the essence of the *neshamah*, which is above revelation—the *ma'or* (the source from which the light is found). By being in a state of *katit* (broken and crushed), the essence of the *neshamah*, the *ma'or*, was revealed. However, from the sequence and order of the maamar, where the interpretation of "crushed for the light" comes after the matter of *Ra'aya Meheimna*, it implies that the concept of "crushed for the light" also relates to Moshe feeding and sustaining the *emunah* so that it becomes internalized.

5) This can be explained based on the well-known concept that the fact that the Jewish people believe in Elokus with simple faith and do not require proofs is due to two reasons. One is that "their *mazal* sees". The *neshamah* above *sees* Elokus (a vision that is above intellect), and this acts upon the *neshamah* clothed in the body to have faith in Elokus. Another explanation is that the root of *emunah* is from the essence of the *neshamah* (which is above "their *mazal* sees"). The fact that the essence of the *neshamah* is connected to Elokus is an essential connection (which does not depend on a cause, nor on the matter of vision that is above intellect). One could say that the difference between these two explanations is that the *emunah* in the *neshamah* clothed in the body, which comes from the vision of the *neshamah* above, is in the category of *makif*. Since the *neshamah* above is above the state of being clothed, its effect on the *neshamah* clothed in the body is in the category of *makif*. In order for the *emunah* (in the *neshamah* clothed in the body) to be internalized, it must be through the revelation of the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah*. For the essence of the *neshamah* is the essence of the *neshamah* clothed in the body, and therefore, the *emunah* of the *neshamah* clothed in the body that comes from the essence of the *neshamah* is internalized.

Based on this, we can explain the connection of the topics in the maamar: the matter of "(crushed) for the light" comes as a continuation of the explanation of *Ra'aya Meheimna*, because Moshe feeding and sustaining the *emunah* so that it becomes internalized is through his revealing the essence of the *neshamah* (which is above "their *mazal* sees"), the *ma'or* that is above the light. And the verse says "*crushed* for the light," and the maamar explains that in order to reach the *ma'or*, one must go through the state of *katit* during the time of exile, because the *primary* revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* (*ma'or*) is through the matter of *mesiras nefesh* (which is primarily during the time of exile), as will be explained.

6) The matter is that the fact that the *emunah* of the Jewish people is such that they sacrifice their lives for it is (primarily) due to the *emunah* that comes from the essence of the *neshamah*. For the *emunah* that comes from the fact that it sees ("their *mazal* sees"), even though it is very powerful [as is known, that the verification that comes from vision is the greatest verification], nevertheless, since his *emunah* is due to a cause (due to the fact that it sees) and is not linked with the essence of his existence, it is not mandatory that he sacrifice his life for it. The fact that the *emunah* of the Jewish people is such that they sacrifice their lives for it is because the *emunah* in Elokus is their very essence, and therefore it is impossible at all to apostatize, G-d forbid.

Based on this, we can explain what is stated in the maamar: that Moshe being *Ra'aya Meheimna* (that he feeds and sustains the *emunah*) also relates to the shepherds of the Jewish people in every generation (the extension of Moshe in every generation), who strengthen the *emunah* of the Jewish people so that they will stand with *mesiras nefesh* in the performance of Torah and mitzvos. Seemingly, Moshe feeding and sustaining the *emunah* is (as explained in many places and in this maamar itself) by influencing the Jewish people with *da'as* (knowledge) of Elokus, through which the *emunah* becomes internalized. And the shepherds of the Jewish people in every generation strengthening the *emunah* (as explained in this maamar) is so that the *emunah* of the Jewish people will be in a manner of *mesiras nefesh*. Based on the above, one can say that the *primary* matter of *Ra'aya Meheimna* is that he feeds and sustains the *emunah* itself, so that the *emunah* will not only be as it is from the perspective of revelations (from the perspective that the *neshamah* above sees Elokus) but from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah*. And Moshe drawing the *emunah* into the internal dimension (into *da'as* and comprehension) is a *result* of him feeding and sustaining the *emunah* itself (the drawing down and revelation of the *emunah* as it is from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah*). As mentioned above (Section 5), the drawing down of *emunah* into the internal dimension (into *da'as*) is through the revelation of the essential connection of the essence of the *neshamah*. Based on this, one can say that in the generations that required *mesiras nefesh* in practice, the shepherds of the Jewish people (the extension of Moshe) strengthened the *emunah* of the Jewish people so that it would be with *mesiras nefesh*, and thus the matter of *Ra'aya Meheimna* (in this regard) was revealed even more than in Moshe himself. For the drawing down and revelation of the *emunah* as it is from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah* [which is through Moshe and his extensions in every generation], its primary revelation is *mesiras nefesh* in practice.

7) It is known that the *mesiras nefesh* in practice for Torah and mitzvos of *all the Jewish people* was on Purim (during the time of Haman’s decree). The *mesiras nefesh* of Chanukah (during the time of the Greek decree) was (primarily) with Mattisyahu and his sons, whereas the *mesiras nefesh* during the time of Haman’s decree was *in all the Jewish people*. Based on this, we can explain what is brought in the maamar from the Midrash that Mordechai in his generation was equal to Moshe in his generation. Even though the extension of Moshe is in every generation, nevertheless, the Midrash states that Mordechai (specifically) was equal in his generation to Moshe in his generation. One could say that one of the virtues of Mordechai the Jew is that he was the *Ra'aya Meheimna* (*in revelation*) of *all the Jewish people* of his generation. Similar to Moshe *Ra'aya Meheimna*, who drew *da'as* to all the Jewish people of his generation, as is also understood from the fact that the generation of Moshe (all the people of his generation) is called the "generation of knowledge". [The difference is that in Moshe, the revelation of this matter (that he is the *Ra'aya Meheimna* of all the Jewish people) was through his drawing *da'as* to all the people of his generation, whereas in Mordechai, the revelation of this matter was through his revealing the power of *mesiras nefesh* that was in all the people of his generation]. One could say that by bringing in the maamar what is stated in the Midrash that Mordechai in his generation was equal to Moshe in his generation, the author of the maamar passed judgment upon himself, that he is the *Ra'aya Meheimna* (*in revelation*) of all the people of the generation.

8) Based on this, we can explain the connection (and order) of the topics (in the maamar) regarding the explanation of the verse "And you shall command": first, it explains the meaning of "And you shall command the Children of Israel," that Moshe connects and bonds the Jewish people (with the Ohr Ein Sof) by feeding and sustaining the *emunah*. Afterwards, it explains that the shepherds of the Jewish people in every generation (the extension of Moshe) also strengthen the *emunah* of the Jewish people, like Mordechai (the Moshe of his generation), who strengthened the *emunah* of the Jewish people to stand firm in Torah study and the performance of mitzvos. Afterwards, it explains the meaning of "crushed for the light" (in the verse "And you shall command," which seemingly speaks of Moshe himself), because Moshe revealing the aspect of the *ma'or* of the Jewish people (the essence of the *neshamah*) is primarily through his extension during the time of exile (*katit*), which aroused in the Jewish people their power of *mesiras nefesh*, through which is the *primary* revelation of the essence of the *neshamah*, the *ma'or*.

We must understand that according to the above explanation, the matter of "crushed for the light" relates to "And you shall command the Children of Israel," to the fact that Moshe and his extensions in every generation connect and bond the Jewish people with the Ohr Ein Sof. And in the verse, "crushed for the light" is stated as a continuation of "that they bring to you pure olive oil," for the matter of "that they bring to you" (that the Jewish people bring oil to Moshe) is that the Jewish people add further light within Moshe (as mentioned above in Section 2 of the maamar).

9) The verse "And the Jews accepted what they had begun to do" speaks (simply) of the time *after* the miracle of Purim. One could say that what is stated in the maamar—that the meaning of "And the Jews accepted what they had begun to do" is that in the days of Achashverosh they accepted what they had started at the Giving of the Torah—is also according to the simple meaning of the verse, that "and the Jews accepted" was after the miracle. Based on this, in the fact that in the days of Achashverosh they accepted what they had started at the Giving of the Torah, there are two matters: the acceptance that occurred during the time of the decree through their *mesiras nefesh* (as explained in the maamar), and the acceptance that occurred after the miracle of Purim, which is even higher than the acceptance during the time of the decree (as will be explained). One could say that the same applies to "crushed for the light," that through the matter of *katit* during the time of exile, one reaches the *ma'or*, and there are two matters in this: when the Jewish people are in a state of *katit* because there are decrees against the performance of Torah and mitzvos (as was the case at the time the maamar was said), and through this they reach the *ma'or* through their *mesiras nefesh*. And another matter in "crushed for the light" is that even when the Jewish people are in a state of expansion—expansion in material matters and also expansion in spiritual matters—but they are in exile [similar to the situation after the miracle of Purim, where the Jews had light, gladness, joy, and honor, simply and also spiritually [and more than that, *even the house of Haman was given to Esther*, where there was also the virtue of *at-hafcha* (transformation)], yet "we are still servants of Achashverosh"], they are broken and crushed (*katit*) from the fact that they are in exile. And through the *katit* of the Jewish people from the fact that they are in exile, they reach the *ma'or*.

The matter is that the fact that the Jewish people are broken from the fact that they are in exile (even when they have expansion in material and spiritual matters) is because the true desire of every single Jew is that there be a revelation of Elokus, to the point that this (revelation of Elokus) touches his very existence. Therefore, the fact that during the time of exile there is no shining of the revelation of Elokus as there was during the time of the Beis HaMikdash [especially when one contemplates what our Sages say: "Anyone in whose days the Beis HaMikdash is not rebuilt, it is as if it was destroyed in his days"], from this itself he is completely shaken, *katit*. And even when he is at a very high level, where a revelation of Elokus shines for him similar to the revelation that was during the time of the Beis HaMikdash, nevertheless, from the fact that in the world at large the revelation does not shine, it is proven that even the revelation that shines for him is a limited revelation. For when the revelation of the Ohr Ein Sof *Bli Gvul* shines, the revelation is everywhere, and when there is one place (even a remote corner) where the revelation of Elokus does not shine, it is because the revelation (even in the place where it does shine) is a limited revelation. [This is what is brought in the maamar of the Alter Rebbe that it is stated in the *Tikkunim* that even if one *tzaddik* would return in complete *teshuvah* in his generation, Mashiach would come, because through complete *teshuvah* one draws down the revelation of the Ohr Ein Sof *Bli Gvul*, and this revelation is everywhere]. And from the fact that the revelation of the essence of the Ohr Ein Sof does not shine for him, he is broken and crushed, *katit*. [Similar to the well-known concept that *choleh* (sick) has the gematria of 49, for even when one attains the 49 gates of *Binah* but lacks the 50th gate, he is sick]. It is known what the Tzemach Tzedek said that he heard from our Master and Rebbe, of blessed memory (the Alter Rebbe): "I do not want anything, I do not want Your Gan Eden, I do not want Your Olam HaBa, etc., I want nothing but You Yourself." And because this was heard from the Alter Rebbe [and the meaning of "it was heard" is that this was not only at special times but was a regular thing], and especially after this was publicized by the Tzemach Tzedek, the power was given to every Jew that his primary desire should be the revelation of the Essence, to the point that when this revelation does not shine—and certainly during the time of exile when not even the revelation (the revelation of *light*) that was during the time of the Beis HaMikdash shines—he is in a state of *katit*, and he requests three times every day (or more), "May our eyes behold Your return to Tzion in mercy," for then there will be a revelation of Elokus, up to the revelation of the Essence. This is "crushed for the light"—that through the matter of *katit* from the fact that we are in exile, we reach the *ma'or*, because the fact that the desire of every single Jew is a revelation of Elokus, to the point that it touches his very existence [which is why he is broken and crushed (*katit*) from the fact that during the time of exile there is no revelation of Elokus], is from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah*, the *ma'or* in the *neshamah*, whose connection to Elokus is an essential connection.

10) One could say that the aspect of the *ma'or* of the *neshamah* that is revealed through the matter of *katit* from the fact that we are in exile is higher than the aspect of the *ma'or* of the *neshamah* that is revealed through *mesiras nefesh*. The matter is that one of the reasons why at the Giving of the Torah there was only the beginning ("began to do") and in the days of Achashverosh there was the acceptance ("and the Jews accepted") is because the fact that they preceded "we will do" to "we will hear" at the Giving of the Torah was because He forced the mountain over them like a barrel, a revelation from above, whereas in the days of Achashverosh, the acceptance was from their own perspective. One could say that the *emunah* of the Jewish people from the perspective that the *neshamah* above *sees* Elokus (an *emunah* that comes from a cause) is similar to the "we will do" preceding "we will hear" (at the Giving of the Torah) from the perspective of the revelation from above, and the fact that in the days of Achashverosh the acceptance was from their own perspective is because then the connection to Elokus that comes from the essence of the *neshamah* was revealed—an essential connection that comes from the essence of their existence. More specifically, one could say that even in the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah*, there are (similar to) the two matters mentioned above. For the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* in the matter of *mesiras nefesh*, one could say, regarding the *revealed powers*, is *like* an additional thing. As we see in practice with many people, that while they were in a place where there were decrees against Torah and mitzvos, they had *mesiras nefesh* in practice for many years, and when they later came to countries where it is possible to engage in Torah and mitzvos with expansion, the *mesiras nefesh* they had before is not (so) apparent in them. For the fact that they stood with *mesiras nefesh* for many years is because the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* shone in them, which is above the revealed powers, and this did not cause a change in the revealed powers themselves. [The fact that the essence of the *neshamah* is the essence (also) *of the revealed powers* (as mentioned in Section 5) is that the essence of the *neshamah* is *their essence*, and this does not relate to the matter of the revealed powers themselves, *to their configuration*]. And the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* in the fact that he is broken and crushed from the fact that he is in exile is that even his revealed powers (*the configuration* of the revealed powers) are as one with the Essence. One could say that the fact that the essence of the *neshamah* and the configuration of the powers are (similar to) two matters is because even the essence of the *neshamah* is defined by a boundary, and its boundary is above the configuration of the powers. But from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the Essence, the simplicity of the *neshamah* and the configuration of its powers are all one. Based on this, one could say that the aspect of the *ma'or* of the *neshamah* that is revealed through *mesiras nefesh* is the essence of the *neshamah* as it is defined in the matter of simplicity that is above the configuration of the powers, and the aspect of the *ma'or* of the *neshamah* that is revealed through the matter of *katit* from the fact that we are in exile is the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the Essence.

11) One should connect this with what is explained in the maamar regarding the meaning of the verse "And you shall command the Children of Israel, that they bring to you pure olive oil...": that after Moshe commands and connects the Jewish people, then the Jewish people will bring to Moshe olive oil, that the Jewish people, through their service, will add a revelation of light in the level of Moshe. One could say that one of the explanations of this in the service of man is that Moshe commanding and connecting the Jewish people means that he feeds and sustains the *emunah*, so that the *emunah* will be, in addition to how it is from the perspective of the revelations of the *neshamah* (from the perspective that the *neshamah* above sees Elokus), also from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah*. [One could say that this is the meaning of "And you shall command the Children of Israel," for the phrasing "And you shall command the Children of Israel" implies that Moshe commands (connects and bonds) the Jewish people themselves, for from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah*, all the Jewish people are one]. And through the service of the Jewish people (after the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* has been drawn into the Jewish people by Moshe), that even their revealed powers (*the configuration* of the powers) will be appropriate to the essence of the *neshamah*, through this an advantage and addition is created in the aspect of the essence of the *neshamah* that was drawn down and revealed in them by Moshe ("that they bring to you"), for through this its true root is revealed, as it is rooted in the Essence.

[**To note**, that through this, an addition is also made in the unity of the Jewish people. The unity of the Jewish people through the essence of the *neshamah* that is revealed in them is *like* an additional thing to their existence. Therefore, this unity is through making their *nefesh* the primary and their body secondary. And through the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the Essence, through which the configuration of the revealed powers is also one with the Essence, the unity of the Jewish people is in all their matters, even in matters relating to the body].

Based on this, one can understand what is stated in the maamar, that through the Jewish people adding (through their service) to the level of Moshe, through this there will be a "lamp continually." Seemingly, the fact that the lamp of the *neshamah* ("The lamp of Hashem is the soul of man") is continual (equally, without change) is through the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* drawn down by Moshe ("And you shall command"), for in the essence of the *neshamah* there is no possibility of change. Yet the maamar says that the matter of "lamp continually" is through the Jewish people adding to the level of Moshe, "that they bring to you." One could say that in the revelation of the essence of the *neshamah* in a way of "from above to below" through "And you shall command," there is a difference between evening and morning. Its primary revelation is when there are concealments and hidings (evening), through which the power of *mesiras nefesh* is aroused and revealed, as explained above (Section 10), that even those who stood with *mesiras nefesh* during the time of the decree (evening), when they came to a place where it is possible to engage in Torah and mitzvos with expansion (morning), the *mesiras nefesh* they had before is not apparent in them. And the true matter of "lamp continually" (in which there is no possibility of change) is through the service of the Jewish people, that even the revealed powers will be one with the essence of the *neshamah*, "that they bring to you."

Based on this, we can explain why "crushed for the light" is stated in the verse as a continuation of "that they bring to you," for in "crushed for the light" is also included the fact that the Jewish people are broken and crushed (*katit*) from the fact that they are in exile. This matter is through the service of the Jewish people, that even their revealed powers will be appropriate to the essence of the *neshamah*, and the aspect of the *ma'or* of the *neshamah* that is revealed through this is the essence of the *neshamah* as it is rooted in the Essence. Therefore, "crushed for the light" is stated as a continuation of "that they bring to you."

12) The meaning (simply) of "And you shall command the Children of Israel, that they bring to you..." is that Moshe commands the Jewish people that they should bring to you. From this, it is also understood in the internal dimension of the matters: that Moshe commanding and connecting the Jewish people is in a way that he draws down for them the empowerment also for "that they bring to you." And so it is regarding the Moshe of our generation, the Rebbe, the Nasi of our generation, whose service is to arouse and reveal the *emunah* in every single Jew that comes from the essence of the *neshamah*, in a way that afterwards they will perform their service with their own power, to the point that they will be a "lamp continually" in which

## With the help of Hashem. Tuesday, Purim Katan 5738.

### With the help of Hashem. Tuesday, Purim Katan 5738.

"The Jews undertook what they had begun to do". My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi of our generation, explains this in his well-known maamar delivered on Purim Katan (5687) in Moscow [which year was the beginning of the second jubilee of his saying it]: that now, in the time of galus, they accepted what they had already begun at the time of Matan Torah. As our Sages of blessed memory state regarding the verse "The Jews confirmed and accepted, etc."—that they confirmed now (in the days of Achashverosh) what they had already accepted at the time of Matan Torah. The maamar emphasizes that, on the face of it, this is a "wondrous" (*nifla*) matter [and he emphasizes in his question that this is a matter of "nifla" (rather than the usual expression in most maamarim—"tzarich iyun"), because the matter (that they accepted in the time of galus what they had begun at the time of Matan Torah) is in the category of *nifla*. As is explained regarding the concept of "It is not *nifla*... nor is it far away": distance and proximity are relative to one another, but *nifla* denotes a matter of wonder and separation, to the point that it is in the category of a *pele* (wonder) relative to it—meaning it is a matter that is completely beyond comparison. And as is known (regarding the Sefiros), the category of *pele* is the category of Keter, and specifically the category of the *pnimiyus* (inner dimension) of Keter, which is completely beyond the entire Seder Hishtalshelus. And from this, one can also understand regarding the wondrous matter brought in the maamar]: at Matan Torah (when they "began to do"), Yisrael were in a state of ultimate elevation and an excellent standing and situation. It was after the splitting of the Sea and the Exodus from Mitzrayim, which are matters that established every single one of Yisrael and the entirety of the Jewish people in an extremely lofty state. At the Exodus, the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He, was revealed to them in His glory and essence and redeemed them; and at the time of the splitting of the Sea [which is the conclusion and seal of the Exodus], a maidservant saw at the Sea what the prophets did not see. And all the more so, the ultimate elevation of Bnei Yisrael at the time of Matan Torah, for Matan Torah was only once, and as is known, Matan Torah will not happen again. It is also explained in Tanya that at the time of Matan Torah there was already a semblance of the ultimate perfection of the world that will be in the future, for there was a revelation of Elokus in sensory vision, and by virtue of these revelations, Yisrael were in the ultimate state of perfection. This is not the case in the time of galus, which is the exact opposite, where Yisrael are at the nadir of descent. Especially in the time of Haman (when "the Jews accepted... they confirmed..."), when he sought, G-d forbid, to destroy, kill, and annihilate all the Jews, from young to old, children and women, in one day. This decree was extremely severe, for it was at once upon all the Jews in the entire world, as Achashverosh ruled over the entire world, as is stated in the Midrash, and there was no ability to escape to a place that was already past the decree, since it was in one day (throughout the world)—which is (within galus itself) the most terrible galus.

The maamar there explains that the intensity of (the decree and) the galus in spirituality was the decree regarding the matter of chinuch. As is stated in the Midrash, he (Haman) said: "I will not lay my hand first except upon these children." This means that in order to carry out, G-d forbid, his plot and evil thought that he conceived against the Jews, may Hashem save us, to destroy them, the beginning is with these *tishbar* (schoolchildren). The intention here is the decree upon the entire matter of chinuch. It can be said that even though the aforementioned *tishbar* refers to a child in years, a semblance of this exists also in a "child" in matters of Yiddishkeit, even if he is a grown man in years—in the language of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, "he already has white hair"—nevertheless, in his state regarding matters of Torah and mitzvos, he is in the category of a child. [And from this very fact (that the existence of the Jewish people depends on chinuch) one understands the greatness of the *mesiras nefesh* that must exist for the chinuch of Bnei Yisrael. One must sacrifice one's soul so that there will be "Do not touch My anointed ones"—these schoolchildren, as is stated in Maseches Shabbos, which is accomplished by bringing the children to their proper place, to the *beis rabban*, and there, the manner of chinuch itself is in the manner of a "house (of the teacher)" and stability—meaning that the children should receive from the teacher the foundation (the entire structure) for all the days of their lives.]

After all this, at Matan Torah there was only "began to do," a matter of beginning only, and the confirmation (of the Torah of Matan Torah), "confirmed and accepted," was specifically in the time of galus, and especially in the time of Haman when Yisrael were in the ultimate degradation of galus, both physically and spiritually, which is a most wondrous matter.

2) This will be understood (as explained in the maamar) based on the explanation of what is written, "pure olive oil beaten for the light"—and he emphasizes there that it does not say "oil to illuminate," but rather "oil... for the light." He explains that "the light" refers to the *maor* (luminary) itself, meaning the source of the light. And this is what is meant by "beaten for the light": when a person is in the category of "beaten" (*kasit*), that he is broken and crushed, then he reaches the *maor*. And this being broken and crushed occurs even when, physically, he is in a state of the greatest expansion, yet nevertheless, he is lacking in spirituality, in his *avodah*. And furthermore, even if he is also complete in spirituality in his fulfillment of Torah and mitzvos, and is even in the ultimate state of elevation and expansion, performing his *avodah* as it ought to be performed, nevertheless, when he contemplates that "all your people are righteous", and every single one of Yisrael is "a shoot of My planting, the work of My hands, in which I take pride" (the plantings and work of the Holy One, blessed be He, in which He takes pride), and he contemplates the greatness of the Holy One, blessed be He, he will weep bitterly in his soul. For how can all his *avodah* (even if it is complete) be considered in relation to his true value and level, which should be "a shoot of My planting and the work of My hands, in which I take pride" of the Holy One, blessed be He, in a revealed way, such that everyone who sees him recognizes this? If so, even the perfection of his *avodah* is not as it ought to be at all. And if even one who is "beaten" before the King is the opposite of vitality and existence, all the more so when his *avodah* is not as it ought to be, and is not in proportion to what it should have been according to the powers given to him. And when he contemplates the magnitude of his descent and weeps bitterly in his soul, he will come to *teshuvah*, until he reaches the state of "and the spirit returns to G-d who gave it", and even higher—that the soul returns with *mesiras nefesh* to its source and its supernal root from which it was taken, etc.. And this is what is meant by "beaten for the light": that through the fact that the person is broken and crushed, in the category of *kasit* [from within expansion in physicality and from within expansion in spirituality, as mentioned above], he reaches the *maor* itself, to the category of "G-d who gave it," etc.

3) The aforementioned *avodah* of being in ultimate *bitul* in the category of *kasit* subsequently acts also upon the actual deed. The matter is, as my revered father-in-law, the Alter Rebbe, explains in *Torah Or*, that the Jews sacrificed themselves to death all the year round, and no extraneous thought, G-d forbid, arose to them. The meaning of this is that they stood with *mesiras nefesh* in all the modes of change that occurred, for "year" (*shanah*) is a term for change (*shinui*), for the year contains within it all possible changes. Similar to the well-known concept regarding the merit of the *esrog* that "it dwells on its tree from year to year", that it endures all the changes of the climates of the four seasons of the year and is not affected by this at all, and "dwells on its tree" [that its vitality and place of dwelling is in its tree, in its root and source]. And not only is it not affected by the changes, but furthermore, it grows from the changes, as is explained in the *Hemshech* "VeKacha". Likewise, it is in the *avodah* of man: in all the modes of changes of levels in *avodah*, all the year round, his *avodah* is in ultimate perfection, such that no extraneous thought, G-d forbid, arises. And one reaches this specifically when he is in ultimate *bitul* in the category of *kasit*. For since there are modes of *avodah* that are diametrically opposed, like *Chesed* and *Gevurah*, therefore, even if his *avodah* in the line of *Chesed* is as it ought to be, it is possible that it would be difficult for him that his *avodah* in the line of *Gevurah* should also be in the same perfection [similar to what is said even regarding our father Avraham, peace be upon him, since his *avodah* was in the category of *Chesed*, "Avraham My beloved", it is said about him, "Now I know that you are G-d-fearing," after he also stood in the tenth trial]. And through the *bitul* of *kasit*, his *avodah* is perfect without changes in all the different modes of *avodah*.

4) Furthermore, another of the effects of the aforementioned *bitul* is that the fact that he recognizes that he is in the ultimate descent, etc., causes him that even if his *avodah* is as it ought to be, nevertheless, he will educate himself like the chinuch of the *tishbar* in its literal sense, because he feels himself in the category of a child in his *avodah* (as mentioned above, that in what way is he considered in relation to his true value). And the matter of chinuch must be with *mesiras nefesh* (as mentioned above), as is explained in the aforementioned maamar regarding the *mesiras nefesh* that existed in this regard in the time of Mordechai. And the Torah is eternal, so in this there is an eternal instruction [Torah from the word *hora'ah* (instruction), as is stated in the Zohar] for everyone at every time and in every place: that one must sacrifice one's soul for the matter of chinuch, both for chinuch itself—as the Mishnah says, "Who is wise? He who learns from every person"—for this chinuch must be every single day throughout all the days of his life. For every day one must ascend in his *avodah*, as the saying goes, "We ascend in holiness," that even when he is in the category of *kodesh* (holiness)—for "Kodesh" itself is the category of *Chochmah* [which is at the level of a "wise man," for yesterday he already learned from every person]—nevertheless, even then there must be an ascent, that upon the arrival of a new day he will perform *his* work and continue to learn from every person and ascend in his level of *Chochmah*. That is, every single day there must be chinuch of oneself. And this is also understood from the well-known teaching of the Baal Shem Tov that in every matter that a Jew sees or hears, he must see and hear the instruction in this for his *avodah* to his Creator—meaning that in every matter he sees or hears, he must educate himself. And all the more so that one must sacrifice one's soul for the matter of the chinuch of *tishbar* in its literal sense. And especially there is an obligation in this upon the young men and young women, as my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, adds in his letter in connection with his aforementioned maamar, that the time demands that every single one of the young men and young women who are alive, not only must they themselves in their private lives be firm and strong in the observance of the fulfillment of the practical mitzvos in all their beauty, etc. [and thereby be a living example], but furthermore [and this is also the essence] to create an environment of Torah learners and mitzvah observers, until it is seen in them openly that they are "a shoot of My planting, the work of My hands, in which I take pride." And in general, this obligation is upon everyone, for everyone is included in the category of young men and young women when it is remembered that every single day one becomes a new creation. Through the chinuch of *tishbar*, it is accomplished that "From the mouths of babes and sucklings You have founded strength... to silence the enemy and the avenger", as is explained in the aforementioned maamar that the "enemy" is the revealed hater, and the "avenger" is the hidden hater who plots schemes, etc. [and in *avodah*, this is the matter of what Yaakov said, "which I took from the hand of the Emori with my sword and my bow." The difference between a sword and a bow is that the sword kills the enemy who stands near him, and the bow can kill even the enemy who stands far away. The nearby enemy refers to the (coarseness of) evil that the person knows and understands is evil, and the enemy who stands far away is the subtlety of evil that he does not know about, etc. And the removal of the enemy and the avenger is through the *avodah* of "my sword and my bow," as is explained in the maamarim regarding the five weapons listed in the Mishnah of Shabbos].

5) The power for the aforementioned *avodah* is through Moshe Rabbeinu. As is explained in the aforementioned maamar, this is what is meant by "And you shall command the Bnei Yisrael, etc." *Tetzaveh* (command) is a term for *tzavta* (partnership) and connection. And "you" refers (in the simple sense) to Moshe, and the extension of Moshe in every generation, that in every one of Yisrael there is a dimension of Moshe, as is stated in *Tikkunei Zohar* and brought in *Iggeres HaKodesh* by my revered father-in-law, the Alter Rebbe. And in everyone, the dimension of Moshe within him is in a revealed state, to the point that because of this, he sees it as a "small matter" relative to him, as is stated in Tanya, Part One. And the dimension of Moshe in every one of Yisrael connects, in the manner of *tzavta* and connection (*tetzaveh*), the root of the soul to the soul that is clothed in the body, and with the animal soul and his body and his portion in the world, and gives the power that he should be "beaten for the light," that from the fact that he is crushed and broken due to the descent of the soul into this lowest world, than which there is no lower—which is a tremendous descent, for even when this world is in ultimate perfection, it is called a "deep pit"—from this it is understood that the very fact that the soul descended into this world is a great descent. And due to the descent, even if he is in a state of ultimate expansion in physicality and also in spirituality, nevertheless, what is he considered in relation to his true standing and situation (before the descent), where he is in the category of "My son", the only one of the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He (as is known from the parable in the *Hemshech* 5666)? From the *kasit* (that he is broken and crushed), he becomes "for the light," that he reaches the *maor* itself, as mentioned above.

6) Based on the above, one will understand what is written, "The Jews undertook what they had begun to do." Even though at the time of Matan Torah, Yisrael were in the ultimate state of perfection (and then everything was already given, as mentioned above), nevertheless, it was then only "began to do," a beginning only, for it was only from above to below. And afterwards, in the *avodah* during the time of galus, in the time of Mordechai and Esther, when it is the intensity of galus in double and redoubled darkness (as it was in the simple sense), then the *avodah* is from the side of the lower beings—"The Jews undertook"—which was not the case at the time of Matan Torah. Furthermore, in the *avodah* during the time of galus, there is an elevation relative to the time of Matan Torah, for specifically then the confirmation is accomplished in the Torah that they accepted at Matan Torah, which is in the category of "began to do" only. For specifically in the time of galus [in the simple sense, and also in the subtle matters, when one contemplates (similar to the above) that even after all the matters he accomplished in his *avodah*, even when they are in ultimate perfection, nevertheless, he is still in a category of galus (in spirituality), because since the Holy One, blessed be He, helps him, it is in his power to reach a higher level, and even to an incomparable degree, similar to the language of Chazal, "(You labored) and you found," that the finding is incomparable to his labor and effort], since then everyone is broken and crushed, then they reach the *maor* itself.

7) One should connect what was explained above regarding the greatness of the elevation in the *avodah* of the lower beings—that specifically through this the confirmation of what they "began to do" is accomplished—with the *parsha* of the week [and in this very thing, with the *shiur* of today, Tuesday (whose matter is that "it was good" was doubled in it, good for Heaven and good for the creatures)—according to the ordinance of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, to study every day of the week one *parsha* of the *parsha* of the week] based on what is explained in the maamarim, and especially in the maamar of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe (Rashab), of blessed memory, from sixty years ago on our *parsha* on the verse, "And now, if I have found favor in Your eyes, etc., so that I may find favor in Your eyes, etc., for I have found favor in Your eyes, etc., for You have found favor in My eyes" [that through this there will be "And we will be distinguished, I and Your people, from all the people that are upon the face of the earth" (for "we will be distinguished" is like the matter of *nifla* explained above)]. For one must understand what is the merit in the matter of *chen* (favor) that Moshe Rabbeinu asked and insisted upon so much, until it was the ultimate perfection of his request, and through the *chen* there would afterwards be "And we will be distinguished, I and Your people." For is it not written, "False is the *chen*"? And it is because *chen* is in the category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* (arousal from above) from His own self, and there is a great advantage in what comes through *is'arusa d'letata* (arousal from below). He explains there that there is a category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* that is drawn down after the *is'arusa d'letata*, which is an *hemshech* (drawing down) from the category of the Essence of the *Ein Sof*, which *is'arusa d'letata* does not reach at all, and it is only in the category of a gift (and it is what is called a "free gift," which is a term for *chen*, etc.), only that this is drawn down after the perfection of the *is'arusa d'letata*, similar to a gift, as our Sages of blessed memory say, "If he does not do it for his own satisfaction, etc.," even though it is in the category of a gift.

The *avodah* in this is [as is understood based on what is explained in the maamar there], that there is the matter of faith in the category of *memalei kol almin* (filling all worlds), but in this category it is possible that there be a semblance of this also in the pious of the nations of the world, and even in Yisrael it is possible that the category of *memalei kol almin* will be in the category of knowledge. But the essence of the faith of the Jewish people is in the level of *sovev kol almin* (encompassing all worlds), for this faith belongs only to Yisrael. This is what is written, "Hear, O Yisrael, Hashem our G-d, Hashem is One," which is the matter of acceptance and faith in the category of *sovev kol almin*, and regarding this it is said "Hear, O Yisrael" specifically, for this faith is only in the Jewish people [unlike what is said, "Blessed be the Name of the glory of His kingdom forever and ever," which refers to the contemplation of the category of *memalei kol almin*]. And this is also what is stated in the Zohar that the *Ayin* of *Shema* and the *Dalet* of *Echad* are large letters, and through their combination the word *Ed* (witness) is formed. It is explained in many places that regarding a revealed matter, there is no need and it is not appropriate for testimony, and even regarding a matter that is destined to be revealed, etc., the Torah did not require testimony, but specifically regarding a matter that is completely hidden, the category of *sovev kol almin*. And this is what is written, "Hear, O Yisrael... One," which refers to every single one of Yisrael, who are specifically the witnesses testifying that Hashem is One [to the point that in every standing and situation that he may be in his *avodah*, every single one of Yisrael says "Hear, O Yisrael," as is explained in the sichos of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe]. For the *Ches* and *Dalet* of *Echad* hint at the seven heavens and the earth and the four directions of the world, and the *Aleph* hints at the "Alufo shel Olam" (the Master/Leader of the world), and Yisrael, through their *avodah*, accomplish being "One," meaning that they draw into the world (the *Ches* and *Dalet*) the Master of the world (the *Aleph*), until they are united in a semblance of the word *Echad*, that the *Aleph* is one word with the *Ches* and *Dalet* (and the content of the word itself is the matter of unity), and in such a manner that the *Aleph* of the Master of the world will be the head and the beginning of the word. And through this *is'arusa d'letata* of the *avodah* of Yisrael in the category of *sovev kol almin* (Hashem is One), they draw down a category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* that is relative to the *is'arusa d'letata*, and afterwards is drawn down a category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* that is above the category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* that is through *is'arusa d'letata*. For the category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* drawn down through *is'arusa d'letata* is a category that is relative to the Seder Hishtalshelus, and it can be said that this is also what is referred to by what is written, "Behold, My angel will go before you." But Moshe's request was that there be an *hemshech* of a category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* that is above this, which is from His own self—"And now, if I have found favor in Your eyes," the matter of *chen*, until there will be "And we will be distinguished, I and Your people," which refers (in the Sefiros) to the Sefirah of Keter, the *pnimiyus* of Keter, and even to the Essence of the Holy One, blessed be He. For even though this category of *is'arusa d'le'eila* is drawn down from His own self—meaning that *is'arusa d'letata* does not reach there—nevertheless, "The Holy One, blessed be He, only dwells in a perfect place", that there must first be the *avodah* of the lower beings in *is'arusa d'letata* and the drawing down of the *is'arusa d'le'eila* that is relative to the *is'arusa d'letata*, and then in the "perfect place," it is drawn down for the Holy One, blessed be He, to dwell, as is explained in *Likkutei Torah*, *Shir HaShirim*.

8) This is what is written, "The Jews undertook what they had begun to do," that through the *mesiras nefesh* in the time of galus, in the days of Mordechai and Esther, they accomplished the confirmation in the Torah of Matan Torah. And likewise, every year, when "these days are remembered and performed", all the matters are drawn down as they were the first time. And the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov is known that one who reads the Megillah out of order has not fulfilled his obligation, for when the reading of the Megillah is in a manner of "out of order"—meaning a matter that was in the past—he has not fulfilled his obligation, for the Megillah is an instruction in the *avodah* of man at every time and in every place and for every one, including the instruction from the above, that one stands with *mesiras nefesh*, and throughout the entire year [in whatever changes there may be], it is not possible for a Jew that an extraneous thought, G-d forbid, should cross his mind, and all the more so speech or action. And this *mesiras nefesh* brings about that "the Jews had light and gladness and joy and honor" in the literal sense, based on and with the introduction of this "light"—this is Torah—up to "honor"—these are *tefillin*, which refers to the entire matter of mitzvos, for the entire Torah is compared to *tefillin*. And with this, they also educate the *tishbar*, as is stated in the Midrash that Mordechai asked three children, "Recite to me your verses," and they answered, "Do not fear from sudden terror and from the destruction of the wicked when it comes", "Devise a plan and it will be thwarted, speak a word and it will not stand, for G-d is with us", "And until old age I am He, and until old age I will carry; I have made and I will bear, and I will carry and deliver". When Mordechai heard this, he laughed and was happy with great joy, etc. And so it will be for us, that everyone will perform his *avodah* with light and gladness and joy and honor, as all the matters explained in this in the words of our Sages of blessed memory, which include the entire Torah. And surely the Holy One, blessed be He, will fulfill His promise, "And I will give your rains in their time", and all the blessings listed in the *parsha*, until there will be light and gladness and joy and honor in the literal sense. And "linking redemption to redemption", from the redemption of Purim to the true and complete redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu. For even though for a short moment we are still servants of Achashverosh, very soon and in our days, Mashiach Tzidkeinu will come and redeem us and lead us upright to our land, speedily in our days, truly.

## [B’ezras Hashem. Purim, 5711]

### [B’ezras Hashem. Purim, 5711]

"The Jews undertook what they had already begun..." The Rebbe raises a difficulty: it is wondrous that they undertook it then, during the time of exile, and specifically during the time of Haman, when the decree was upon them—it was precisely then that they fulfilled what they had already undertaken. He resolves this by explaining that at that time, they were engaged in mesiras nefesh throughout the entire year. 

Now, to understand why it was necessary to engage specifically in avodah of mesiras nefesh in order to fulfill "what they had already begun," we must first clarify the concept of "what they had already begun," which refers to the time of Mattan Torah. As is known, the innovation of Mattan Torah was the giving of the Torah and mitzvos specifically within physicality—meaning, to nullify the decree that "the upper ones shall not descend [to the lower ones, and the lower ones shall not ascend to the upper ones]." Therefore, they were given to souls within bodies, specifically in their union together. Furthermore, even within the performance of Torah and mitzvos, there must be a union between the physical aspect of the act and its spirituality—meaning, that one makes it a mitzvah—and this is only possible after the refinement that occurs in exile.

Therefore, there are several modes of fulfilling Torah and mitzvos. The primary ones are: 

1) One performs the mitzvos as a rote habit, without vitality, or "not for their own sake" (shelo lishmah), such as to be called a "rabbi" or similar motivations, which lacks vitality. Or, one performs them out of a fear of Hashem, which is also not vitality, but rather, on the contrary, a form of tzimtzum and constriction. 

2) One performs the aforementioned out of a love for Havayah with great vitality, which stems from the soul as it expands and gains strength. This category generally includes avodah based on nature, or a habit that has become second nature, or avodah based on intellectual understanding (to'am v'da'as). There is also a level above intellectual understanding, where one does not exist as an independent entity, but simply desires to fulfill the will of the Creator.

Now, these same distinctions exist within Torah study itself. There is Torah study for the sake of knowing the practical law, which is indeed the ultimate purpose; consequently, this involves limitations—such as the laws of the festivals during the festivals, and so on—and it is specifically in the "four cubits" [of Halacha] that we study. Then there is the study of Torah for its own sake, where one is "constantly intoxicated by its love" due to its inherent excellence, at least according to intellectual understanding. And higher than all of the above is study "for its own sake" in truth—for the sake of the Torah itself—which is above intellectual understanding. At that level, one also connects the Torah, which "emanates from Chochma," to its source in Keter. 

And through what can this be achieved? It is also through showing, within oneself, that one is in a state of mesiras nefesh. There must always be a connection between the Torah and its source; for, seemingly, one could say...

## Purim 5711

### Purim 5711

"The Jews accepted what they had begun to do". My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, explains in his maamar on this subject (which has now been published) that they accepted what they had begun at the time of the Giving of the Torah.
