The Rebbe (Rashab), the *baal hahillula* of the 2nd of Nissan, notes in his *maamar* titled *Zeh* from the year 5668, why the verse says "head of the months" and then adds "it shall be the first of the months of the year"—what is the need for the term "head of the months"? He brings the teaching of our Sages on this verse: "This month shall be for you the head of the months—this is Nissan, and there is no other Nissan," meaning that when the day arrives that is fit to be Rosh Chodesh Nissan, we do not intercalate the year. He continues in the *maamar* that from this verse we also learn that we do not intercalate the month, for we intercalate the year for a need, but we do not intercalate the month for a need. And when the day arrives that is fit to be Rosh Chodesh Nissan, we do not intercalate the year either.

To understand this, the *maamar* prefaces that one must understand why the *parsha* of *HaChodesh* was stated specifically immediately after the exodus from Egypt. It explains that the *parsha* of *HaChodesh* is the essential beginning of the Torah, as Rashi states that the Torah should not have begun anywhere but from "This month shall be for you" (and the reason it opens with *Bereishis* is because of "He declared the power of His works to His people"). This means that the *parshiyos* prior to *Parshas HaChodesh* speak of the revelations within the worlds, while from *Parshas HaChodesh* onward, the subject of the revelations of Torah (which are higher than the revelations within the worlds) begins. In order for the revelation of Torah to be possible, there first had to be the preparation of the exile of Egypt and the exodus from Egypt. This is why immediately after the exodus—the preparation for the Giving of the Torah—the *parsha* of *HaChodesh* was stated, marking the beginning of the revelations of Torah.

2. In order to explain the greatness of the transcendence of the revelations of Torah, the *maamar* first explains the transcendence of the revelations within the worlds, noting that the revelation within the worlds is higher than the worlds themselves. The worlds are in a state of measurement and limitation; just as the heavens and earth below (in this physical world) are measured and limited—from the earth to the firmament is a journey of 500 years, the thickness of each firmament, etc.—so too is it regarding the heavens and earth in the spiritual worlds of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*, which are in a state of measurement and limitation. The root of the limitation of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah* is from the *keilim* of *Atzilus*, from which it is understood that even the *keilim* of *Atzilus*, despite being *Elokus*, are in a state of *gevul* (limitation). This is not the case for the revelation within the worlds—the *ohr* (the *Kav*) that is drawn into them, whose root is from the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is before the *Tzimtzum*—which is in a state of *bli gevul* (limitlessness). It is only through its enclothement in the *keilim* that it comes into a state of configuration and division. However, the fact that it comes into a state of configuration and limitation through its enclothement in the *keilim* is because, even in its own right (before it is enclothed in the *keilim*), it is within the category of limitation. Since its drawing forth from the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum* is through the interruption of the *Tzimtzum*, it is measured (in its totality) according to the capacity of the *keilim* and the worlds. Therefore, after it is drawn (through several *masachim* and *parsaois* before *Atzilus*) into the *keilim* of *Atzilus*, it enclothes itself in them and comes into a limited configuration, and through this, it is drawn and enclothes itself (via the *parsah* between *Atzilus* and *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*) also into the created beings, to vivify them with a vitality that is limited according to their capacity.

3. It can be said that the statement in the *maamar* that the revelation within the worlds is the *Ohr HaKav* (which is drawn into them) is to explain that even the revelation within the worlds is *bli gevul*. Since the *Ohr HaKav* is attached to its source, the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum* (even though its attachment is through the interruption of the *Tzimtzum*), it is like its source. Therefore, even after it is enclothed in the *keilim* (in their interior) and vivifies them, it is not grasped by them. This is because the *ohr* enclothing itself in the *keilim* in an internal enclothement, similar to the vitality of the *neshamah* enclothed in the body, is (primarily) regarding the *keilim*—that the *keilim* receive their vitality from the *ohr* as an internal vitality (like the body that lives from the *neshamah*)—but regarding the *ohr* itself, even after it is enclothed in the *keilim*, it remains distinct from them. The same applies to created beings; the vitality of the created beings that is in the category of *ohr*, which is a *ha'arah* of a *ha'arah* of a *ha'arah* from the *Kav*, even while it is enclothed in the created beings, is holy and distinct from them. Furthermore, even the vitality of the created beings that is drawn into them from the *ohr* is unlimited [and the connection of the vitality from the *ohr* with the body of the created being (whose root is from the *keilim*) is a connection of two opposites]. All this is regarding the revelation within the worlds (*Ohr HaKav*) as it is drawn into the *keilim* and the worlds. And all the more so, the *ohr* as it is before it is drawn into the *keilim* and the worlds (for "revelation within the worlds" also includes this level) is unlimited. What is called *gevul* is only because, through the *Tzimtzum*, the measurement was created within it so that when it is drawn into the worlds (via *masachim* and *parsaois*), the drawing forth will be in a manner that the worlds can receive. From this, we understand the greatness of the transcendence of the revelations of Torah, which are higher than the revelation within the worlds, for through the Torah, the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is before the *Tzimtzum* is drawn forth and revealed.

4. The *maamar* continues that the measurement in the *Kav*, while it is through the *Tzimtzum* (as mentioned above), has its root in the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum*. When it arose in His will, blessed be He, to create the worlds, He estimated within Himself in potential (before the *Tzimtzum*) everything that would be in actuality after the *Tzimtzum*, so that the coming into being of the worlds would be as it arose in His will, blessed be He. It explains that even the root of the measurement before the *Tzimtzum* (the estimation in potential, and also the rising of the will) is included in the revelations within the worlds. For although the totality of the *ohr* before the *Tzimtzum* is not in the category of the worlds, and in order for an *ohr* that is in the category of a source for the worlds (*Ohr HaKav*) to be drawn from it, there had to be a removal of the *ohr*, nevertheless, in its very *Atzmus*, so to speak, it is a revelation that belongs to the worlds. From this, the greatness of the transcendence of the revelations of Torah is understood even more, for through the Torah is the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above the category of belonging to the worlds.

It can be said that what is stated in the *maamar* (at its beginning) that the intention in the giving of the Torah is to draw forth the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum*—even though the transcendence of the Torah is that even within the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum* itself, the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is higher than the *ohr* belonging to the worlds is drawn forth—is because even the drawing forth of the *ohr* belonging to the worlds (before the *Tzimtzum*) is through a *Tzimtzum*. For although the *Tzimtzum* that occurred in this *ohr* (belonging to the worlds) is called the *Tzimtzum HaRishon*, it is explained elsewhere that even before the *Tzimtzum HaRishon* there were various *tzimtzumim*. For in addition to the fact that the drawing forth of the *ohr* belonging to the worlds from the *ohr* that is above the belonging to the worlds is through a *Tzimtzum* as mentioned above, the drawing forth of the *ohr* that is above the belonging to the worlds is also through a *Tzimtzum*. Since even this *ohr* (that is above the belonging to the worlds) is in the category of drawing forth and revelation, and before that it was included in the *Atzmus* of the *Maor* itself [and in particular, it can be said that the meaning of "included in the *Atzmus* of the *Maor* itself" is that it was included in the *Atzmus* that is above the description of *Maor*], it is understood that the drawing forth of the *ohr* that is in the category of revelation from the *ohr* included in the *Atzmus* of the *Maor* is through a tremendous *Tzimtzum*. Based on this, it can be said that what is stated in the *maamar* that the intention in the giving of the Torah is to draw forth the *ohr* before the *Tzimtzum*—the intention here is (not only to the *ohr* before the *Tzimtzum HaRishon*, but) also to the *ohr* before all the *tzimtzumim*.

5. Based on this, one can also explain what is stated in the *maamar* that the *parsha* of *HaChodesh* was stated immediately after the exodus from Egypt. Seemingly, this is puzzling, for it is explicit in the verse that the *parsha* of *HaChodesh* was stated in the land of Egypt. It can be said that what is stated in the *maamar* that the *parsha* of *HaChodesh* was stated after the exodus from Egypt refers to after the telling and recounting of the exodus from Egypt (the exodus as it is in the Torah). After all the commands (in the Torah) were stated to Pharaoh to send the Jewish people from Egypt, up to the final warning of the plague of the firstborn, after which Pharaoh said, "Go, you and all the people," immediately after the telling of the exodus, the *parsha* of *HaChodesh* was stated. The matter is that the superiority of the revelation of Torah over the revelation of the worlds (explained in this *maamar*) is also over the revelation of the worlds as it is in the Torah. This is what the *maamar* brings from Rashi: "The Torah should not have begun anywhere but from 'This month shall be for you,' and what is the reason it opens with *Bereishis*?"—that even the subject of "In the beginning G-d created" (and similarly the stories of the Patriarchs, etc.) as it is in the Torah is only (like) a preface to the *parsha* of *HaChodesh*, for this *parsha* is the *essential* beginning of the Torah. Therefore, also regarding the subject of the exodus from Egypt, which is a preparation for the Giving of the Torah, the *maamar* speaks of the subject of the exodus as it is in the Torah.

It is possible to connect this matter [that the revelation of Torah is higher even than the revelation of the worlds as it is in the Torah] with what was explained above (section 4) that the revelation of Torah is the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above all the *tzimtzumim* (even the *tzimtzumim* before the *Tzimtzum HaRishon*), based on the well-known principle that the totality of the entire Torah (even the stories of the Torah before *Parshas HaChodesh*) preceded the world. Since the beginning of the subject of "world" is from the *Tzimtzum HaRishon*, the Torah that preceded the world is in the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum*. Furthermore, it can be said that since even in the *ohr* before the *Tzimtzum* there is already the beginning of the root of the measurement of the worlds, the Torah that preceded the world is in the *ohr* that is in the category of revelation to itself, which is above the belonging to the worlds. This is also true in the stories of the Torah before *Parshas HaChodesh*. And the transcendence of *Parshas HaChodesh*, the essential beginning of the Torah, is the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* before all the *tzimtzumim*.

6. This is "This month shall be for you the head of the months," for in the month of Nissan is the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*. This is why when Rosh Chodesh Nissan arrives, we do not intercalate the year, because the category of "year" is in the *ohr* belonging to the worlds. And since the drawing forth of this *ohr* is through the elevation of *man*, we intercalate the year for a need, so that the elevation of *man* will be as it should be. Therefore, when Rosh Chodesh Nissan arrives—the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *is'arusa d'lesata* (arousal from below)—we do not intercalate the year, as is explained at length in the *maamar*. This is also why the month of Nissan is the month of redemption, for "in Nissan they were redeemed and in Nissan they are destined to be redeemed," because the subject of the redemption is that then "your Teacher will no longer be hidden," the revelation of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* without any garment.

May it be His will that all this will be speedily in our days, in this month of Nissan of this year, and we will eat there from the sacrifices and from the Pesach offerings, with joy and goodness of heart.

## Vayikra, 3 Nissan 5732

"And He called to Moshe, and Hashem spoke to him from the Tent of Meeting, saying: When a person (adam) among you brings an offering..." We must understand: every utterance, every statement, and every commandment was preceded by a "calling"—an expression of affection. Why, then, is this calling explicitly stated specifically in the parsha of the offerings ? 

Simply put, one could say that the reason "And He called" is written in the parsha of the offerings is because this parsha was the beginning of the speech from the Tent of Meeting. But this itself requires explanation: why was the beginning of the speech from the Tent of Meeting specifically the parsha of the offerings ? This implies that the revelation of Torah (the essence of the Tent of Meeting) is primarily found in the laws of the offerings. It can be said that this is also why the book of Vayikra (the book of offerings) is the most "severe" (weighty) of the books. This is also why it is the third of the five books of the Torah; the virtue of the Torah—that it is a "threefold Torah" (Orian Talisa'i) —is revealed more fully in the laws of the offerings of the Torah. And this is why the fact that all the utterances of the Torah were preceded by a calling of affection is learned from what is written in the parsha of the offerings: because even for this virtue of the Torah, the primary revelation is in the laws of the offerings.

2) This can be explained based on what the Rebbe (Rashab) of blessed memory, the *baal hahillula* of the 2nd of Nissan, said in his maamar on the verse "When a person (adam) brings an offering" : Seemingly, it should have said "When you bring an offering", without mentioning who is bringing it. He brings in the maamar that it is stated in the Zohar : "Fortunate are Yisrael, for Hashem calls them *adam*... 'When a person (adam) among you brings an offering'—why does He call them *adam*? Because it is written, 'And you who cleave to Hashem your G-d...'" 

Based on this, he explains why it says "When an *adam* brings an offering" (specifically *adam*): because the power that Yisrael has to bring offerings is because they are in the category of *adam*, meaning they are cleaved to the Holy One, blessed be He. He continues in the maamar that a similar idea is found in the Midrash : that the reason the Holy One, blessed be He, commanded Yisrael to bring offerings is because "they are cleaved to Me," as it is written, "For as the belt cleaves to the loins of a man..." He explains in the maamar that the Midrash speaks of the cleaving of *keilim* (vessels), where the *keilim* are in a state of *metzius* (independent existence), and their union (cleaving) is not an essential union—like the belt that cleaves to a man, where the belt is an existence unto itself, only it is attached to the man. The Zohar, however, speaks of the cleaving of *oros* (lights), where the cleaving and union of the *oros* is an essential union. Therefore, it cites the verse "And you who cleave to Hashem your G-d"—a literal cleaving.

3) Based on this, it can be said that the reason it is written at the beginning of the book of offerings "When an *adam* brings an offering"—and the reason Yisrael is called *adam* is because of "And you who cleave," which is the cleaving of *oros*—is that the power Yisrael has to bring offerings is (primarily) because their cleaving to the Holy One, blessed be He, is at the level of the cleaving of *oros*. 

And regarding the fact that the Midrash states that the bringing of offerings is connected to the level of cleaving (of the belt) of *keilim*, it can be said that the essence of the offerings is that through them the world is sustained [as it is stated in the Yerushalmi on the verse "To plant the heavens and to found the earth"—these are the offerings, for through the offerings the statutes of heaven and earth are sustained]. However, this power that exists in the offerings comes because the one who brings the offerings is an *adam* (who is above the world), as it is stated in the Zohar that the reason it says *adam* brings an offering [among the four names—*adam, gever, enosh, ish* —the name *adam* denotes the highest level, as it is written, "And G-d created the *adam* in His image..."] is because the existence of the upper and lower worlds is through the offerings. 

Therefore, in the *avodah* of the *adam* who brings the offering, there must also be two aspects. The matter of the offerings themselves is through the *avodah* of the *adam* at the level of the *keilim* within him. [For the fact that the offerings sustain the world is because they contain all the types of the inanimate, vegetative, animate, and speaking of the world—the offerings themselves, which come from the inanimate, vegetative, and animate, and the *adam* who brings them]. And the power in the offerings to sustain the world—because the one who brings them is an *adam* who is separated from the world—is through the *avodah* of the *adam* at the level of the *oros* within him.

This is why the concept of "When an *adam* brings an offering" is specifically at the beginning of the book of offerings: because the offerings themselves are from the animal, etc., which also includes the "animal" within the *adam*—the *nefesh habehamis*. This is accomplished through the aspect of *Ban* of the *nefesh elokis* —*Ban* in *gematria* is *behemah* (animal) —which in general is the *neshamah* as it is at the level of *keilim*. But the power for this is through the aspect of *Mah* of the *nefesh elokis*—*Mah* in *gematria* is *adam* —which in general is the level of *oros* of the *neshamah*. Therefore, the opening of the book of offerings is "*Adam* when he brings an offering."

4) It is known that Torah and *mitzvos* are analogous to *oros* and *keilim*. Based on this, it can be said that the two aforementioned levels of Yisrael's cleaving to the Holy One, blessed be He, are achieved through the study of Torah and the performance of *mitzvos*. Through the performance of *mitzvos*—the cleaving of *keilim*; and through the engagement in Torah—the cleaving of *oros*. 

Based on this, one can connect this to what is stated in *Tikkunei Zohar*, that the three prayers—*Shacharis, Mincha, and Maariv*—correspond to the lion, the ox, and the eagle, and the matter of "When an *adam* brings an offering" is the Torah. And because of this, they instituted in the prayer to read the Torah, so that there should be in it a complete offering through the *adam*, etc., "This is the Torah of the *adam*". The explanation for this is that the fact that prayers correspond to offerings is against the offerings themselves [for the matter of prayer is the refinement of the *nefesh habehamis* in the manner of bringing the animal], while the matter of "When an *adam* brings an offering" is the Torah—this is the power of Yisrael to bring offerings because they are in the category of *adam*, the cleaving of *oros* that is achieved through engagement in Torah.

Based on this, we can explain why the beginning of the speech from the Tent of Meeting was the parsha of the offerings: because the revelation of Torah is primarily in the laws of the offerings (as mentioned in section 1), for the fact that through the offerings the refinement of the world is accomplished, and likewise that through them the world is sustained, is by the power of the Torah—"When an *adam* brings an offering" is the Torah. This can be connected to the second day of Nissan, on which Nesanel ben Tzuar, the prince of Yissachar, brought his offering. The fact that he merited to bring on the second day for the tribes is because the children of Yissachar were knowledgeable in Torah, and because they gave advice to the princes to volunteer these offerings. It can be said that the two matters are dependent on one another: because they were knowledgeable in Torah, and the revelation of Torah is in the matter of the offerings, therefore they gave the advice to volunteer the offerings.

5) We must understand: the Torah is above the world, as explained in *Tanya* that the fact that Dovid was punished for calling the words of Torah "songs" is because Dovid was praising the Torah by saying that all the worlds depend on one of the minutiae of the Torah, and he was punished for this, because regarding the Torah, being united with the *Ohr Ein Sof*, all the worlds are considered as nothing, and it is not appropriate that the revelation of Torah should be in the matter of the offerings, which are the refinement and sustenance of the world.

The explanation for this is that the fact that the Torah is above the worlds is the Torah as it is from its own perspective. But from the perspective of the root of the Torah, as it is rooted in the *Atzmus* (Essence)—since His Essence, blessed be He, is not defined, G-d forbid, by any definition, not even by the fact that all the worlds are as nothing before Him—therefore, through His desire to have a dwelling place in the lower worlds, that He should have *nachas ruach* and *taanug* from the *avodah* of the lower beings, it is brought about in the Torah as well that the matters of the world occupy a place, so to speak, regarding the Torah. Based on this, we can explain why Dovid was praising the Torah by saying that the existence of all the worlds depends on one of the minutiae of the Torah, even though the Torah is above the worlds: because from the perspective of the root of the Torah, as it is rooted in the *Atzmus*—which desired to have a dwelling place in the lower worlds, and that the dwelling place in the lower worlds should be through the Torah—the fact that the vitality of all the worlds depends on one of the minutiae of the Torah is the praise of the Torah, so to speak. [And the reason he was punished for this is because praise is appropriate for revelation, and from the perspective of the revelation of the Torah, all the worlds are as nothing.]

6) Based on this, we can explain what is stated in *Tanya*, after it says that the Holy One, blessed be He, contracted His will and wisdom and brought them down below, that therefore the Torah is compared to water. For just as water descends from a high place to a low place, so too the Torah descended from its place of honor, which is His will and wisdom, which no thought can grasp at all, and from there it traveled and descended until it was clothed in physical things and the matters of this world. Seemingly, this requires explanation: the fact that the Torah descended from above to below is understood even without the parable of water; what is added to the understanding of the matter of the Torah's descent from above to below by the parable of water? 

The intention here is that just as with water, the fact that it descends from a high place to a low place is because even when it was in the high place, it had no connection to the (high) place where it was found; on the contrary, the fact that by *nature* it is drawn specifically to a low place indicates the connection of the water (even while in the high place) to the low place. Likewise, it is with the Torah, which is compared to water: the fact that it descended from above to below does not mean that its place is above and in its descent below it is found not in its place, but rather that its true place (even while it is above) is below.

It should be added that this is what is said in *Tanya* there at the end of the chapter, that what is said, "And His right hand embraces me," refers to the Torah, which is the aspect of *Chesed* and water. The fact that he specifies *Chesed* and water (even though to explain the reason why the Torah is called "His right hand," it would seemingly have been enough to say it is the aspect of *Chesed*) is because the *hemshech* (drawing down) from above to below in *Chesed* is in a manner where the "above" still occupies a place. Like the influence of a person of *Chesed*: in that he influences another (a *hemshech* below), the *Chesed* of the *influencer* is felt. This is not so with the *hemshech* from above to below of water, which is in a manner that they *leave* the high place and go (specifically) to the low place. This is why he adds that the Torah is the aspect of (Chesed and) water: that the fact that the Torah descended from above to below is in the manner of the descent of water, *that they leave* the high place, for the essence of the Torah is specifically below. As is explained in other places, the fact that the Torah is in every place, above and below, is the Torah as it is in the aspect of revelations, but the Torah as it is rooted in the *Atzmus* was given specifically below.

7) This is "This is the Torah of the *adam*," that the Torah is compared to an *adam*, for in an *adam* there are two matters. That he is called *adam* on account of *adameh l'elyon* (I will be like the Most High), and that he is called *adam* on account of being created from the *adamah* (earth), as it is stated in the Midrash that the first *adam* said: "I am fit to be called *adam* because I was created from the *adamah*." It is explained in Chassidus that the reason the first *adam* called his name after the matter (body) and not after the form (soul) is because the first *adam* in his wisdom grasped that the fact that his body was created from the *adamah* [inanimate, and not like other creatures whose bodies were vegetative], has an advantage even over the virtue of his soul, because everything that is higher, higher, falls lower, lower. 

This is "This is the Torah of the *adam*," for also in the Torah there are these two matters. That the Torah is above the world, to the point that all the worlds are as nothing before it, is in the manner of the name *adam* on account of *adameh l'elyon*. And that the Torah descended below specifically because its root is in the *Atzmus* is in the manner of the name *adam* on account of being created from the *adamah*, because its root is in the "higher, higher."

8) The two matters in the aspect of the "water" of the Torah—that the Torah descends below, and that through the descent below specifically the root of the Torah is revealed as it is rooted in the *Atzmus*—are primarily in the *halachos* of the Torah. The matter of *halachos* is a final ruling regarding action—a descent to the lowest level—and the root of the *halachos* is higher than the root of the other parts of the Torah. As is explained in *Hemshech Tarsav*, the fact that "these and these are the words of the living G-d" is specifically the aspect of *Elokim*, while *halachos* are from the name *Havayah*, as our Sages say, "And *Havayah* is with him" —that the *halacha* follows him—for the intention of the name *Havayah* here is even to the level of His name that is included in His Essence, the capability of the *Atzmus*.

It should be added that the fact that the aspect of the "water" of the Torah is primarily in the *halachos* of the Torah is also regarding what our Sages said that the words of Torah are compared to water, for just as water leaves a high place and goes to a low place, so too the words of Torah only endure in one whose mind is humble. As is explained in *Hemshech Tarsav* that in order to arrive at the *halacha* in its truth, it is through *bitul* (self-nullification) and humility. This is why the *halacha* was established according to Beis Hillel, because they were gentle and humble, for through their *bitul* they reached the essential concealment.

Based on this, we can add an explanation to what was explained above (section 4), that the fact that the children of Yissachar gave advice to volunteer offerings is because they were knowledgeable in Torah, and the revelation of Torah is in the matter of the offerings. For the fact that the tribe of Yissachar brought on the second day and preceded the tribe of Yehudah before them is from the side of their *bitul* [similar to how Beis Hillel preceded the words of Beis Shammai to their own], and therefore they merited to arrive at the *halacha* in its truth, as explained in the discourses of the Rebbe Maharash. Based on this, it can be said that the fact that their knowledge in Torah brought them to the matter of the offerings—the refinement and sustenance of the world—is because through their engagement in Torah being in *bitul*, they reached the essence of the Torah as it is rooted in the *Atzmus*, and therefore it was felt by them that the purpose of the knowledge of Torah is to complete the intention of the *Atzmus*, to make for Him, blessed be He, a dwelling place in the lower worlds.

9) This is "And He called to Moshe, and Hashem spoke... When an *adam* brings an offering..." The fact that all the utterances, etc., were preceded by a calling—an expression of affection—is also a precedence in virtue, for the calling is above the speech. To the point that it is said "And He called" generally, without saying who is calling, because the calling is from the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *Havayah*. However, even so, from the fact that the calling was specifically before the speech (even though a calling of affection is a matter in its own right, and not a preparation for the speech), it is understood that the calling also belongs to the speech. It can be said that the utterances, statements, and commandments of the Torah are from *Havayah*, as is said in all the utterances, etc., of the Torah (and also in the speech in this verse), "And Hashem spoke," while the calling is from the *Atzmus* as mentioned above. The matter of "And He called" and "And He spoke" is the *hemshech* and revelation of the *Atzmus* (which is above Torah) in the speech of the Torah. In general, it is the *hemshech* of the Torah as it is rooted in the *Atzmus*. 

This can be connected to what is said, "And He called" with a small *aleph*, and according to the teaching of the Alter Rebbe (who said to the Tzemach Tzedek when he was three years old), that the small *aleph* of *Vayikra* indicates the humility and *bitul* of Moshe. For even though he knew his own virtues, he did not consider himself, and on the contrary, this knowledge brought him to be even more humble in his own eyes (as explained there at length). It can be said that one of the reasons why the small *aleph*—which hints at the *bitul* of Moshe—is in the word *Vayikra* is because through his *bitul* he became a vessel for the revelation of *Vayikra*, the *hemshech* and revelation of the Torah as it is rooted in the *Atzmus* (similar to what was explained above regarding Yissachar). Based on this, we can understand why it is said "And He called" in the parsha of the offerings, even though all the utterances, etc., were preceded by a calling, because this matter of the Torah—that it is rooted in the *Atzmus*—is revealed primarily in that the Torah deals with the laws of the offerings, to refine the world and make it a dwelling place for Him, blessed be He.

It is known that in every single Jew there is an aspect of Moshe, including the *bitul* of Moshe. From this it is understood that the matter of "And He called to Moshe" applies also to every single Jew, especially when he studies this verse. Through this, he is also drawn the empowerment for the *avodah* of the offerings in spirituality, and this becomes a close preparation for the *avodah* of the offerings in its literal sense, and there it will be done before You according to the commandments of Your will, in the Third Beis Hamikdash, which will be built speedily in our days, and very soon.

## Shabbos Parshas Tazria, Rosh Chodesh Nissan 5725

“When a woman conceives and gives birth to a male,” and our Sages of blessed memory state: “If the woman conceives first, she gives birth to a male; if the man conceives first, she gives birth to a female.” The Alter Rebbe (in *Likkutei Torah*, discourse beginning *Zeh*) cites the philosophers who explain that the reason a woman conceiving first results in a male is because when the woman conceives first, the man’s drop comes last, and “the superior one prevails” (*ilaya gavar*). The Alter Rebbe notes that this interpretation is a great stretch, and he explains—as is evident from the simple meaning of the text—that the birth of a male comes from the woman’s seed (and not from the fact that the man’s seed arrives last). It can be said that the reason *Likkutei Torah* also brings the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the man’s drop (which arrives last, because *ilaya gavar*), and furthermore, that it places this interpretation before the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the woman’s seed, is because man and woman (in spirituality) are Hashem and Knesses Yisrael; Hashem is called “man” and Knesses Yisrael is called “woman.” It is known regarding *is’arusa d’leila* (arousal from above) and *is’arusa d’lata* (arousal from below) that each has its own merit. The merit of *is’arusa d’lata* is that the *hemshech* (drawing down) that comes through it is from the *pnimiyus* (inwardness) of the light. The merit of *is’arusa d’leila* in its own right is that the *hemshech* is from a higher place that *is’arusa d’lata* cannot reach (though the *hemshech* from there is only from the aspect of *chitzoniyus*). It can be said that the two interpretations of “When a woman conceives and gives birth to a male” according to its simple meaning derive from this: the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the man’s seed, because *ilaya gavar*, derives from the merit of *is’arusa d’leila*, while the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the woman’s seed derives from the merit of *is’arusa d’lata*. Therefore, it also brings the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the man’s seed (even though, physically, this interpretation is contrary to the final conclusion) in order to hint that there is also a merit to *is’arusa d’leila*.

b) It should be added that we read Parshas Tazria (in many years) in the month of Nissan, whose theme is *is’arusa d’leila*. Therefore, it brings this interpretation (that the birth of a male is from the man’s seed) *before* the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the woman’s seed, because in the month of Nissan, the emphasis is on the merit of *is’arusa d’leila*. The fact that the final conclusion is that the birth of a male is from the woman’s seed is because after the giving of the Torah, even in the month of Nissan, the emphasis is on the merit of man’s *avodah* (*is’arusa d’lata*). This is also understood from the discourse *HaChodesh* by the Rebbe (Rashab) *n.e.*, the *baal hahillula* of the 2nd of Nissan, where he emphasizes (at the beginning of the discourse): “Why is *avodah* by one’s own strength necessary, and what is the merit in this? Are not the *ahavah* and *yirah* that come from above much higher?” The fact that he emphasizes this (what is the merit of *avodah* by one’s own strength), even though in many other *drushim* the merit of *is’arusa d’lata* (*avodah* by one’s own strength) is explained (as above), can be explained by the fact that in the discourse *HaChodesh*, the merit of *is’arusa d’leila* is highlighted, and therefore there is room for the question of what the merit of *avodah* is. But from the fact that this matter (what is the merit of *avodah*) is only a question, it is understood that even in the month of Nissan (*HaChodesh*), in which the merit of the *hemshech* coming from above is highlighted, the conclusion is that there is (also) a merit to *avodah* by one’s own strength (*is’arusa d’lata*) over the *ahavah* and *yirah* that come from above (*is’arusa d’leila*). Furthermore, this matter (the merit of *is’arusa d’lata*) is the *primary* merit, which is why the *conclusion* (in *Likkutei Torah*) is that the birth of a male is from the woman’s seed—the merit of *is’arusa d’lata*.

c) The matter is as follows: the merit of *is’arusa d’leila* (in its own right) is that the *hemshech* is from a place that *is’arusa d’lata* cannot reach, and the *hemshech* is because “He desires kindness.” According to what is known regarding the matter of “With whom did He consult? With the souls of the righteous” (and “Your people are all righteous”), the fact that He was, so to speak, aroused to be one who “desires kindness” is because it arose in the Thought (the essential Thought, which is above the revelation of “desiring kindness”) that Yisrael would fulfill *Torah* and *mitzvos* and make a dwelling place for Him in the lower realms. It is understood that the root of Yisrael’s *avodah* is higher even than the matter of “desiring kindness.” And through the revelation of “desiring kindness” being aroused and drawn down by man’s *avodah*, a higher excellence is added to it (beyond what it is in its own right, from above), as there is drawn into it from the essential Thought that is above revelation. Based on this, it can be explained that the reason we read “When a woman conceives and gives birth to a male” (emphasizing the merit of *is’arusa d’lata*) in the month of Nissan (whose theme is *hemshech* from above) is because the primary excellence in the *hemshech* from above (and the revelation of “desiring kindness”) is when it is drawn down through *avodah*.

d) Now, this matter (that a woman conceiving first results in a male indicates the merit of *is’arusa d’lata*) applies also to the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the man’s drop that arrives last, because *ilaya gavar*. However, according to this interpretation, the merit of *is’arusa d’lata* (the woman’s seed) is that it is the cause for the *is’arusa d’leila* drawn down through it to be from a higher aspect (*ilaya gavar*), whereas according to the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the woman’s seed, the merit of *is’arusa d’lata* is (also) in its own right. Based on this, one can explain the hint in the order of the two interpretations: the reason it precedes with the interpretation that the birth of a male is from the man’s seed, even though the conclusion is that it is from the woman’s seed, is because at the beginning of *avodah*, the merit of *is’arusa d’lata* is that the *is’arusa d’leila* drawn down through it is higher, and through this, the merit of *is’arusa d’lata* itself is revealed later.

This will be understood according to what is explained in *Likkutei Torah* that there is a third aspect in *is’arusa d’leila*, which is by way of a gift. This *is’arusa d’leila* is also drawn from a place that *is’arusa d’lata* cannot reach at all, but in order for there to be the revelation of this *is’arusa d’leila*, there must first be the perfection of the actions of the lower beings, the *is’arusa d’lata* and the *is’arusa d’leila* drawn down through it. One must understand: the intention that Hashem desired to have a dwelling place in the lower realms is that the making of the dwelling place should be through man’s *avodah*. From this it is understood that even the highest revelations, even the light that is above relevance to created beings (which *is’arusa d’lata* has no power to reach), and up to the revelation of the *Atzmus* (Essence), are drawn down through man’s *avodah*. How does this fit with what was brought above from *Likkutei Torah* that through *avodah* only that which *is’arusa d’lata* can reach is drawn down, and the drawing down of the aspects above that is by way of a gift from above? The explanation for this is that the fact that *is’arusa d’lata* reaches only the light relevant to the worlds is because of the matter of created beings [*is’arusa d’lata* as it is in the aspect of “tata” (lower), which is distinct from “eila” (higher)]. But from the perspective of the power of the *Atzmus* that exists in the *avodah* of Yisrael [as explained above (section c) regarding “With whom did He consult? With the souls of the righteous,” that the root of Yisrael’s *avodah* is in the essential Thought that is in His *Atzmus*, which is above all revelations, even the highest revelations], it is not relevant to distinguish between “tata” and “eila,” for Yisrael and the Holy One, blessed be He, are one. Rather, the revelation of this matter (the root of Yisrael’s *avodah* as it is in the essential Thought) is specifically after everything relevant to man’s *avodah* is completed—the *avodah* itself (*is’arusa d’lata*) and the *is’arusa d’leila* that comes through it. Therefore, the beginning of the *hemshech* that comes through *avodah* (before the power of the *Atzmus* within it is revealed) is only from the light relevant to the worlds, a place that *is’arusa d’lata* reaches. But after the perfection of *avodah* (both the *avodah* itself and the *is’arusa d’leila* that comes through it), when its “inwardness” is revealed—the satisfaction and *taanug* (pleasure) that is above in the *avodah* of Yisrael, “satisfaction before Me that I said and My will was done”—this satisfaction and *taanug* (that My will was done, through Yisrael) is in His *Atzmus* (“satisfaction before”), and through the *avodah*, the light that is above relevance to the worlds is also drawn down, up to the drawing down of the *Atzmus*.

e) Based on this, one can explain the order of the two interpretations of “When a woman conceives and gives birth to a male”: that at first (before the “inwardness” of Yisrael’s *avodah* is revealed), the *avodah* of Knesses Yisrael and the *hemshech* from above are two separate things, and consequently, the *hemshech* that comes through it is from the light relevant to the worlds (which *is’arusa d’lata* reaches), and the merit in Yisrael’s *avodah* then is that the *avodah* is a *cause* for the *is’arusa d’leila* drawn down through it to be from the inwardness of the light—the first interpretation, that the woman’s seed is a cause for the strengthening of the man’s seed (*ilaya gavar*). And through this, the inwardness of Yisrael’s *avodah* is revealed, the power of the *Atzmus* within it (from the fact that Yisrael and the Holy One, blessed be He, are one), and all the *hemshech*s are through the *avodah* itself (and not that the *avodah* is only a cause for the *hemshech*)—the second interpretation, that the birth of a male is from the woman’s seed, and the merit of the *hemshech* from above (the birth of a male) is (not only the drawing down of the inwardness of the light relevant to the worlds, but also) the drawing down of the light that is above relevance to the worlds, up to the drawing down of the *Atzmus*.

f) One should connect this with the second day of Nissan, for according to the opinion that the world was created in Nissan, meaning that the creation of man was on Rosh Chodesh Nissan and the creation of the world was on the 25th of Adar, the first Shabbos was on the second of Nissan. According to what is known regarding the reconciliation of the two opinions on the time of creation, that the actual creation was in Tishrei (the 25th of Elul) and the thought to create the world was in Nissan (the 25th of Adar), it can be said that the actual creation in Tishrei included the revelation of “desiring kindness,” and the fact that in Nissan there was the thought to create can be said to be similar to the thought of “With whom did He consult,” the intention and purpose of creation that was not drawn down into revelation in the creation. On the Shabbos, the 2nd of Nissan, the perfection of the thought was achieved. One should also connect this with the fact that the 2nd of Nissan is the *hillula* day of the Rebbe (Rashab) *n.e.*, and the day on which the Rebbe (Rayatz) (the only son of the *baal hahillula*) was crowned with the crown of leadership, because the intention and purpose of creation is that there should be in the world the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above relevance to the worlds (as above), and this revelation is (primarily) through the study and dissemination of the inwardness of the Torah. The inwardness of the Torah in its own right is above revelation (*satim d’oraisa*), and through the study of the inwardness of the Torah with understanding and comprehension, and especially through disseminating it outward (and to the furthest outward), one draws down the light that is above relevance to the worlds, up to the essence of the light that is above revelation, so that it will be in revelation in the world. This is the connection of the 2nd of Nissan, the first Shabbos of the work of creation in thought (in which the perfection of the thought was achieved) to the *baal hahillula* and his only son who succeeded him, because through the *drushim* of the *baal hahillula*, who is the Rambam of the Torah of Chassidus (things that are explained until it is orderly in everyone’s mouth), and especially through the founding of Tomchei Temimim, a new stage began in the dissemination of the wellsprings. And after this, through the Rebbe (Rayatz), the dissemination was done with even greater strength and power. This (the dissemination of the wellsprings outward) is the preparation and the vessel for the coming of our righteous Moshiach (when the intention of the dwelling place in the lower realms will be completed), very soon.

## 11 Nissan 5731

"On the eleventh day, the prince of the children of Asher [offered his sacrifice]." It is stated in the Midrash that all the tribes had their names chosen for the sake of the redemption of Israel and for their praise. Asher’s name was chosen for the sake of Israel’s redemption, as it is written, "And all the nations shall call you fortunate (ashru), for you shall be a desirable land, says Hashem Tzeva-os." And for their praise, as it is written, "Fortunate (ashrei) is the people who have it so; fortunate is the people whose G-d is Hashem." The "fortunate" status of Israel is only because they chose the Holy One, Blessed be He, to be their G-d, and the Holy One, Blessed be He, chose them to be His treasured people. Therefore, when the prince of Asher came to offer his sacrifice, he offered it on account of the choice that the Holy One, Blessed be He, made in Israel from among all the nations, as it is written, "And Hashem your G-d has chosen you to be His treasured people."

We must understand: at the beginning of the matter, it says that Israel’s "fortunate" status is that they chose the Holy One, Blessed be He (and the fact that it continues by saying that the Holy One, Blessed be He, chose them seems like an additional point), yet afterwards it says that Asher (on account of Israel’s "fortunate" status) offered his sacrifice on account of the choice that the Holy One, Blessed be He, made in Israel. Furthermore, we must understand that "choice" is only applicable between two things that are equal, or where each has a quality the other lacks. But regarding the Holy One, Blessed be He, and—to distinguish—the gods of the nations, how can it be said that Israel "chose" the Holy One, Blessed be He? Similarly, in the fact that the Holy One, Blessed be He, chose Israel, Israel is incomparably superior to all the nations; what does it mean that the Holy One, Blessed be He, *chose* Israel? Also, what is written in Tanya, that "You chose us from every people and tongue" refers to the physical body, which appears in its materiality like the bodies of the nations of the world, requires explanation. The fact that the body of a Jew *appears* (externally) like the bodies of the nations of the world is not (seemingly) an explanation regarding the Holy One, Blessed be He.

2) This can be understood by prefacing what is written, "Hashem is my portion, says my soul." The Midrash relates a parable of a king who entered a province, accompanied by dukes, governors, and generals. One said, "I will take the dukes to my side," another said, "I will take the governors to my side," and another said, "I will take the generals to my side." One clever person was there and said, "I will take the King, for all of them are replaceable, but the King is not replaceable." So too, the nations of the world—some worship the sun, some worship the moon, etc., but Israel worships only the Holy One, Blessed be He. This is the meaning of "Hashem is my portion, says my soul." The well-known precision here is: seemingly, even someone who is not clever, and even a small child, knows that the King is greater than the dukes, etc. Why is "cleverness" needed to choose the King? Furthermore, even if the dukes, etc., were not replaceable, it would be understood simply that the King is greater than them [especially according to the well-known interpretation of the verse, "From his shoulders and upward he was taller than all the people," meaning that even the "shoulder" of the King—the attributes that are drawn down from the intellect in the head—is higher than the "head" (the intellect) of all the people, which includes even the greatest ministers]. Why is the reason given that "all of them are replaceable"?

3) Seemingly, this could be explained based on the fact that in earlier generations, those who worshipped stars and constellations knew that the bounty bestowed upon the earth through them (from the choice fruits of the sun and the choice products of the moon) was not that they themselves were the source, but rather that the Holy One, Blessed be He, bestowed it through them. However, they erred and thought that the bounty bestowed by Him through the stars was a matter of the stars' own "choice," not knowing the truth that they have no choice at all, and are merely like an axe in the hand of the woodchopper. Therefore, they bowed to the stars and worshipped them, because according to their error (that the bounty was a matter of the stars' choice), it was appropriate to thank them for it, similar to the saying, "Wine for the master, thanks to the cupbearer." Also, because according to their error that the stars were possessors of choice, they thought that by worshipping them, they would bestow more upon them. From this, it later devolved into an even greater error: they thought that Hashem had abandoned the earth into the hands of the stars and constellations, and that the conduct of the earth depended solely on them. This is like a king who appointed ministers to govern the state; even though their power to govern is because the King appointed them, nevertheless, once appointed, the governance of the state is through the ministers, and the King does not intervene (except on rare occasions, for a great need). [It can be said that when the nations of the world call Him "G-d of gods," both types are included. Even those who think Hashem abandoned the earth to the stars call Him "G-d of gods," because in addition to Him being higher than them and ruling over them, even in the very fact that the stars are "gods" (in their opinion)—meaning they have the power to govern the earth and bestow bounty—it is not from themselves, but because the Holy One, Blessed be He, gave them the power for it, like the king's ministers. On the other hand, the error that the bounty bestowed by the Holy One, Blessed be He, through the stars is a matter of their "choice" is also included (subtly) in "G-d of gods," because the ability to act as they wish (to bestow or not) is a matter of dominion, "gods." The negation of the concept of "other gods" is achieved only through the knowledge that all intermediaries are merely like an axe in the hand of the woodchopper.] Based on this, one could explain the cleverness of the one who said, "I will take the King" (in the parable): the fact that Israel knows the truth—that all intermediaries through whom bounty comes are merely like an axe in the hand of the woodchopper—is due to the greatness of their *da'as*, for they see the inner reality. Externally, it appears that the bounty comes through the stars because they possess a quality that makes them worthy of being intermediaries, and the fact that Israel knows they are merely like an axe in the hand of the woodchopper is due to the greatness of their *da'as*.

4) However, from the fact that the Midrash brings a parable of a king and dukes, even though in the parable the dukes possess choice—which is why it makes sense that choosing the dukes ("I will take the dukes") could cause them to bestow more upon those who chose them [unlike the reality, where worship of the stars is of no benefit at all, since they do not possess choice]—it is understood that the clever person said "I will take the King" because the service of the King is more important to him than the benefit (the bounty) he would receive through the ministers. From this, it is understood in the reality as well: the primary reason why the nations of the world worship the sun and moon, etc., and Israel worships only the Holy One, Blessed be He, is (not because the nations err in thinking the stars have choice and Israel knows the truth that they are merely an axe in the hand of the woodchopper, but) because for the nations, the main thing is to receive the bounty, while for Israel, the main thing is to serve the King. The matter is that the primary reason they worshipped the stars is (as explained in the *drushim*) because in order to receive bounty from holiness, one must be in a state of *bitul*. Since they did not want to nullify themselves, they worshipped the stars, for in order to receive bounty from them, *bitul* is not required. As it is stated in the Zohar, the *ruach hatumah* is always present for free and for nothing, as it is written, "The cucumbers that we ate in Egypt for free," for the bounty from the *sitra achra* (Egypt) is "for free." Another reason they wanted to receive their bounty from the stars is that the bounty itself (drawn through the stars) is greater. Since the *yenika* of the *sitra achra* is from the *makif* that is above the *seder hishtalshelus*, where the bounty is not according to measure, the bounty is in great abundance. From this, we understand the greatness of the praise and the cleverness of Israel, who worship only the Holy One, Blessed be He; even though to receive from holiness requires *avodah* and exertion, and even then the bounty is (initially) measured and limited according to the measure of the *avodah*, nevertheless, they forgo the abundance of bounty (in physicality) [even though Israel can also receive (temporarily) bounty from the *sitra achra*, and not only that, they take the lion's share, yet they forgo it] and the limited bounty drawn from holiness is dearer to them.

5) The reason of the clever person (for choosing the King and not the ministers) is that "all of them are replaceable, but the King is not replaceable." Seemingly, one could say the intention here is that the bounty coming from the *sitra achra* (despite being in greater abundance than the bounty coming from holiness) is something replaceable that has no permanence. For after the *birur*, when the *nitzotzos* that fell into the *kelipos* (which are their life-force) are clarified, they will be completely nullified. [And all the more so regarding Israel, that even now, the fact that Israel can receive life-force from the *sitra achra* is only temporarily.] Likewise, on the other hand, the fact that the bounty coming from holiness is measured and limited is only now, but after the root of the *pnimiyus* is revealed, whose root is from the *pnimiyus* of the *makif*, the bounty from holiness will be in much greater abundance than the abundance bestowed from the *chitzoniyus* of the *makif* (which also pertains to bestowing upon the *kelipos*), as the Sages said, "If for those who transgress His will it is so, for those who do His will, how much more so." However, this explanation is insufficient, for according to this, the clever person’s choice of the King is also for the bounty he will receive through it, and the difference between him and those who chose the ministers (dukes, etc.) is that they think only of the present situation, while the clever person thinks of what will be in the future, similar to the Sages' saying, "Who is wise? He who sees what is born." But from the fact that the Midrash says that Israel worships only the Holy One, Blessed be He, because "Hashem is my portion, says my soul," it is proven that the clever person chooses the King not for the benefit he will have from it, but because of the King. This means that Israel chooses the Holy One, Blessed be He, even though now the abundance of bounty is for those who transgress His will, (not because later the bounty for those who do His will will be greater, but) because the bounty for those who do His will is from the *pnimiyus* of His will and His true desire, while the bounty for those who transgress His will is like one who throws something behind his back to his enemy, against his will. Therefore, they desire specifically the bounty for those who do His will, despite it being limited bounty, because it is drawn from the *pnimiyus* of His will. And what it says, "all of them are replaceable," means that the fact that they are later replaced proves that even now they are not a true reality. The fact that they possess life-force to bestow life-force to animals and the nations of the world is not from themselves, Heaven forbid, but because they draw from holiness. Since the life-force of holiness within them does not unite with them (and on the contrary, it is in a state of *galus* within them), they are, by their essence, a state of death. [And similarly for those who receive the life-force from them, as explained in Tanya, that the wicked in their lifetime are called dead is because their life is drawn from the place of death and impurity.] This is "I will take the King, for all of them are replaceable"—he does not want the bounty drawn from the place of death and impurity, even though it is bounty in abundance, and he desires specifically the bounty that is from holiness, even though it is limited bounty.

6) We must understand: seemingly, even according to this explanation—that the clever person’s choice of the King is not for the benefit he will have from it but because of the King’s superiority, which is why he forgoes the abundance of bounty coming through them—his choice of the King is based on intellect. Since "all of them are replaceable, but the King is not replaceable," it is appropriate to forgo the abundance of bounty coming through the ministers and choose the limited bounty coming from the King. From the fact that the Midrash connects this matter ("I will take the King") with "Hashem is my portion, says my soul," it implies that Israel’s choice of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is from the *neshamah* (which is a "portion of Hashem"), which is above intellect. The point of the explanation is that human intellect, as it is in itself, compels one to desire only what is good for oneself. This is why the nations of the world choose the ministers; even though they can understand that the King is the main thing (for "all of them are replaceable, but the King is not replaceable"), because they are in a state of *yesh*, they do not want to forgo the benefit they will have by receiving from the ministers. The fact that in Israel there exists the recognition that one must forgo one’s benefit and one’s reality and choose *Elokus* is from the *neshamah* ("portion of Hashem"), whose choice of *Elokus* is an essential choice that is above intellect (for this choice is not because of the superiority of *Elokus*—that the King is not replaceable—but because of the *Atzmus*). This acts upon their intellect, so that even the intellect recognizes that since *Elokus* is the Truth that lives and exists with eternal existence (the King is not replaceable), one must forgo one’s reality and choose *Elokus*.

7) Perhaps we can add that by the choice of the *neshamah*—which is above intellect—being drawn down and acting upon the intellect, it gains a higher superiority than it has in itself. This can be understood by prefacing what is explained in the Siddur, in the *maamar* "We were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt," regarding the bounty drawn to those who transgress His will, which is only incidentally, like one who throws something behind his back. It is like a king who makes a feast for the great ministers and important servants and spends much money on it, and from the leftovers of the feast, the lowly ones—like slaves and maidservants—receive, and even the dogs eat the bones thrown down. Surely the king did not intend to spend money for them, and the fact that they receive leftovers (even though the leftovers of the feast are also great wealth) is only incidentally. Similarly, in the parable, the bounty for those who transgress His will is only incidentally, like one who throws something behind his back, as explained there at length. It can be said that the fact that it brings two examples where the bounty to them is only incidentally (and they do not sit at the King’s table)—slaves and maidservants, and dogs—and two examples of those who sit at the King’s table—great ministers and important servants—is in accordance with the four levels in the parable. The lowest level is the dogs, who do not serve the King and whose desire is that physicality be bestowed upon them, more and more, as it is written, "The dogs are bold-spirited; they do not know satiety." This can be connected to the well-known idea that *kelev* (dog) is from the words *kulo lev* (all heart). The nature of man in his creation is that the brain rules over the heart, and the one who is in the state of a "dog" is at the opposite extreme. Not only does the heart rule over the brain, but furthermore, he is *all* heart. Therefore, his entire concern is to fulfill the desires of his heart. Above them are the lowly servants (the slaves and maidservants), who serve the King, but their service is only out of necessity (due to the fear of the rod), the opposite of their desire and *taanug*, for "a slave is comfortable with lawlessness." Since their desire and *taanug* are in the pleasures of this world, and the fact that they do not transgress His will is due to the fear of punishment, their place is outside the King’s table. Not so the important servants; their service to the King is with desire and longing. Even though their primary service is through *kabbalas ol* [as is known, the difference between a son and a servant: the son does his father’s will out of love, while the servant fulfills his master’s will through *kabbalas ol*], nevertheless, this itself is with desire and longing, *that they want* to accept upon themselves the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven. Therefore, their place is at the King’s table. Above them are the ministers, who have knowledge of the conduct of the kingdom [which is why many matters of governance are conducted according to them], and they also have comprehension of the King’s superiority, and because of this, they have love for Him, and their service is not only through *kabbalas ol* but also through love. And among the ministers themselves, there are many levels, which are divided generally into two: ministers (ordinary) and great ministers. It can be said that these three types who are at the King’s table [important servants, ministers, and great ministers] correspond to the three types in the Midrash—dukes, governors, and generals—who are together with the King, as the Midrash says, "and they were with Him," like the important servants and ministers who are at the King’s table. Based on this, it can be said that even though the intention (simply) in the dukes, etc., is to the *chitzoniyus* of the ministers of *nogah* (as brought above from the *drushim*), nevertheless, the fact that it specifies that the dukes "were *with Him*" is to hint that it speaks here [not only of the *chitzoniyus* of the ministers, who are a *yesh* and a distinct entity, like the lowly servants whose place is outside the King’s table, but] also of the angels who are nullified to *Elokus*, and higher—the *Sefiros* of *Atzilus*, where "He, His life-force, and His essence are one," who are always with the King.

Based on this, the greatness of the cleverness of the one who said "I will take the King" is understood even more: in addition to not wanting to receive the bounty like the dogs, and also not like the lowly servants outside the King’s table (even though the bounty bestowed upon them is in great abundance), and wanting to be at the King’s table, he does not even want the dukes, etc. (angels, *Sefiros* of *Atzilus*, and higher), but only the King: "I will take the King." This is like the well-known teaching of the Baal Shem Tov on the verse, "A prayer for the poor man when he is faint and pours out his speech before Hashem," that he does not ask for anything, and his prayer is that he pours out his speech before Hashem. For this, great cleverness is needed. Like a king of flesh and blood: in order to enter the chamber where the King is and to see the King’s face, one must first pass through several halls, in each of which there is precious wealth. There are those who, when they see the precious wealth in the outer hall, are delighted with it with a wondrous *taanug* and linger there. There are those who are delighted in a more inner hall. But specifically one who is a great wise man—the importance and value of the immense wealth found even in the most inner hall does not affect him, and his entire desire and longing is to enter the chamber where the King is and to see the King’s face.

8) It can be said that this matter is the love of "Whom have I in heaven, and with You I desire nothing on earth," and in the words of the Alter Rebbe, "I do not want anything; I do not want Your Garden of Eden, I do not want Your World to Come, etc.; I want nothing but You alone." Even though the revelations in the Garden of Eden, and all the more so the revelations of the World to Come, are extremely lofty revelations—and the Alter Rebbe had comprehension of this, a true comprehension in a way that the intellect united with the object of comprehension—nevertheless, he does not want the revelations of the Garden of Eden or the revelations of the World to Come, and his entire desire is only for the *Atzmus*: "You alone." It can be added that the main cleverness in this—that he does not want the dukes, etc. (the levels in the *seder hishtalshelus*, and even those above *hishtalshelus*)—is that even though the *Sefiros* (from the word *sefiros* and *behirut*) do not conceal the *Ohr Ein Sof*, for in the bounty drawn through the *Sefiros* (the vessels) it is seen clearly that the bounty is not from the *Sefiros* themselves, Heaven forbid, but that the *Ohr Ein Sof* bestows through them, and even more so in the lights of the *Sefiros*, through which the light above them is revealed, and all the more so in the levels above *Atzilus*, through which the light reveals all that can be revealed of the *Ma'or*—nevertheless, he does not want them, but only the King: "I will take the King." It can be said that this is also the precision in the Alter Rebbe’s words, "I do not want Your Garden of Eden, I do not want Your World to Come," for even though the Garden of Eden and the World to Come are "*Your* Garden of Eden," "*Your* World to Come"—that in addition to the revelations of the Garden of Eden and the World to Come themselves being extremely lofty revelations, through them is revealed all that can be revealed of the *Ma'or*—nevertheless, he does not want them, and his entire desire is only for the *Atzmus*: "You alone."

9) It can be said that this interpretation of "I will take the King"—that he does not want the levels (of holiness) above, but only the *Atzmus*—and the interpretation of "I will take the King" according to its simple meaning (that he does not worship the stars) are related. This can be understood according to the well-known idea that the love of "Whom have I in heaven"—that he does not want the revelations of the Garden of Eden but the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*—is such that even though in the Garden of Eden he would remain in his reality and have comprehension and *taanug* in *Elokus* (enjoying the radiance of the *Shechinah*), whereas from the revelation of the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* he would be nullified from his level, nevertheless, since the revelation of the Garden of Eden is only a radiance (*ziv* of the *Shechinah*), which is incomparable to the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, he chooses the essential and not the secondary. From this, it is understood that when he chooses the revelations of the Garden of Eden (which are as naught compared to the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*) for his own sake, in order to remain in his reality and enjoy the radiance of the *Shechinah*, it is possible that from this (through the multiplicity of *hishtalshelus*, etc.) he will come to a state where, even though he knows that the bounty through the ministers is drawn from the place of death (as above, end of section 5), nevertheless, he will desire this bounty more than the bounty from holiness, in order to have bounty in abundance. Similarly, this applies to the error of those who worship the stars and consider them a reality (that the bounty comes from the stars themselves, or that they have choice at least to bestow). From the fact that he wants the revelations of the Garden of Eden and not the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*—meaning he gives importance to a matter outside the *Atzmus*—it is possible that from this (through the multiplicity of *hishtalshelus*, etc.) he will come to give importance to the stars, that they have choice in their bestowing, and furthermore that the bounty is from them themselves, Heaven forbid. It can be added that even when his desire for the Garden of Eden is because it is the Garden of Eden of the Holy One, Blessed be He ("*Your* Garden of Eden"), even then it is possible that from this will come the error that they (the stars) have choice to bestow, and even the error that the bounty is from them themselves, since even according to this error, the power they have to bestow is (not from themselves but) because the Holy One, Blessed be He, appointed them for it, as above in section 3 (*like* "*Your* Garden of Eden"). And in order that there be no place for "I will take the dukes, etc.," it is by arousing within himself (a semblance, at least, which is within the power of every Jew, as follows) not to desire even "*Your* Garden of Eden," but "nothing but You alone."

10) It can be added as an explanation of the connection of "I will take the King" to the love of "Whom have I in heaven"—that he wants the *Ohr Ein Sof*—by prefacing that the main point of the service of star worship is that they consider something that is only a means to be the essential. Both regarding the stars—that the truth is they are only a means (an axe) through which bounty is drawn, and those who worship the stars think they are the essential—and regarding the physical bounties bestowed through them, that the bestowing of physicality is in order that they use it for the service of Hashem, and those who worship the stars consider the physicality to be the essential (which is why they want the bounty from the *sitra achra*, in order to have an abundance of physical bounty, as above in section 4). Now, the revelations, even the loftiest revelations, are not the goal in themselves; they are only a means through which the inner intention of "a dwelling place in the lower worlds" will be completed. Therefore, when he desires the revelations (and not the *Ohr Ein Sof*), it means he considers them the essential, and it is possible that there will be a place to consider the means (the stars and the physical bounty) as the essential. Based on this, it can be said that the fact that the nations of the world—some worship the sun and some the moon—and Israel worships only the Holy One, Blessed be He, is because the root of the nations of the world is from the *chitzoniyus* of the will (that desires some thing for the sake of another matter), and the level of the *chitzoniyus* of the will (as it is a source for the nations of the world) is such that it is not felt in it that the will is for the sake of another matter, but that this itself is the goal. Therefore, it follows from this that the nations of the world consider the intermediaries of the stars to be the essential. The root of Israel is from the *pnimiyus* of the will, and from the *pnimiyus* of the will, there is no place for any other matter. Therefore, Israel has the recognition that the stars are only a means, like an axe in the hand of the woodchopper (and likewise, the physical bounty is only for the sake of the service of Hashem), and therefore they worship only the Holy One, Blessed be He: "I will take the King."

11) Based on this, one can also explain the superiority in the fact that Israel’s choice of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is not only from the will of the *neshamah* that is above intellect, but also from the intellect (as above in section 7). For the fact that the root of Israel is in the *pnimiyus* of the *makif* (*pnimiyus* of the will) is because Israel is a level of *pnimi*, and as is known, the root of the *pnimi* is in the *pnimiyus* of the *makif*. Therefore, the main superiority of Israel’s choice of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is when the will of the *neshamah* that is above intellect (*makif*) is drawn down and acts also upon the intellect (*pnimi*), for specifically through this is drawn from the essence of the *neshamah* (*pnimiyus* of the *makif*), which is above even the will that is above intellect (*chitzoniyus* of the *makif*). It can be said that this is also what is written, "Hashem is my portion, says my soul" ("my portion" specifically), for a portion is a level of *pnimi* (as is known regarding the matter of "How good is our portion, how pleasant our lot, and how beautiful our inheritance"), and the superiority of Israel’s choice of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is when the choice of the *neshamah* is drawn into the *pnimiyus*, into understanding and comprehension, and in a way that it acts in thought, speech, and action, through which is the drawing down of the *pnimiyus* of the *makif*.

12) This can be connected with what is written in Midrash Tehillim on the verse, "For the conductor, a psalm of David, to bring to remembrance." A parable of a king who had sheep, and he became angry at them and drove the sheep away, dismantled the fold, and removed the shepherd. After a time, he gathered the sheep and built the fold, but he did not mention the shepherd. The shepherd said, "The sheep are gathered, the fold is built, and I am not mentioned." So David said, before the psalm, "For G-d will save Zion and build the cities of Judah"—the fold is built—"and they shall dwell there and inherit it, and the seed of His servants shall inherit it, and those who love His name shall dwell in it"—the sheep are gathered—"and I am not mentioned." Therefore, "For the conductor, a psalm of David, to bring to remembrance." We must understand: the fact that he gathered the sheep after he was angry at them (and they were not pleasing to him) is because the will toward them was aroused again. Why did he not mention (of his own accord) the shepherd, and it requires a special request for this? The matter is that will is a *makif*, and it is possible that the drawing down of the will will be in the *chitzoniyus*. This is what David requested—that the shepherd be mentioned—for a shepherd is a matter of *pnimiyus* bounty (as is known the difference between shepherds and princes). As long as there is no *pnimiyus* bounty (the shepherd), the essential is missing. For the drawing down of the *makif* itself is from the *chitzoniyus* of the *makif*, and the root of the *pnimi* is from the *pnimiyus* of the *makif*. And even though when the sheep are gathered, they are given all their needs, and how can it be said that they have no shepherd? It can be said that the *pnimiyus* bounty that is through the shepherd is the study of Torah, and since the Torah of now is vanity compared to the Torah of Moshiach, it turns out that the true matter of the shepherd (*pnimiyus*) is still missing. And even though the discussion here is after "For G-d will save Zion and build the cities of Judah," etc., which will be in the future redemption, the main revelation that will be in the future redemption is the revelation of the *Atzmus* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above comprehension, and this was David’s (David the King Moshiach) request, and through his request this is accomplished—that there will also be the matter of the shepherd, the understanding and comprehension of the Torah in the ultimate perfection.

13) Based on all the above, one can explain the connection and relationship between Israel’s choice of the Holy One, Blessed be He, and the Holy One, Blessed be He’s choice of Israel. The Holy One, Blessed be He’s choice of Israel to be His treasured people—that the *pnimiyus* will of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is in Israel (and not that they are a means for another matter)—is the root and the empowerment for Israel’s choice of the Holy One, Blessed be He, as explained at length that the fact that Israel chooses the Holy One, Blessed be He, is because their root is in the *pnimiyus* of the will. [It can be said that the fact that the choice of Israel is in the body, from this is drawn to them the power that even in physical matters there will be the revelation of the *pnimiyus* of the will, through which it is felt in them that even physicality is for the sake of the service of Hashem.] Based on this, one can explain that the sacrifices of Asher were on account of the choice that the Holy One, Blessed be He, made in Israel, even though Israel’s "fortunate" status is only because they chose the Holy One, Blessed be He, because the main superiority of Israel is (not that which they have from above, but) that they accomplish through their *avodah*. Specifically in this (what they accomplish through their *avodah*) is the main satisfaction and *taanug* of the Holy One, Blessed be He. However, in order that they have the empowerment for this, it is through the drawing down and revelation of the Holy One, Blessed be He’s choice of Israel, and this revelation is drawn down through the sacrifices that Asher offered. For in addition to the power that is in every Jew from his root in the *pnimiyus* of the will, through the sacrifices of Asher, this power is drawn down in greater revelation.
