It can be said that through the Alter Rebbe’s saying, "I want nothing but You alone," and especially after this was publicized by the Tzemach Tzedek, the power was given (even more) to every Jew, and especially to those who walk in his footsteps and his ways, that even though he has a multitude of desires, and even desires that are from the *nefesh habahamis*, all his desires will be without a double heart, and his main desire will be to complete the intention of the *Atzmus* to make for Him, blessed be He, a dwelling place in the lower worlds (*similar* to and a semblance of what was with the Alter Rebbe, who did not want anything but You alone), and also his desire for physicality will be (primarily) in order to make the physicality a vessel for *Elokus*. Through this, he will also have drawn down to him an abundance of bounty in physicality, as the Alter Rebbe said, "When the Almighty gives Jews physicality, Jews make from the physicality spirituality." From this, it is understood that when the Holy One, Blessed be He, gives Israel an abundance of physicality, Israel makes an abundance of spirituality—abundance in quantity and abundance in quality—up to the ultimate perfection, until they connect and unite with the Holy One, Blessed be He; Israel (Torah) and the Holy One, Blessed be He, are all one. And all this is done with joy and a good heart, in the days of preparation for the time of our freedom, and this also brings about our time of freedom in actuality, the true and complete redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, very soon.

## 11 Nissan 5738

"When your son asks you tomorrow, saying: 'What are the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances that Hashem our G-d has commanded you?'" — this question is the question of the *chacham* (wise son), as it is stated in the Haggadah: "The *chacham*, what does he say? 'What are the testimonies, etc.'" It is a well-known point of precision in the *maamarim* of our Rebbes that since he is called by the title *chacham* (and true wisdom is the wisdom of Torah), he surely knows the *mitzvos*. What, then, is his question, "What are the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances, etc."?

It should be added that from the continuation of the verse, "And you shall say to your son... 'And Hashem commanded us to perform all these statutes... for our good...'" — which explains the virtue of performing the *mitzvos* ("for our good") — it is understood that the meaning of "What are the testimonies, etc." is: "What is the *concept* of the *mitzvos* (what virtue do they possess)?" This requires explanation: how is it possible for a *chacham* to ask such a question? Furthermore, it is difficult to understand why, in the explanation of the *mitzvos* (in the response, "And you shall say to your son"), two matters are mentioned: "And Hashem took us out of Egypt... and commanded us to perform all the statutes," for since He took us out of Egypt, we are enslaved to Him to fulfill His *mitzvos*. And a second matter regarding the fulfillment of *mitzvos*: that they are "for our good all the days." From the fact that the response explains both matters, it is understood that both are a novelty to the son. But it is incomprehensible: since he does not know the concept of the *mitzvos* (not only that he does not know that the *mitzvos* are "for our good," but he does not even understand the obligation of fulfilling the *mitzvos* by way of *kabbalas ol*), how can he be called by the title *chacham*?

Also, one must understand (as is noted in the *maamarim*) why he says, "that Hashem our G-d has commanded *you* (etchem)" — *you* and not *us*. Even though by saying "Hashem our G-d" there is no room for the error that he is, G-d forbid, excluding himself from the collective, it still requires explanation why he says "you," for he should have said "that Hashem our G-d has commanded *us*," or simply "that Hashem our G-d has commanded."

The core of the explanation in the *maamarim* is based on the well-known principle that the fulfillment of *mitzvos* by the Patriarchs was (primarily) a spiritual *avodah*, and the action they performed in the physical realm was like a vessel for that spiritual *avodah*. However, the *mitzvos* given at *Mattan Torah* are primarily about the actual performance. The performance of the *mitzvah* is not a preparation or a vessel for its intention; rather, on the contrary, the performance is the essence. As in the case at hand, the *mitzvah* of eating *matzah*: if one intends the secrets of the *mitzvah* of *matzah* but, G-d forbid, lacks the actual physical act, he will not draw down anything. Conversely, when he performs the *mitzvah* in action, even without *kavanah*, he draws down the *Ohr*.

The question of the *chacham* is: how does one draw down the revelation of *Ohr Ein Sof* through the *action* of the *mitzvos*? This is what he means by "What are the testimonies, etc., that Hashem our G-d has commanded *you*?" — *you* specifically, because since your *avodah* (after *Mattan Torah*) is action (and not spiritual *avodah* like the *avodah* of the Patriarchs), how is the revelation of *Ohr Ein Sof* drawn down through this *avodah*? The answer ("And you shall say to your son: 'We were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt, and Hashem took us out of Egypt... and Hashem commanded us to perform all these statutes...'") is that the exile of Egypt and the exodus from Egypt were a preparation for *Mattan Torah*. At *Mattan Torah*, the power was given that through physical *mitzvos* ("to perform all these statutes," *la'asos* specifically), one can draw down influences higher than those drawn down by the Patriarchs through their spiritual *avodah*, even to the point of drawing down the *Atzmus*.

2) It must be understood that, on the face of it, the above explanation—that the *chacham*'s question is specifically about the *mitzvos* after *Mattan Torah*—only explains why, after saying "Hashem our G-d has commanded," he adds "you" to emphasize that his question is not about the *mitzvos* of the Patriarchs, but about the *mitzvos* that Hashem our G-d commanded *you* after *Mattan Torah*. But this would seemingly have been understood even if he had used a more inclusive term, "that Hashem our G-d has commanded *us*."

One must also understand why he says "the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances." Since his question is about the *mitzvos* in general, he should have said "What are the *mitzvos*?" Why does he specify "testimonies, statutes, and ordinances"? Especially since the distinction between these categories is in the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* (the *mitzvos* of *chukim* must be performed because they are the command of the *Ribono shel Olam*—"a decree I have decreed"—while the *mitzvos* of *eidus* and *mishpatim* should also be performed for their reason). The question "What are the testimonies, etc." is about the influence drawn down through the *action* of the *mitzvos*.

Furthermore, one must understand that the son's question is about all three types of *mitzvos*, yet in the response ("And Hashem commanded us to perform all these statutes"), only *chukim* are mentioned. Also, the distinction between the three types is: *mishpatim* are *mitzvos* necessitated by intellect (like theft, robbery, honoring parents); *eidus* are *mitzvos* that are a sign and remembrance (like *Shabbos*, *Pesach*, *Sukkos*, and *Tefillin*), which, even if the intellect by itself (without the Torah's command) would not have necessitated them (unlike *mishpatim*, where even if the Torah had not been given, we would have learned modesty from a cat and theft from an ant), once the Torah commanded them, the intellect agrees; and *chukim* are *mitzvos* that even after the Torah commanded them, have no place in the intellect, and are only by way of "a statute I have enacted, a decree I have decreed." According to this, it would seem one should have said "ordinances, testimonies, and statutes" (in the order of below-to-above) or "statutes, testimonies, and ordinances" (in the order of above-to-below), yet the order in the verse is "testimonies, statutes, and ordinances."

3) This can be understood by prefacing what the Rebbe Rayatz said in his *maamar* "Zeh" (which he said on the first *Pesach* after arriving in the United States to reside): that in the question of the *chacham*, it is written "you" (*etchem*), and by saying "you" (and not "us"), he is seemingly similar to the *rasha* (wicked son). The *maamar* adds that even though the *chacham* says "Hashem our G-d," thereby including himself in the collective of Israel and accepting upon himself the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, this is regarding the essence, but regarding the details of the *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim*, he says "you." The language of the *maamar* implies that the precision in saying "you" is not only that it requires explanation why he says "you," but also that by saying "you" and not "us," there is room for the mistake that his saying "our G-d" and including himself in the collective is only regarding the essence (accepting the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven), but regarding the details of *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim*, he excludes himself from the collective.

One can say the explanation for this is that there are two aspects to *mitzvos*: that the *mitzvos* are the command of Hashem (which is equal in all *mitzvos*), and that the *mitzvos* are divided into three types: *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim*. These two aspects also apply to the fulfillment of *mitzvos*. In the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos*, there are two aspects: a general *kavanah*—to fulfill the command of Hashem (which is equal in all *mitzvos*); and a specific *kavanah*—fulfilling the *mitzvah* because it is an *eidus* (a sign and remembrance) of a certain matter, or because it is a *chukah* and decree, or because it is also necessitated by intellect. The *chacham*'s question, "What are the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances?" is: since all *mitzvos* are the command of Hashem, what is the relevance of the categories of *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim*? Therefore, the *maamar* emphasizes that even after he says "our G-d," there is room for the mistake that regarding the details of *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim*, he excludes himself, G-d forbid, from the collective. To negate this mistake, he should have said "us."

4) This can be understood based on what is explained in the aforementioned *maamar*: that the three aspects of *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim* are in all *mitzvos*. Even the *mitzvos* of *chukim* and *mishpatim* are called by the name *eidus* (as it is written, "He established a testimony in Yaakov, etc."). Similarly, regarding *chukim*: even in *mitzvos* that have a reason (*eidus* and even *mishpatim*), the reason is only for the general *mitzvah* and not for the details, and the details are in the manner of "a statute I have enacted, a decree I have decreed." Similarly, regarding *mishpatim*: even in the *mitzvos* of *eidus* and *chukim*, there is the aspect of *mishpatim*.

The matter is that in the revelation of *Elokus* through the *mitzvos*, there are three general levels: the *Ohr* that is clothed in the worlds (generally, *Ohr HaMemalei*); the *Ohr* that is above being clothed in the worlds but relates to the worlds (generally, *Ohr HaSovev*); and the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above relation to the worlds. *Ohr HaMemalei* is grasped even by the intellect, and therefore its drawing down is through the aspect of *mishpatim*—intellectual comprehension. *Ohr HaSovev* does not allow for the comprehension of obligation, only the knowledge of negation; that is, the fact that he knows and understands that in this *Ohr*, it is *impossible* to have intellectual comprehension, is the aspect of the nullification of comprehension. Therefore, the drawing down of *Ohr HaSovev* is through the fulfillment of *chukim*, whose essence is that he nullifies his intellect—"you have no permission to ponder."

Regarding the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, which is not in the category of relation to the worlds, it is not even appropriate to say that it is negated from intellectual comprehension. As explained in *Tanya*, one who says about Hashem that He cannot be grasped is like one who says about some high and deep wisdom that it cannot be felt with the hands—anyone who hears him will laugh at him. Because the aspect of negation—saying that something is negated from something else—is only applicable to things that have a relationship between them; intellect and touch are two completely separate things that have no relationship between them. [Perhaps one could add that in the analogy of intellect and touch, since both are limited created beings, there is a relationship between them. This is not so in the *nimshal* (the concept being explained). Therefore, the fact that it is inappropriate to say about Hashem that He cannot be grasped is even more so than saying about an intellectual concept that it cannot be felt with the hands.] Therefore, the drawing down of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* is not even through the aspect of *chukim* (the nullification of intellect), because regarding the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, it is not appropriate to say even the aspect of the negation of intellect. The fact that through the *mitzvos* there is a drawing down of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* is because the *mitzvos* are the will and command of the *Atzmus*.

This is the concept of *eidus* in the *mitzvos*: the fact that all *mitzvos* (even the *mitzvos* of *chukim* and *mishpatim*) are called by the name *eidus* is because they draw down and reveal the essential concealment of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, which is above even *Sovev*. Just as the concept of *eidus* (testimony) in its simple sense is only about something hidden—for about something revealed, testimony is not applicable because it is revealed, and even about something that is destined to be revealed, testimony is not needed—so too in spirituality: *Ohr HaMemalei* is something revealed that is grasped by the intellect, and *Ohr HaSovev* (which is above clothing in the worlds) is something destined to be revealed (a concealment that relates to revelation), because through the comprehension of *Ohr HaMemalei* that is clothed in the worlds—which is only an illumination (as is proven by the very fact that it is in a state of clothing)—one comes to the knowledge that there is an *Ohr* that is wondrous above the worlds, which is the source from which the illumination in the clothing is drawn. The concept of *eidus* is in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*, which is above even *Sovev*, which is completely hidden. This is why all *mitzvos* are called *eidus*, because they draw down and reveal the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above even *Sovev*.

5) The explanation of the virtue of *eidus* over *chukim* in more detail, even though in *chukim* it is also felt in revelation that they are His will, a will that is above reason, can be understood based on what is written in the *maamar* that *chukim* is from the word *chakikah* (engraving). From the continuation of the matters in the *maamar*, it is implied that there are two aspects to this. The aspect of engraving above: as it is written in the *maamar* that the virtue of the letters of engraving over the letters of writing is that the letters of engraving are not something additional to the essence, and it explains that this is the level of *Keter*. One can say that the fact that *Keter* (*Sovev*) is like the letters of engraving, which are not something additional to the essence, is because the revelation of *Ohr HaMemalei*—being a revelation in the category of limitation and not like the essence—is like something additional to the essence, whereas the revelation of *Ohr HaSovev*—being *bli gevul* (limitless) like the essence—is not something additional to the essence.

Another aspect of *chukim* from the word *chakikah* is that through *chukim*, the aspect of *chakikah* is created in the world. As it is stated in the *Midrash* on the verse, "If not for My covenant by day and night, I would not have set the statutes of heaven and earth"—the *chukim* through which I engraved the heavens and the earth. The *maamar* explains that the concept of "I engraved the heavens and the earth" is the nullification that is created in the world through the drawing down of *Sovev*, the *chakikah* from above.

Now, even in *chakikah*, through engraving, a change is made in the thing being engraved, so that it is not as it was initially. Like in the engraving of letters in stone: initially the stone was simple, and now it is depicted with letters [especially when the engraving is in a clear and shining stone, through the engraving a change is made in the clarity and shine of the stone, that in the place of the engraving it does not shine as much]. Another aspect of this [in addition to the change (descent) that is made *in the stone* through the engraving] is that the engraving is connected to the void, that it is *lacking* in the stone, meaning that the engraving itself is like the opposite of the stone. One can say that these two aspects are also in the *nimshal* of *Ohr HaSovev*. The descent in the *Ohr* to be *Sovev l'almin* is like the change and descent that is made in the stone, that it is not simple (and does not shine as much) as it was initially. Through this, a place is given for the world (which is brought into being from *Ohr HaSovev*), whose existence (*metzius*—from the word *he'elem*, concealment) is the opposite of the revelation of the *Ohr*, like the engraving where something is *lacking* from the stone.

One can say that it is the same with *chukim*: that they are called by the name *chukim* from the word *chakikah* is due to the two aforementioned aspects. The matter is that the meaning of *chukah* is as the Sages said, "A statute I have enacted, a decree I have decreed, and you have no permission to ponder," which has two aspects: that the will of *chukim* is (not as it is in its essence, but) in a state of descent, so to speak, to negate the intellect ("you have no permission to ponder"), like the change and descent made in the stone through engraving; and that the intellect (which the will negates) is in a manner where its understanding and comprehension is the opposite of the will, which is why one must negate it and say "you have no permission to ponder." According to this, the virtue of *eidus* over *chukim* is better understood: the concept of *chukim* is the will of the *mitzvos* as it is in a state of descent to negate things (the intellect) that are opposites, while the concept of *eidus* is the will of the *mitzvos* as it is in its essence.

6) Now, just as above the distinction between *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim* is that *chukim* and *mishpatim* are in the *Ohr* that relates to the worlds [except that *mishpatim* are in the *Ohr* that is clothed in the worlds (*Memalei*) and *chukim* are in the *Ohr* that is above clothing (*Sovev*)] and *eidus* are in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above relation to the worlds, so too it is in the *avodah* of man: *chukim* and *mishpatim* are in the intellect that is in man (or at least in the level of the *neshamah* that relates to the intellect)—*hisbonenus*. [Except that in *mishpatim*, the *hisbonenus* is in the reasons for the *mitzvos*, including the *hisbonenus* that even the *mitzvos* of *chukim* have a reason, but the reason for these *mitzvos* is in His wisdom and is not drawn down into the intellect of the created beings. And the *hisbonenus* of *chukim* is that all *mitzvos* (even the *mitzvos* of *mishpatim*) are His will, a will that is above reason, above even the reasons in His wisdom.] And the concept of *eidus* is in the person himself. As it is written, "You are My witnesses," which means "you yourselves." Since every one of Israel, his *neshamah* is truly a part of G-d from above, and the root of the *neshamos* is in the *Atzmus* (and higher than the root of Torah and *mitzvos*), therefore, the very existence of Israel testifies to the *Atzmus*. However, the revelation of the root of the *neshamah* is through the *eidus* of the *mitzvos*.

7) One can say that the essence of the *mitzvos* is that they are *eidus*, the drawing down of the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*. The concept of the *chukim* and *mishpatim* of the *mitzvos* (the drawing down of *Ohr HaSovev* and *Ohr HaMemalei*) is because the intention in drawing down the *Atzmus* through the *mitzvos* is that the drawing down should be in revelation. Since the *Atzmus* is above revelation, therefore the revelation of the drawing down of the *Atzmus* (*eidus*) is through *Ohr HaSovev* and *Ohr HaMemalei* (*chukim* and *mishpatim*), which are revelations. This is why the order of these three matters is *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim*: first is the concept of *eidus*—the drawing down of the *Atzmus*; and afterwards, in order for the drawing down to be in revelation, it is through the revelation of *Ohr HaSovev* that is drawn down through the concept of *chukim* in the *mitzvos*; and afterwards, in order for the revelation to be in an internal manner, it is through the revelation of *Ohr HaMemalei* that is drawn down through the concept of *mishpatim* in the *mitzvos*.

According to this, one can say that what is implied at the beginning of the *maamar*—that the *chacham*'s question, "What are the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances, etc.," is because since all *mitzvos* are the command of Hashem, why are they divided into *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim* (as mentioned in section 3)—is also for the explanation (in the continuation of the *maamar*) that the *chacham*'s question is how the revelation of *Elokus* is drawn down through the *mitzvos* after *Mattan Torah*, whose essence is actual performance. The matter is that the fact that through the performance of *mitzvos* there is a drawing down of *Elokus* is not a wonder, since the *mitzvos* are the will of Hashem. The *chacham*'s question is about the fact that through the performance of *mitzvos*, a *revelation* of *Elokus* is drawn down. The concept of revelation seemingly belongs to spiritual *avodah* and not to physical performance. Therefore he says, "What are the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances, etc.?" and not "What are the *mitzvos*?" because the fact that through the *mitzvos* there is a drawing down of *Elokus* is due to the common point in all *mitzvos* equally—that they are the command and will of Hashem—and the fact that the *mitzvos* are divided into the categories of *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim* is regarding the revelation of the drawing down [that in *eidus*, the drawing down is in concealment; in *chukim*, the drawing down is in revelation in a *makif* (encompassing) manner; and the revelation of *mishpatim* is a revelation in an internal manner]. The *chacham*'s question, "What are the testimonies, the statutes, and the ordinances that Hashem our G-d has commanded *you*?" is: the *mitzvos* that Hashem our G-d commanded *you* after *Mattan Torah*, whose essence is actual performance, their concept is the *drawing down* of *Elokus*, and if so, what are the *eidus*, *chukim*, and *mishpatim*, for the distinction between them is in the matter of revelation.

8) The answer, "We were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt, and Hashem took us out of Egypt, etc., and Hashem commanded us to perform all these statutes, etc., for our good," is that at *Mattan Torah*, which was after the introduction of the exile of Egypt and the exodus from Egypt, the power was given that through physical *mitzvos*, one can draw down the most exalted influences (as in section 1), and also that the influences through the performance of *mitzvos* will be in revelation. This is "And Hashem commanded us to perform all these statutes... to fear, etc." *Yirah* (fear) is the letters of *re'iyah* (seeing, as written in the *maamar*), that the influences through the performance of the *mitzvos* ("And Hashem commanded us to perform") will be in revelation, even to the point of *re'iyah*. The essence of the revelation of the influences through the performance of the *mitzvos* will be in the time to come, when there will be *re'iyah* at the most exalted levels, even to the point of "fearing Hashem our G-d"—seeing the essence of *Ein Sof Baruch Hu*. This is "And Hashem commanded us to perform all these statutes," *chukim* specifically, because when one fulfills the *mitzvos* (only) because of the reason, the influences through the *mitzvos* are in concealment, and in order for the influences of the *mitzvos* to be in revelation, it is through the fulfillment of the *mitzvos* (even the *mitzvos* of *eidus* and *mishpatim*) being because they are the command of Hashem, like *chukim*.

9) Now, after all the above, it requires explanation: since the Torah of truth calls him by the name *chacham*, it is reasonable that he knows the novelty of *Mattan Torah*, that at *Mattan Torah* the power was given to draw down through physical *mitzvos* the most exalted influences and that the influences should be in revelation. What, then, is the question, "What are the testimonies, etc.?" This can be explained based on what is written elsewhere: that the fact that the deeds of the Patriarchs are mentioned in the Torah (even though the *avodah* of the Patriarchs was seemingly only a preparation for *Mattan Torah*) is because even now, after *Mattan Torah*, there must be an *avodah* that is like the *avodah* of the Patriarchs. The matter is that the fact that we are commanded every single day that they should be in your eyes "as new" (and more than that, "new"), is not only regarding the preciousness and loveliness of Torah and *mitzvos*, but also regarding the study of Torah and the fulfillment of the *mitzvos* themselves. That every day one must be at an even more exalted level, as the command "we elevate in holiness," even to an incomparable elevation, until the Torah and *mitzvos* that he had before are (as if they are not considered or) not considered compared to the Torah and *mitzvos* of this day, which is why the Torah and *mitzvos* of this day are (as if they are new or) new. This is why even now, after *Mattan Torah*, there must be an *avodah* that is like the *avodah* of the Patriarchs before *Mattan Torah*, because *Mattan Torah* is every day—"the Giver of the Torah" is in the present tense—and every day it is at a more exalted level. Therefore, there must be an *avodah* every day that is like the *avodah* of the Patriarchs, which is a preparation and introduction to the *Mattan Torah* of that day. According to this, one can explain the *chacham*'s question, "What are the testimonies, etc., that Hashem commanded, etc., *you*?" because the Torah and *mitzvos* of the *chacham* are truly new every day, and therefore he is always in his beginning, in the state of before *Mattan Torah*. One can say that the fact that he says "that Hashem commanded, etc., *you*"—*you* and not *us*—is to hint that for him, it is the state of before *Mattan Torah*.

10) One can connect this to the fact that the *chacham*'s question is primarily on the night of *Pesach*, even though the question "What are the testimonies, etc." (in its simple sense) is regarding the *mitzvos* in general, because on *Pesach* is the birth of the people of Israel. This is why the festival of *Pesach* is especially related to the matter of education, because the education of children begins immediately when they are born. This is the connection of the *chacham*'s question with the night of *Pesach*, because the fact that he asks "What are the testimonies, etc." is because (due to the fact that he is always rising from elevation to elevation) he is in a state of before *Mattan Torah*, and he does not yet belong to any matter, like a baby the moment he is born.

11) The answer, "We were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt, and Hashem took us out of Egypt with a strong hand," is that even one who is at the level of a baby the moment he is born (including one who is at this level in a simple sense, and not like the *chacham* whose being at this level is due to his elevations), and more than that, even one who is in the state of a slave to Pharaoh in Egypt (before the birth of the exodus from Egypt), Hashem takes him out of Egypt with a strong hand. For "with a strong hand" is the intensity of the revelation, until the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, was revealed to them in His glory and His essence and redeemed them, which is a leap from one extreme to the other—from the lowest (slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt) to the highest—the revelation of "in His glory and His essence."

And so it will be for us, "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show him wonders," a redemption by way of a leap from one extreme to the other, that from the double and redoubled darkness of exile, and especially in the generation of the footsteps of *Moshiach* where the darkness is even greater, we come immediately to the revelation of "And the glory of Hashem shall be revealed, and all flesh shall see, etc.," and "Hashem shall be King," with the coming of *Moshiach Tzidkeinu*, speedily in our days, truly.

## 11 Nissan 5742

"As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders." There is a well-known precision noted in the Zohar regarding this verse—cited in the discourses of our Rebbes—concerning the word *kimiy* ("as in the days of"), which is in the plural form. Yet, the exodus from Egypt occurred on a single day. Similarly, regarding the commandment to remember the exodus, it is written: "Remember this day on which you went out from Egypt," and "so that you may remember the day of your exodus from the land of Egypt." Why, then, is it stated in the plural, "as in the days"?

Furthermore, we must understand the content of the verse, "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders." The simple interpretation is that the future redemption, which will come speedily in our days through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, will be in a manner of "I will show you wonders," just as the redemption from Egypt was. This is difficult to understand, as it is explained in many verses and statements of our Sages that the future redemption will be a complete redemption, after which there will be no more exile—unlike the redemption from Egypt. Thus, the future redemption will be far more exalted than the redemption from Egypt. This is why, according to one opinion, we will not mention the exodus from Egypt in the days of Mashiach; and even according to the opinion that we *will* mention it, it requires a special teaching. What, then, is the meaning of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders"—that the superiority of the future redemption is that even then, there will be "wonders" just as there were in the exodus from Egypt? Moreover, the phrasing implies that the wonders of the days of the exodus are the beginning and the root of the wonders that will occur in the future redemption, which implies that the redemption from Egypt possesses a certain superiority over the future redemption.

2) The core of the explanation is that this verse has two interpretations. The first interpretation is that the future redemption will be like the redemption from Egypt, in that there will be wonders just as there were then. The second interpretation is that the wonders of the future redemption will be "wonders" even in comparison to the wonders of the days of the exodus from Egypt. That is to say, although in a general sense the wonders of the future redemption are similar to those of the Egyptian redemption (as per the simple interpretation), there will nevertheless be an advantage in the future wonders, to the point that they will be considered wonders even when compared to the wonders of the exodus.

In the language of Kabbalah, the superiority of the future wonders over those of the exodus is explained as follows: It is known that at the exodus, it was necessary to extract the Jewish people from the forty-nine gates of impurity. This is why the days of Sefiras HaOmer begin immediately after the exodus, as each day we emerge from one of those gates. This emergence was accomplished through the drawing down of the fifty gates of Binah. Therefore, the exodus is mentioned in the Torah fifty times, corresponding to the fifty gates of Binah. It is known that there are various levels within these fifty gates of Binah: the fifty gates as they are drawn down into Malchus (which is the drawing down of the *sitra d'yovla*—Binah—into Malchus); higher than that, the fifty gates as they exist in their own place, in Binah; and higher still, as they are included in the fiftieth gate, in the level of Keter (and within Keter itself, in the level of Atik).

The fact that the future revelations will be "wonders even in comparison to the wonders of the exodus" is because the fifty gates of Binah drawn down at the exodus were the gates of the level of Binah (specifically, as drawn down through Malchus). In contrast, in the future, there will be a drawing down of the fifty gates of Binah as they exist in Keter. Furthermore, within Keter itself, the drawing down will be from the level of Atik, and within Atik itself, from the level of the *pnimiyus* (inner dimension) of Atik. As it is written regarding Mashiach: "Behold, My servant shall prosper; he shall be exalted and lifted up, and shall be very high." The word "very" (*me'od*) refers to the level of the *pnimiyus* of Atik. In the language of *Pri Etz Chaim*, all current drawing-downs are only from the level of the *chitzoniyus* (outer dimension) of Atik, whereas in the future, there will be a drawing down and revelation of the *pnimiyus* of Atik.

Nevertheless, the verse compares the future wonders to those of the exodus, as per the simple interpretation, because even at the exodus, the most exalted revelations occurred. As explained in the *Hemshech* of 5672, the revelation was of "the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, in His glory and His essence." "In His glory and His essence" refers to the most exalted levels. However, at the exodus, the revelation of this level was through its enclothement in the level of Malchus—"And Hashem passed over the doorway"—as the doorway is the level of Malchus. In the future, this will be revealed without any garment, as it is written: "Your Teacher shall no longer be hidden by a garment."

3) Based on this, we must understand why the verse compares the future redemption to the days of the exodus. It is even more difficult to understand according to the opinion that we will mention the exodus even in the days of Mashiach. Seemingly, since the revelations of the future will be far more exalted than those of the exodus, why would we mention the exodus then? The explanation for this (as written in the discourse "KiYimei Tzeitcha" by the Rebbe Rayatz) is that the redemption from Egypt inaugurated the general concept of redemption and opened the channel for all subsequent redemptions—including the future redemption, even though it will be more exalted.

This is similar to the idea that the exodus was a preparation for the Giving of the Torah, as it is written: "When you bring the people out of Egypt, you shall serve G-d on this mountain." Although the essence of redemption and freedom occurred specifically at the Giving of the Torah—because before that, the decree remained that "the upper realms shall not descend to the lower, and the lower shall not ascend to the upper"—it is understood that until the Giving of the Torah (even after the exodus), there was not yet the true redemption and freedom of the lower realms. Nevertheless, at the time of the exodus, the power was granted for the redemption (the nullification of the decree) that occurred at the Giving of the Torah. Similarly, at the exodus, the power was granted for the future redemption, because at the time of the Giving of the Torah, there was already a "taste" of the future revelation. However, at the Giving of the Torah, the revelation was only temporary, whereas in the future, it will be constant and eternal. This is because the revelation then will not only be from Above, as it was at the Giving of the Torah, but also from below—the created beings will be in a state of ultimate refinement and purification, becoming vessels for the revelation of Elokus, to grasp the knowledge of their Creator according to human capacity. This creates a "complete place" for the indwelling of the Ohr Ein Sof that transcends human capacity.

4) This is also the meaning of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt" (plural). All the days from the first redemption from Egypt until the future redemption are "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," as explained in that discourse. This means that the fact that the channel for the future redemption was opened at the exodus follows a sequence: every single day (from the exodus until the future redemption) an act of "exodus from Egypt" occurs in an increasingly exalted manner, until it reaches its ultimate peak in the future redemption.

The concept is that our Sages taught: "In every generation and every day, a person is obligated to see himself as if he went out from Egypt today." Every day there must be an exodus from Egypt, especially during the recitation of the Shema. As the Alter Rebbe explains, the reason the passage of the exodus was instituted specifically during the Shema is that they (the Shema and the exodus) are one and the same. This concept (that the *avodah* of the exodus must be present during the Shema) applies also to the evening Shema. As the Rambam writes, the reason we recite the passage of Tzitzis during the evening Shema, even though the mitzvah of Tzitzis does not apply at night, is because it contains the remembrance of the exodus from Egypt.

This is the meaning of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt" (plural), even though the exodus occurred on one day. Because we mention the exodus every day (which occurred for the first time on one day), this makes them "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt." Every day we emerge from more refined boundaries and limitations, and we establish ourselves in a state of "spreading out" (*u'faratzta*) beyond measurement and limitation, until we reach the ultimate perfection of *u'faratzta* in the true and complete redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, of whom it is written: "The breaker has gone up before them." Mashiach is a descendant of David, and the sign of Mashiach is that he occupies himself with Torah and mitzvos like his ancestor David, and fights the wars of Hashem. David is from the descendants of Peretz, of whom it is written: "What a breach you have made for yourself," a breaching of all boundaries, measurements, and limitations.

5) This sequence of redemption—which begins with the exodus in its simple sense, and through the *avodah* of remembering the exodus daily, leads to the complete redemption through "the breaker who goes up before us"—has a parallel in every single day. At the beginning of the day, before davening, a person is in "Egypt," in the straits and boundaries of the body. As it is written: "Cease from man, in whose nostrils is a breath, for how is he to be accounted?" Do not read *bameh* (how), but *bamah* (a high place/altar). At the beginning of the day, the soul is only in the nostrils and has not yet spread throughout the body; therefore, he is then considered like a *bamah*, which represents the strength of the straits and boundaries of the materiality of the body.

Therefore, the beginning of the *avodah* is in the manner of *iskafya* (coercion), where he overcomes the materiality of the body, the animal soul, and the *sitra achra*, until he causes even the body and animal soul to answer "Amen" against their will and assist in serving Hashem. But even this is only in the manner of *iskafya*. Later, he arrives at the level of *it-hafcha* (transformation). Within this, he first experiences it in the manner of "my heart is hollow within me," and subsequently he reaches "And you shall love Hashem your G-d with all your heart"—with both your inclinations—where even the *yetzer hara* itself is transformed into good. This is similar to *teshuvah sheleimah* (teshuvah from love), where willful sins become like merits, and eventually become actual merits.

These two aspects that occur every day (iskafya and it-hafcha) are parallel to the redemption from Egypt and the future redemption. Regarding the exodus, it is written: "For the people fled," because evil was in its strength, and they had to flee from the evil—this is the level of *iskafya*. In the future, "I will remove the spirit of impurity from the earth" (parallel to "my heart is hollow within me"), and more than that, even the evil itself will be transformed into good (parallel to "with all your heart, with both your inclinations"). As it is written: "For then I will turn to the peoples a pure language, that they may all call upon the name of Hashem, to serve Him with one consent"—even the nations themselves will be transformed to serve Him, and "strangers shall stand and feed your flocks." This will also apply to animals: "I will cause the evil beast to cease from the land"—meaning, I will cause them to cease from harming. Even though the existence of evil beasts will remain, they will not harm; furthermore, they will assist and serve in the *avodah* of Hashem. As it is written: "The wolf shall dwell with the lamb... and the lion shall eat straw like the ox," meaning there will be benefit from the wolf as from the lamb, and from the lion as from the ox. And even the snake (the viper and the asp) will be a "great servant"—not only will the snake serve the *avodah* of Hashem, but its service will be in a manner of greatness, a "great servant."

6) Although the level of *it-hafcha* is, in general, far more exalted than the level of *iskafya*, there is nevertheless an advantage in *iskafya* over *it-hafcha*. This is understood from what is written in the Tanya: that the definition of a "servant of G-d" is specifically one who coerces and changes his nature, an advantage not present in a *tzaddik gamur*. Through the *avodah* of *iskafya*, "the glory of the Holy One, Blessed be He, is exalted in all the worlds"—a drawing down and revelation of the light that is in the level of transcendence.

This is explained at length in the *Hemshech* of the day of the Hilula, with a parable of a king of flesh and blood who, for the sake of winning a war, spends all his treasures. In normal years, he not only does not use them for anything, but they are hidden and sealed from the sight of any observer. Specifically during the time of war, he not only opens all the treasures, but furthermore, he spends them without any measurement or limitation, giving them to the soldiers in order to win the war. The parallel to this Above is that there is a level of treasure that is hidden and sealed from the sight of any observer—"No eye has seen it, O G-d, except for You"—and in order to win the war against the *yetzer* (the *avodah* of *iskafya*), this treasure is also given, and in a manner of "spending." This giving of the treasure is specifically to the simple soldiers, because since their *avodah* is with *mesiras nefesh* (self-sacrifice) beyond the measurement and limitation of the intellect, the giving of the treasure to them is also in a manner of "spending" beyond all measurement and limitation.

Based on this, we can understand why we will mention the exodus even in the future, even though the revelations of the future will be more exalted (wonders) than those of the exodus. Because the drawing down of the level of the "treasure" is specifically for the sake of winning the war, and in the future there will be no war with the *yetzer*—for "I will remove the spirit of impurity from the earth"—therefore, we will mention the exodus even then, so that there will also be the advantage of *iskafya*, the drawing down of the level of the treasure. And this drawing down will then be in ultimate revelation (like all things in the future), and "Your Teacher shall no longer be hidden by a garment."

7) This is the meaning of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders": that in the future redemption, both advantages will be present—the advantage of *iskafya*, "as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," and the advantage of *it-hafcha*, "I will show you wonders." Through our deeds and our *avodah* during the time of exile—where the general *avodah* of the time of exile is in a manner of *mesiras nefesh* beyond measurement and limitation, for in the time of exile, and especially in the footsteps of Mashiach, there are obstacles and hindrances and a multitude of trials, even the trial of "not being ashamed before people who mock him," and nevertheless, one does not take into account all the obstacles, and one learns Torah and fulfills mitzvos without any calculations, to the point that "even the empty ones among you are full of mitzvos like a pomegranate"—not only do they fulfill mitzvos, but they are *full* of mitzvos. Through this, we emerge from exile with a "great wealth" of mitzvos and a "great wealth" of Torah (for Torah study is also a mitzvah, and on the contrary, it is equal to all the mitzvos). Since this (the great wealth of Torah and mitzvos) adds to the merit, it also adds urgency to the "I will hasten it," in a manner of "coming with the clouds of heaven," that the true and complete redemption will be very soon, and in the manner of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders"—meaning that both advantages will be present.

Since the redemption is through our deeds and our *avodah* during the time of exile, and especially in the footsteps of Mashiach, therefore there will be "as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," the advantage of *iskafya*, and together with this, there will be "I will show you wonders," wonders even in comparison to the wonders (signs and miracles) that were at the exodus, including the wonders that were at the splitting of the Red Sea. Although at the splitting of the sea there were most exalted revelations—as each one pointed with his finger and said, "This is my G-d and I will glorify Him," and even a maidservant at the sea saw what the prophets did not see—all of this was "at the sea," enclothed in the level of Malchus (similar to the revelation of the exodus, before the splitting of the sea, which was at the "doorway"). Furthermore, the revelation of the splitting of the sea was only temporary, because the revelation then was from Above, and the lower realm was not a vessel for it; in general, it was a matter of *iskafya* and not *it-hafcha* (similar to the exodus, which was in the manner of "for the people fled"), because it did not come from the side of the *avodah* of the lower realm. However, in the future, there will be "I will show you wonders," wonders even in comparison to the wonders of the exodus and the splitting of the sea, where the revelation will be without any garment and also from the side of the lower realm. Together with this, there will also be the advantage of "as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," speedily in our days, with the coming of Mashiach Tzidkeinu.

## 13 Nissan, 5749

"This matzah that we eat—for what reason? Because the dough of our ancestors did not have time to become leavened before the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, revealed Himself to them and redeemed them." It is explained in the discourses of our Rebbes that the reason given for eating matzah on Pesach—that it did not have time to become leavened—applies even though the commandment to eat matzah was given beforehand. For regarding the commandment to eat matzah, it is written, "In the evening you shall eat matzah" (*matzah* spelled without a *vav*), whereas in the verse, "And they baked the dough which they had brought out of Egypt into cakes of *matzos*," it is written *matzos* (spelled with a *vav*). The commandment to eat matzah was before midnight, and since before midnight they were still in Egypt, before the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, had revealed Himself to them, this matzah is written as *matzah* (without a *vav*). In contrast, the verse "And they baked the dough... into cakes of *matzos*" refers to the matzah they baked (and ate) after they had left Egypt (the dough which they had brought out of Egypt), which was after midnight, after the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, had revealed Himself to them and redeemed them; therefore, this matzah is written as *matzos* (with a *vav*). This is also why, regarding the matzah before midnight, it is written, "And you shall guard the *matzos*," implying it requires guarding so it does not become leavened, whereas regarding the matzah after midnight, it is written, "And they baked the dough... *matzos*, for it did not leaven," meaning it did not become leavened on its own, because the matzah after midnight is in the category of *matzos* (with a *vav*), the revelation of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, having been revealed to them, and therefore it does not require guarding against leavening.

Now, this distinction between before midnight and after midnight existed only during the first Pesach, before the giving of the Torah. After the giving of the Torah, however, even though the matzah we eat on the night of Pesach is before midnight, it is in the category of *matzos* (with a *vav*), possessing (also) the transcendence of the revelation of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He. This is what is meant in the Haggadah: "This matzah that we eat—for what reason? Because... it did not have time to become leavened (meaning it did not become leavened on its own) before the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, revealed Himself to them and redeemed them."

b) It can be said that the matzah we eat after the giving of the Torah is even higher than the category of *matzos* (with a *vav*) of the first Pesach before the giving of the Torah. Since the revelation of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, in the matzah we eat after the giving of the Torah comes through the preceding *avodah* of keeping the *mitzvos* throughout the entire year (before Pesach), it is a more elevated revelation. This is similar to the superiority of the revelation of *Chag HaShavuos*, the time of the giving of the Torah, which comes after the *avodah* of *Sefiras HaOmer*, over the revelation of the exodus from Egypt. The revelation of the exodus, while being an extremely lofty revelation (the King Himself), was nonetheless a revelation from above and therefore only temporary. Through the *avodah* of *Sefiras HaOmer*—the *avodah* of the lower being (after the revelation of the exodus)—we draw down, in addition to the forty-nine gates, and after the completion of the forty-nine gates, the fiftieth gate as well (which is also drawn down through our *avodah*, as it is written, "You shall count fifty days"). Within the fiftieth gate itself, there is—in addition to its lower aspect, which relates to the forty-nine gates (analogous to *Arich*)—also its higher aspect, which is above any relation to the forty-nine gates (analogous to *Atik*), which is more elevated even than the revelation of the exodus.

Furthermore, the superiority of the matzah we eat after the giving of the Torah over the matzah after midnight of that time is (in addition to the fact that the revelation in the matzah we eat comes through the preceding *avodah* of keeping the *mitzvos* before Pesach) also because the eating of the matzah itself is a *mitzvah*. The matzah after midnight then was (primarily) because the dough of our ancestors did not have time to leaven (due to the revelation from above), and there was no *avodah* (or fulfillment of a *mitzvah*) in it. The matzah we eat after the giving of the Torah is connected with the *avodah* of man, the fulfillment of the *mitzvah* of eating matzah.

c) One must understand why we say, "This matzah that we eat—for what reason? Because the dough of our ancestors did not have time to leaven." After all, the matzah we eat (after the giving of the Torah) possesses the virtue of *avodah* and the virtue of fulfilling a *mitzvah* [and in this itself—a *mitzvah* after the giving of the Torah]. How can we say that the matzah we eat is because it did not have time to leaven (which implies that its being matzah and not *chametz* happened automatically, because it did not have time to leaven)? The explanation for this is that the *mitzvah* of recounting the exodus from Egypt is to tell the story of the exodus as it was then. Furthermore (and primarily), since "if the Holy One, Blessed be He, had not taken our ancestors out of Egypt... we would still be enslaved to Pharaoh in Egypt," therefore, even now (and especially on the night of Pesach), even though we are already after the exodus and after the giving of the Torah, the status and situation that existed then also exists. Therefore, we say (in the story of the exodus in the Haggadah), "This matzah that we eat... because the dough of our ancestors did not have time to leaven before the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, revealed Himself to them and redeemed them." For the matzah we eat before midnight possesses, in addition to the virtue of the matzah from before midnight—the virtue of the *avodah* of the lower being in fulfilling the command of the Holy One, Blessed be He (as it was before the giving of the Torah)—also the virtue of the matzah from after midnight: the virtue that it did not have time to leaven (that it is impossible for it to become *chametz*) due to the revelation from above. In addition to this, the matzah we eat also possesses the transcendence that comes from the giving of the Torah: the fulfillment of a *mitzvah* after the giving of the Torah. Moreover, it also contains a semblance of the virtue of eating matzah on the seventh day of Pesach, which is a *reshus* (a voluntary act, which is higher than an obligation), which is a semblance of the eating that will be in the time to come.

d) May it be the will of Hashem, especially as we conclude (as is customary) by giving *tzedakah*, similar to how the conclusion of all the days of Pesach is on the seventh day of Pesach, and even more so, the conclusion of all the days of Pesach (outside of Israel) is on the last day of Pesach, in which the revelation of the light of *Moshiach* shines. This is why the *Haftorah* of the last day of Pesach is about the coming of *Moshiach* [and this can also be connected to the eighth day of Pesach, for in the *Haftorah* it is written, "On that day, Hashem will again add His hand a second time to acquire the remnant of His people who will remain from the seven places of exile (Assyria, Egypt, etc., and the eighth is the islands of the sea), and He will bring them to the state of the eighth—the state of the true and complete redemption"], and may the prophecy be fulfilled simply and in all its details, and also internally: "The earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea."

## 1. 

"Happy is the one You choose and bring near, that he may dwell in Your courtyards; may we be satisfied with the goodness of Your house, the holiness of Your palace". This verse is recited before a Bris Milah, as stated in the Siddur in the order of the Milah. Its source is what is written in the Zohar: "The pious ones of old... when they would bring their sons to the offering, they would open and say, 'Happy is the one You choose and bring near, that he may dwell in Your courtyards,' etc." [And although the Zohar there mentions "the pious ones of old," the Alter Rebbe included this in his Siddur, as it is a matter relevant to every soul]. 

The explanation of the connection between this verse and Bris Milah is that this verse enumerates four levels: "Your courtyards" (in the plural, indicating two types of courtyards), "Your house," and "Your palace." The order of these matters in the verse is from below to above. As the Zohar states there: "First, Your courtyards; then, Your house; and then, Your palace," for one is more internal than the other, and one is higher than the other. It can be said that these four matters correspond to the four worlds of ABYA (as will be explained in section 6). Therefore, we recite this verse before the Bris, because through the Milah there is an *hemshech* (drawing down) from above the *hishtalshelus* (chain of progression), and in order for this *hemshech* to occur, there must first be the perfection of the *hishtalshelus* (the four worlds), as will be explained (section 7).

## 2. 

The matter is that the difference between a courtyard and a house (in their literal sense) is that a house is a person's dwelling, while in a courtyard one finds animals and birds, as stated in the Mishnah: "If one was keeping an animal in the courtyard... and raising chickens". Within the courtyard itself, there are two types: a covered courtyard and an uncovered courtyard. The difference between a covered and uncovered courtyard exists in Halacha as well: an uncovered courtyard (when it is larger than two *se'ah*) is a *reshus hayachid* by Torah law, but by Rabbinic law it is a *karmelis* because it resembles a *reshus harabim*, whereas a covered courtyard is a *reshus hayachid* even by Rabbinic law. Simply put, the difference is that animals are kept even in an uncovered courtyard [provided the courtyard is enclosed by partitions], whereas birds (which can fly over the partitions) are kept only in a covered courtyard.

This can be explained based on the Gemara: "An animal, which is created from dry land, is rendered fit by two signs... a bird, which is created from the marsh, is rendered fit by one sign." It is explained elsewhere that an animal, which is created from the earth (dry land)—and therefore possesses a healthy and strong vitality—alludes to the *nefesh habehamis* as it exists in its state of coarseness. Therefore, it is rendered fit by two signs. A bird, which is created from the marsh (water and earth), alludes to the *nefesh habehamis* as it exists in its state of subtlety. Therefore, it is rendered fit by one sign. More specifically, the animal is the *nefesh habehamis* itself, which is from the lower levels of *nogah* (its coarseness), while the bird is the *nefesh hasichlis* (the intellectual soul), which is from the higher levels of *nogah* (its subtlety). Therefore, it serves as an intermediary between the *nefesh habehamis* and the *nefesh ha'elokis*. 

This is why the bird "flies in the air" (from time to time). This can be explained in two ways: The flight in the air (of the bird) is due to the *nefesh hasichlis* itself. For it is the nature of the intellect (even the intellect of the *nefesh hasichlis*—even though it is a natural intellect, an intellect of *nogah*, nevertheless, since it is intellect, its nature is) to be drawn to that which is above it. Another aspect of this is that through the *hitlabshus* (enclothement) of the *nefesh ha'elokis* within the *nefesh hasichlis*, it can grasp matters of *Elokus* that are above the world. However, even then, it only agrees with the apprehension of the *nefesh ha'elokis*, while remaining in its essence—a human intellect.

It can be said that the reason animals are kept even in an uncovered courtyard while birds are kept only in a covered courtyard (even though birds are more subtle) is that the subtlety of *nogah* is not as recognizable, and therefore requires extra guarding. To add: just as in the physical realm, the reason birds are not guarded in an uncovered courtyard is because they can fly (over the partitions), so too in the spiritual realm: the *nefesh hasichlis* requires extra guarding because it possesses the trait of "flying in the air." That is, since the intellect of the *nefesh hasichlis* is drawn to that which is above it [and furthermore, it grasps (through the enclothement of the *nefesh ha'elokis* in the *nefesh hasichlis*) matters of *Elokus* that are above the world], therefore, in order to find the evil within the intellect, it must be through the contemplation that *Elokus* is wondrously beyond human intellect [meaning that even the matters of *Elokus* grasped by human intellect are not grasped by the intellect as they are in their truth, for since they are *Elokus*, they are *bli gevul* (infinite), and as the intellect grasps them, it necessarily limits them]. By the *nefesh hasichlis* (the bird) sensing the wonder of *Elokus* that is above intellect—its "courtyard" being covered by a "roof"—the subtlety of the evil within it is refined and it is guarded.

## 3. 

Based on this, we can explain why the law that an uncovered courtyard is a *karmelis* is a Rabbinic decree. The guarding of the animal (in man's *avodah*) relates primarily to thought, speech, and action, where, due to the desires of the *nefesh habehamis*, one might stumble into some prohibition, *chas v'shalom*. The guarding of the animal is by fencing the courtyard (the place where the animal is found) with partitions—meaning, not to do what one wants, but only what one is required to do. The guarding of the bird (in man's *avodah*), which relates to the subtlety of *nogah* (as mentioned above), relates primarily to the internal faculties: intellect and emotions. Because of the *yeshus* (selfhood) and pride within him (from the fact that he is a man of intellect: "a wise man in his own eyes"), it is not enough to fence the courtyard with partitions. For even when he is fenced in his thought, speech, and action, and even when he is at the level of a *beinoni* who has never committed a sin, nevertheless, due to the pride and coarseness within him, his intellect and emotions are coarse. Therefore, his courtyard must be covered by a roof, so that the wonder of *Elokus* is felt within it, as mentioned above. And since the main thing is the deed, therefore, by Torah law, even an uncovered courtyard is a *reshus hayachid*—the *Yachid* (Unique One) of the world. It is only by Rabbinic decree [whose purpose is to elevate and raise man, so that he becomes a vessel for *Elokus* even through his intellect and emotions] that in order for *man* to be a *reshus hayachid* for His Unity, may He be blessed, it must be covered by a roof.

## 4. 

Now, since the intellect of the *nefesh hasichlis* is, by its own nature, a state of *yesh* (existence), and furthermore, it is from the *kelipah* of *nogah*, therefore, even after the intellect of the *nefesh hasichlis* grasps the disparity between itself and *Elokus* [that its grasp of matters of *Elokus* is through limitation and corporeality, and it does not grasp the matters as they are in their truth], which brings it to a state of *bitul*, its *bitul* is not at all comparable to the *bitul* of the *nefesh ha'elokis*. For in the *nefesh ha'elokis*, its *bitul* is in its very existence, while in the *nefesh hasichlis*, its *bitul* is something added to its existence, and in essence, it is a state of *yesh*. This is why even a courtyard covered by a roof is a courtyard—a place where living creatures are found (as mentioned in section 2) and not a human dwelling. For *Adam* (man), which has the numerical value of *Mah* (45), is the *bitul* of the *nefesh ha'elokis*, which is called *Adam* after the "likeness of the One Above". This is not the case with the *bitul* of the *nefesh habehamis* and the *nefesh hasichlis*, which is a *bitul* of *yesh* and is a level of "living" that is below *Adam*.

Now, even in the *bitul* of the *nefesh ha'elokis*, there are two general levels. It is known that for the *nefesh ha'elokis* to act upon the *nefesh habehamis* and the *nefesh hasichlis*, it is through the intellect and emotions of the *nefesh ha'elokis* coming into measurement and limitation, in a way that they are relevant to the *nefesh hasichlis* and the *nefesh habehamis*, until they are enclothed within the intellect and emotions of the *nefesh hasichlis* and the *nefesh habehamis*. Thus, there are two levels in the intellect and emotions of the *nefesh ha'elokis*: as they are in themselves, and as they are enclothed in the *nefesh hasichlis* and the *nefesh habehamis*. Based on what is explained elsewhere that the power the *nefesh ha'elokis* has to effect the refinement and purification of the *nefesh habehamis* is because the *nefesh ha'elokis* is *Elokus* in its very essence, it can be said that in the intellect and emotions of the *nefesh ha'elokis* *as they are enclothed in the nefesh habehamis*, there are also two aspects: in their revelation (as they are relative to the *nefesh hasichlis* and *habehamis*), they are in measurement and limitation, and their *bitul* is *similar* to the *bitul* of *yesh* (which is why they act upon the *nefesh hasichlis* and *habehamis*); and in their inwardness, they are *Elokus*, and their *bitul* is *bitul* in essence.

## 5. 

It can be said that the four matters mentioned above—the two levels of the courtyard, which are the refinement of the *nefesh habehamis* and the *nefesh hasichlis*, and the two aspects of the faculties of the *nefesh ha'elokis* as they are in revelation and as they are in their essence—are similar to the four worlds of ABYA. The matter is that *Asiyah* and *Yetzirah* are *metzius* (existence), *Beriah* is only the possibility of existence, and *Atzilus* is the nullification of existence. Among the differences between *Asiyah* and *Yetzirah* (existence) and *Beriah* (the possibility of existence) is that the *bitul* in *Asiyah* and *Yetzirah* is a *bitul* of *yesh*, for since *Asiyah* and *Yetzirah* are existence, the *bitul* within them is something added to their existence. The *bitul* in *Beriah*, which is only the possibility of existence, is *bitul* in essence, meaning that its entire existence is *bitul*. This is why *Yetzirah* is the abode of the angels, whose *avodah* is through natural love and fear, while *Beriah* is the abode of the souls of the *tzaddikim*, whose *avodah* was through intellectual love and fear (and the angels whose *avodah* is through intellectual love and fear also have their place in *Beriah*). For natural love and fear are the nature (the existence) of the created being, whereas intellectual love and fear mean that the love and fear are not from his nature and existence, but because the *Eloki* matter he has grasped necessitates that one must love Hashem and fear Him. However, even so, since *Beriah* is the realm of apprehension, and the *bitul* in *Beriah* is because the intellect necessitates that regarding *Elokus* there is no place for existence, the *bitul* itself is still an existence. The truth of the matter of *bitul* in essence is in *Atzilus*, where the *bitul* is not from the apprehension of the intellect but from *Elokus*, that He alone is, and there is nothing else. Therefore, in the world of *Atzilus*, the existence of angels is not applicable (not even intellectual angels), and the world of *Atzilus* is the abode for the great *tzaddikim* who were in the state of a *merkavah* (chariot) above intellect—*bitul* in essence. And although there are ten *Sefiros* (intellect and emotions) in *Atzilus*, the *Sefiros* of *Atzilus* are not an existence (for themselves) at all, for "He and His life are one, and He and His attributes are one".

This is why the four matters mentioned above in man's *avodah* are similar to the four worlds of ABYA: the *bitul* in the *nefesh habehamis* and the *nefesh hasichlis*, where the *bitul* is something added to their existence, is along the lines of the *bitul* of *Asiyah* and *Yetzirah*. [To add: the fact that a courtyard requires guarding is because *Asiyah* and *Yetzirah* are existence, and therefore the things that have progressed from them require guarding. And the term "Your courtyards" in the plural is because the *avodah* from the world of *Asiyah* is primarily in actual deed, for which the guarding of partitions—an uncovered courtyard—is sufficient, while the *avodah* from *Yetzirah* is primarily in emotions, for which the guarding of a roof—a covered courtyard—is needed]. The intellect and emotions of the *nefesh ha'elokis* that are enclothed in the *nefesh habehamis*, where the *bitul* is not something added to their existence but is similar to the *bitul* of *yesh*, is along the lines of the *bitul* of *Beriah*, and the *bitul* of the *nefesh ha'elokis* itself (including the *bitul* of the level of the *neshamah* enclothed in the *nefesh habehamis* as it is in its inwardness), which is *bitul* in essence, is along the lines of the *bitul* of *Atzilus*.

## 6. 

This is the meaning of "First, Your courtyards; then, Your house; and then, Your palace." "First, Your courtyards" (in the plural: uncovered courtyard and covered courtyard) is the refinement and purification of the *nefesh habehamis* and the *nefesh hasichlis*, which, even after their refinement and purification, are at the level of living creatures. "Then, Your house" is the revelation of the *nefesh ha'elokis* as it is enclothed in the *nefesh hasichlis* and the *nefesh habehamis*, whose *bitul* is similar to the *bitul* in *Beriah*. It is called "house" because just as a house is a place for a person to dwell, as it is written, "like the beauty of a man, to dwell in a house," so too the world of *Beriah* (the world of the Throne) is the dwelling place of the *Adam HaElyon* (the Supernal Man). The primary dwelling of the *Adam HaElyon* within created beings is when their *bitul* to *Elokus* is in such a way that this (*bitul*) is their entire existence. "And then, Your palace" is the revelation of the *neshamah* that is above enclothement in the *nefesh habehamis* (including also the inwardness of the level of the *neshamah* enclothed in the *nefesh habehamis*), whose *bitul* is *bitul* in essence, like the *bitul* of *Atzilus*. It is called "Your palace," for the matter of "Your palace" is as it is written, "Hashem is in His holy palace; be silent before Him, all the earth," for before "Hashem is in His holy palace," all matters of existence ("all the earth") are nullified (*hes*), to the point where even the *possibility* of existence is not applicable.

## 7. 

This is why before Bris Milah we recite the verse "Happy is the one You choose and bring near," etc., for the four worlds of ABYA are within the order of *hishtalshelus*, and Milah is above *hishtalshelus*. As our Sages say regarding the verse, "For the leader, upon the eighth," referring to the Milah that was given on the eighth day. It is explained in *Likkutei Torah* (in the *maamar* "For the leader, upon the eighth") that the reason David's song was about the fact that Milah was given *on the eighth* is because the eighth is the level of *Adam Kadmon* which is above *Atzilus*. Seemingly, one can add that Milah is above not only the four worlds but also the four letters of the name *Havayah* (from the word *mehoveh*—bringing into being), from which the four worlds are brought into being. As is written in *Torah Or* on the verse, "Who shall ascend for us to heaven?"—which are the initial letters of *Milah* and the final letters of *Havayah*. The fact that *Milah* is the initial letters and *Havayah* is the final letters is because *Milah* is above *Havayah*. However, in order for the *hemshech* of the revelation that is above *hishtalshelus* to occur through the Milah, there must first be the perfection of the *hishtalshelus*, as our Sages say, "so that one Shabbos may pass over him," for Shabbos is the perfection of the *hishtalshelus*, and through this it is possible to reach the revelation that is above *hishtalshelus* (Milah on the eighth). It can be said that this is what is written, "Walk before Me and be whole (*tamim*)," that first there must be "before Me" (*lefanai*), and then "and be" (*vehiyeh*—the letters of *Havayah*), and through this one subsequently arrives at the level of *tamim*—the revelation through Milah. Therefore, we recite before the Bris the verse "Happy is the one You choose and bring near, that he may dwell in Your courtyards; may we be satisfied with the goodness of Your house, the holiness of Your palace" [and also on the night before the Bris (which is the beginning of the preparation for the Bris), it is customary to study this *maamar* of the Zohar on this verse], because the *avodah* in the four matters in this verse, which correspond to the four worlds and the four letters of the name *Havayah*, is the preparation and the vessel for the revelation that is above *hishtalshelus*.

## Acharon Shel Pesach 5712

"As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show him wonders." It requires understanding: the future redemption will be far more elevated than the redemption of the exodus from Egypt. [Therefore, according to the opinion of Ben Zoma, we do not mention the exodus from Egypt in the days of Mashiach. And even according to the Sages who hold that we do mention the exodus from Egypt in the days of Mashiach, because there is a certain superiority in the exodus from Egypt even relative to the future redemption, the redemption of the exodus from Egypt will be secondary to the future redemption.] What, then, is the meaning of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show him wonders"—that the elevation of the future redemption is that even then, "I will show him wonders" as it was during the exodus from Egypt?

Furthermore, one must understand the emphasis on "I will *show* him wonders." The primary superiority of the future redemption is not in the wonders themselves that will occur then, but in the fact that Hashem will *show* the wonders. Also, one must understand the use of the word *ar'enu* ("I will show him"), which is in the third person (hidden), whereas the beginning of the verse says *tzeit'cha* ("your exodus"), which is in the second person (direct address); it should have said *ar'echa* ("I will show you"). Regarding what is stated in the Zohar that *ar'enu* is in the third person because it refers to Moshe, we must clarify the connection between this interpretation and the literal interpretation that *ar'enu* refers to the Jewish people.

The crux of the explanation is that there are, generally speaking, two types of miracles: miracles that transcend nature [such as the miracles of the exodus from Egypt—turning water into blood, etc., up to the miracle of the splitting of the Sea, where the sea was turned into dry land], and miracles that are clothed within the ways of nature. Within the latter, there are two modes: those where the "garments" of nature are clearly seen to be merely a garment for the miracle clothed within them, such as the miracles of Chanukah and Purim, where, despite being clothed in the ways of nature, the miracle within them was clearly evident (to the point that "all the ends of the earth saw" it); and those where the garments of nature conceal the miracle clothed within them.

It is known that the root of the miracles clothed in nature is from a higher place than the root of the miracles that transcend nature. And the root of the miracles clothed in nature in such a way that it is not even recognizable that the garments of nature are merely a garment, is from an even higher place. As it is written, "To Him who does great wonders alone," and our Sages said, "Even the beneficiary of the miracle does not recognize his miracle," for the root of these wonders (where the beneficiary does not recognize his miracle) is from the aspect of "alone."

This is the innovation in the fact that in the future redemption there will be "I will show him wonders": the wonders of the future redemption will be drawn from Him, may He be blessed, "alone." As our Sages said, "In the past (at the exodus from Egypt), I and My court would walk before them, but in the future to come, I alone." The miracles that occurred at the exodus from Egypt were drawn from Him, may He be blessed, through His court, whereas the miracles of the future redemption will be from Him alone (not through His court). This is the innovation of "I will show him wonders"—that even though the wonders drawn from Him, may He be blessed, alone are (generally) in a manner where He alone knows it is a miracle, nevertheless, there will be "I will show him wonders," meaning the miracle within them will be visibly revealed.

2) It is known that the precision in the phrase "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt"—*kim'ei* (as in the days), in the plural—is that the exodus from Egypt occurred on a single day [and regarding the mitzvah of remembering the exodus from Egypt, it is written: "Remember this day on which you went out of Egypt," "so that you may remember the day of your exodus from the land of Egypt"]. Why, then, is it said *kim'ei* in the plural?

The explanation is that the exodus from Egypt also includes the splitting of the Sea. [This is also the case in the mitzvah of remembering the exodus from Egypt every day, which includes the remembrance of the splitting of the Sea. Likewise, in the mitzvah of recounting the exodus from Egypt on the night of Pesach, "whoever expands upon the recounting of the exodus from Egypt is praiseworthy," which also includes the recounting of the splitting of the Sea.] Therefore, it is said *kim'ei tzeit'cha*—in the plural. [Regarding the fact that the mitzvah of remembering the exodus from Egypt says "the *day* of your exodus from the land of Egypt," this is because the essence of the mitzvah of remembering the exodus from Egypt is the remembrance of the exodus itself, and the remembrance of the splitting of the Sea is not indispensable.]

The meaning of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show him wonders" is that in the future redemption, there will be the miracles and wonders that occurred at the exodus from Egypt itself, as well as the miracle and wonder of the splitting of the Sea. As it is written, "And Hashem will utterly destroy the tongue of the Egyptian sea... and there shall be a highway... as there was for Israel on the day he went up from the land of Egypt." We must understand the connection between these two matters in "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show him wonders"—that even the wonders drawn from Him, may He be blessed, alone will be in a manner of "I will show him" (that they will see the miracle within them), and that the wonders of the future redemption will also include the wonder of the splitting of the Sea.

3) This can be understood by prefacing that the reason the root of miracles clothed in nature is from a higher place than the root of miracles that transcend nature is because, in miracles that transcend nature, the revelation of the Ohr Ein Sof, the *Bli Gevul* (Infinite) that is above the worlds (from which the miracles are drawn), is in a manner where nature is nullified by the revelation—a disruption of the laws of nature—and consequently, the revelation is not drawn into the world. In miracles clothed in nature, the revelation of the Ohr Ein Sof, the *Bli Gevul* that is above the worlds, is within the world itself.
