In general, this is the *avodah* of *mesiras nefesh* that is above *ta'am v'da'as* (reason and knowledge), for the matter of *mesiras nefesh* is that one does not enter into any debate, but rather it is impossible at all to be in any other way.

The matter is that during the time of the Beis HaMikdash, all of Yisrael were on an exalted level. In addition to being possessors of great comprehension, when they ascended for the pilgrimage, they also had a vision of Elokus (which is above comprehension), as it is written, "Three times a year shall all your males appear..." and "Just as he comes to appear, so he comes to see." This is not the case in the time of *galus*, for it is written, "I sleep," and our Sages say, "in *galus*." Just as in sleep in the literal sense, all internal faculties are concealed—beginning with the faculty of intellect, for during sleep the faculty of intellect departs and only the faculty of imagination remains, and the primary concealment (resulting from sleep) is in the faculty of vision (to the point that this is also evident in the limbs of the body, as the eyes close during sleep)—so too is it in the "sleep" of *galus*. The primary concealment of *galus* is that during the time of *galus*, there is no vision of Elokus; "we have not seen our signs." Furthermore, the comprehension of Elokus (which does exist during the time of *galus*) is not at all comparable to the comprehension of the time of the Beis HaMikdash, to the point that the comprehension of the time of *galus* (relative to the comprehension of the time of the Beis HaMikdash) is analogous to the faculty of imagination in sleep. Because of this, the *ahavah* and *yirah* (of the time of *galus*) are also not as they should be. For in addition to the fact that in *galus* it is impossible to have the *bitul* of prostration (internal *bitul*) which comes specifically from the vision of Elokus—as it is written, "And we are unable to ascend and appear and prostrate ourselves before You," for since we cannot ascend and appear, we naturally cannot (also) prostrate ourselves to be in a state of internal *bitul*—the external *bitul* (the *bitul* of bowing), which comes from the comprehension of Elokus (which exists, in general, even during *galus*), is also not as it should be, since even the comprehension of Elokus during *galus* is not true comprehension. The primary *avodah* during *galus* is by way of *nitzachon*, as mentioned above. Within the time of *galus* itself, from generation to generation, the minds and hearts diminish, as our Sages say, "The hearts of the early ones..." and "We are like the eye of a fine needle." This is especially true in the generation of the footsteps of Mashiach, where the concealments and hiddenness have increased (in addition to the diminution of the minds). Therefore, in this time, the matter of *nitzachon* and *mesiras nefesh* that is above *ta'am v'da'as* is even more relevant. Even those who possess minds today must set aside their intellect and not follow *ta'am v'da'as* (for through intellect one might, Heaven forbid, stray from the path of truth, especially now that the concealments and hiddenness have increased, as mentioned above), but rather stand strong not to deviate, Heaven forbid, from the *Shulchan Aruch* even by a hair's breadth—an *avodah* by way of *nitzachon* and *mesiras nefesh* that is above intellect.

3) The maamar explains the reason why the concealment and hiddenness of *galus* (including the concealment and hiddenness of the generation of the footsteps of Mashiach) is only on the internal faculties of the *neshamah* and not on the attribute of *Netzach* (victory): it is because the root of *Netzach* is in the essence of the soul. It is the same above, where the root of *Netzach* is in the inwardness of *Keter*, and in the inwardness of *Keter*, the matter of change does not apply. As it is written, "And also the *Netzach* of Yisrael will not lie nor repent, for He is not a man to repent." The matter of repentance and change applies only to the level of "man"—*hishtalshelus* (the order of descent). Since the root of *Netzach* is in the level of "not a man," above *hishtalshelus*, change does not apply to it, and it exists in revelation even during the time of *galus*. Furthermore, during the time of *galus*, and especially in the footsteps of Mashiach, the power of *nitzachon* and *mesiras nefesh* is in greater revelation than during the time of the Beis HaMikdash.

One could say that the reason the maamar explains (at length) the virtue of the root of *Netzach* is (also) to explain why the revelation of the future to come comes specifically through the prerequisite of the *avodah* of the time of *galus*. Since the *avodah* during *galus* is by way of *nitzachon*, and the root of *Netzach* is from the inwardness of *Keter*—the level of "He made darkness His hiding place," which is above revelation—therefore, through the *avodah* of the time of *galus*, the matters that are above comprehension and vision will be revealed in the future to come (above even the comprehension and vision of the future), only that Mashiach will reveal them in the manner of kingship, as mentioned above.

4) The maamar continues that the explanation of this matter (that the revelation of the future comes specifically through the *avodah* of the time of *galus*) can be understood from the matter of the descent of the *neshamah* below, for the descent is for the sake of an ascent. From this, it is understood also regarding the descent of *galus*, that it is for the sake of an ascent. Through the *avodah* of the time of *galus* specifically, there will be the revelation of the future to come, which is more exalted than the revelation that was during the time of the Beis HaMikdash. One could say that the addition of this explanation (that the intent of the descent of *galus* is from the outset for the sake of an ascent) is to explain the reason why even the revelation of Mashiach—which he will reveal as a *rav*, in comprehension and vision—comes specifically through the *avodah* of the time of *galus* (even though the matter of comprehension and certainly vision was specifically during the time of the Beis HaMikdash, as mentioned above). This can be understood from the matter of the descent in the general *hishtalshelus*: the beginning of the descent is the *Tzimtzum HaRishon* (the first contraction). It is known that the action of the *Tzimtzum* is both in the *Ohr Ein Sof* (the infinite light) and in the light that is in the category of *gevul* (limitation—the light that illuminates the worlds). The action of the *Tzimtzum* on the infinite light is that it is concealed and included in its source, and does not shine in revelation in the place of the *chalal* (the void); that is, the action of the *Tzimtzum* on this light is not in the light itself. The reason may be that since this light is above the value of the worlds, the *Tzimtzum* does not have the capacity to effect a descent and limitation in it, and the entire action of the *Tzimtzum* on the light is that it should not shine in the place of the *chalal*. This is not the case for the action of the *Tzimtzum* on the light that illuminates the worlds, which is in the light itself, in several respects: 1) Before the *Tzimtzum*, this light was mixed and included in the infinite light, and through the *Tzimtzum* it was separated on its own, whereby it descended from its level; 2) Through being drawn through the leap of the *Tzimtzum* not in a gradual manner, it comes into the category of the division of the ten *Sefiros* (as explained at length, that the matter of the *Sefiros* in the light is not only from the perspective of the vessels, but rather the matter of the *Sefiros* is also from the perspective of the light); 3) Through the *Tzimtzum*, the light is (subsequently) clothed in the vessels, whereby it comes into materiality, becoming like the vessels.

5) The same is true regarding the concealment of the descent of the *neshamah* below (for all concealments have their root in the concealment of the first *Tzimtzum*), which contains two matters, analogous to the two matters mentioned above regarding the first *Tzimtzum*. 1) When the *neshamah* is above, all its faculties shine in revelation, and upon its descent below, they are in concealment. This concealment is not that the body conceals, but rather that these matters of the *neshamah* remain above and do not clothe themselves in the body, analogous to how the infinite light does not shine in the place of the *chalal*. 2) Even these matters that do clothe themselves in the body are in materiality, analogous to the concealment that comes to the light of the *Kav* (the line) from the perspective of the vessels. The same is true regarding the concealment of *galus*, which contains the equivalent of these two matters: 1) That during the time of *galus*, the revelations of the Beis HaMikdash do not exist, and this is why, since the Beis HaMikdash was destroyed, we cannot ascend and appear, for there is no vision of Elokus. 2) That even the comprehension during the time of *galus* is not true comprehension, which is (not only because the revelations of the Beis HaMikdash do not exist, but also) because of the concealment and hiddenness of *galus*, which conceals and hides the comprehension of Elokus.

6) It is known that the intent of the *Tzimtzum* is so that the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* will be also in the worlds. Before the *Tzimtzum*, when the *Ohr Ein Sof* filled the place of the *chalal*, it was impossible for the existence of the worlds to be, and consequently there was no revelation in the worlds. Through the *Tzimtzum*, it was innovated that the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* would be also in the worlds. This can be understood by introducing the known principle (regarding the concealment of the *Tzimtzum*, that the infinite light was concealed and included in its source): before the *Tzimtzum*, when the *Ohr Ein Sof* filled the place of the *chalal* (meaning the infinite light shone within it), there was Elokus in simplicity and existence in innovation; through the removal of the light, there became existence in simplicity and Elokus in innovation. Regarding the distinction between these two matters in the *avodah* of man, there are several levels. In general, it is the distinction between the vision of Elokus and the comprehension of Elokus. When the knowledge of Elokus is in a manner of vision—even in the vision of *Chochmah* and certainly in the vision of prophecy, which is actual vision—the knowledge is in simplicity, such that one does not need proofs and evidence for it. This is not the case when the knowledge of Elokus is from the compulsion of the intellect, which is by way of innovation. But in particular, the matter of Elokus in simplicity will be in the future to come, whereas now, even the revelation of prophecy is by way of innovation. For in physical matters, anyone can see, and there is no wonder in this at all; whereas the matter of prophecy is specifically for one who is worthy of it, for prophecy only rests upon one who is a great sage in wisdom, mighty in his attributes, etc. The same is true in the vision of *Chochmah*, which is only for the select few. Thus, the revelation of prophecy now is by way of innovation and not by way of simplicity, and this is why they say about him as a novelty that he is a prophet. This is because the general revelation now, even the revelation of prophecy, is from the light of the *Kav* that is after the *Tzimtzum*. This is not the case in the future to come, when there will be the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is before the *Tzimtzum*; prophecy will be like a natural thing, which is why it will be in everyone, even in young boys and girls, as it is written, "I will pour out My spirit upon all flesh, and your sons and daughters will prophesy."

7) The same was true regarding the vision of Elokus that was in the Beis HaMikdash. The fact that the revelation of vision in the Beis HaMikdash was in everyone—"all your males shall appear"—is because in the Beis HaMikdash, the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is before the *Tzimtzum* shone. Nevertheless, even the revelation of the Beis HaMikdash was not like a truly natural thing. Since the revelation was only in the Beis HaMikdash (and not like the vision of physical things, for which one does not need a special place), it is understood that the fact that there was a vision of Elokus in the Beis HaMikdash was due to some cause—due to the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that shone in the Beis HaMikdash. This is not the case in the future to come, when the vision of Elokus will be like a truly natural thing, as it is written, "And all flesh shall see," for the properties of the eyes of flesh will be in such a way that they will see Elokus.

8) One must understand: the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* and the existence of the worlds are two opposites. Therefore, when the *Ohr Ein Sof* filled the place of the *chalal*, it was impossible for the formation of the worlds to be, and in order for there to be a place for the worlds, a removal of the light was necessary. Since the formation of the existence of the worlds is specifically from the removal of the light (the opposite of the matter of revelation), how is it possible that the existence of the worlds (and certainly the existence of physical flesh) can be in such a way that in its existence there will be a revelation of Elokus? The matter is that since the intent of the *Tzimtzum* is for the sake of revelation (that the revelation will be also in the existence of the worlds, as mentioned above), the inwardness of the *Tzimtzum* (and consequently also the worlds that were formed from the *Tzimtzum*) does not contradict the revelation; on the contrary, this (the matter of revelation) is its internal essence. However, in order to reveal the inwardness of the *Tzimtzum*, this is done through the *avodah* of Yisrael, and especially during the time of *galus* (as below in section 9). Based on this, one can understand why the maamar adds that the descent is for the sake of an ascent, by which it explains (as mentioned in section 4) that the fact that through the *avodah* of the time of *galus* there will be the revelation of the future to come is not only the revelation of the matters that are above comprehension and vision (which Mashiach will reveal by virtue of being a king), but also the matters of comprehension and vision, which Mashiach will reveal as a *rav*. Even the revelation of these matters is through the *avodah* of the time of *galus*, because the fact that in the future to come "all flesh shall see" by way of nature and the properties of flesh itself is specifically through revealing the internal intent of the descent—that the descent is for the sake of an ascent.

9) This matter (that the *Tzimtzum* is for the sake of revelation) is revealed (primarily) through the *avodah* of the time of *galus*. The descent of *galus* is for the sake of an ascent (analogous to how the *Tzimtzum* is for the sake of revelation). However, in this matter (the descent of *galus*), it is added that even during the time of *galus* itself, one sees in actuality how the concealment of *galus* awakens the power of *mesiras nefesh*. The matter is as explained in the maamar: during the time of *galus*, and especially in the generation of the footsteps of Mashiach, the power of *mesiras nefesh* is in greater revelation than during the time of the Beis HaMikdash. It brings a parable for this from the power of will in the human soul: although it is an encompassing power, which is why it is found in all the limbs, nevertheless, the primary revelation of the will is in the heels, which is why it is easier for a person to put his heel into boiling water than to pour boiling water on his head. So too is it in the configuration of the stature of *Knesses Yisrael*: the power of *mesiras nefesh* (a power that is unlimited, which is above clothing in vessels) is in greater revelation in simple people (the category of heels) than in those who possess minds. The same is true in the configuration of the stature of all the generations, that the primary revelation of the power of *mesiras nefesh* is in the generation of the footsteps of Mashiach. The reason for this—that the concealment of *galus* awakens and reveals the power of *mesiras nefesh* (even though concealment and revelation are two opposites)—is because the root of the concealment of *galus* is the concealment of the first *Tzimtzum*, whose intent is for the sake of revelation. Through the *avodah* of *mesiras nefesh* during the time of *galus*, the internal matter of the concealment (of the concealment of *galus*, and up to its root, the concealment of the first *Tzimtzum*) is revealed in actuality, for its essence is for the sake of revelation.

10) Based on all of the above, one can also understand why in the *avodah* of the time of *galus*, and especially in the generation of the footsteps of Mashiach, there are two matters: the study of the inwardness of Torah specifically with understanding and comprehension, and together with this, the *avodah* of *mesiras nefesh*. For in the inwardness of Torah that will be revealed in the future, there are two levels: that which can be reached in comprehension, and the essence of the inwardness of Torah which is above comprehension (and above vision as well). Since all the revelations of the future depend on our deeds and our *avodah* now, therefore, from everyone is demanded both *avodos*: engagement in the study of *Chassidus* (a taste of the Torah of Mashiach) with understanding and comprehension, and together with this, the *avodah* of *mesiras nefesh*, the surrender of the will. Through these two *avodos*, there will be the revelation of Mashiach in both manners, as *rav* and as king, speedily in our days.

## Acharon Shel Pesach 5738

### Acharon Shel Pesach 5738

"As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders". In his *maamar* on this verse [delivered thirty years ago], the Rebbe Rayatz brings the *Zohar’s* precision regarding the use of the plural form, "as in the days" (*kimiy*). After all, the exodus from Egypt occurred on a single day ("they left in one moment"). Similarly, regarding the remembrance of the exodus, it is written, "So that you may remember the day of your exodus from the land of Egypt" (using the singular "day"). Why, then, is the plural "days" used here? He explains that all the days from the time of the exodus from Egypt until the future redemption are "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," and therefore the plural "days" is used.

The *maamar* continues: to understand this, we must preface it with the *Mishnah’s* teaching on the verse, "So that you may remember the day of your exodus from the land of Egypt all the days of your life": "The days of your life" refers to this world; "all the days of your life" includes the days of Moshiach. We must understand: the future redemption will be far more exalted than the redemption from Egypt [both regarding the redemption itself, as the future redemption will be a complete redemption after which there is no exile, unlike the redemption from Egypt, and regarding the revelations of the redemption, as the revelations in the future redemption will be far more exalted than those of the exodus from Egypt]. Why, then, will the exodus from Egypt be remembered in the future? He explains that during the redemption from Egypt, a fundamental matter was innovated—the opening of the conduit for all redemptions, including the future redemption. Therefore, all the days from the time of the exodus from Egypt until the future redemption are "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," in the plural. Seemingly, one could say that the connection between the two matters in the *maamar* [the matter of the plural "days," and the fact that the exodus from Egypt will be remembered even in the days of Moshiach] is that the plural "days" is used because the conduit for all redemptions was opened during the exodus from Egypt, and therefore the exodus from Egypt will be remembered even in the days of Moshiach. Seemingly, this explanation requires further clarification, for the connection between the two matters is that both stem from the same reason. The fact that the *maamar* states that to understand the matter of the plural "days" one must first understand why the exodus from Egypt will be remembered in the future implies that the remembrance of the exodus from Egypt adds clarity to the matter of the plural "days."

2) The precision (in the *maamar*) regarding the fact that the exodus from Egypt will be remembered in the future is, on the face of it, also applicable to the verse "As in the days of your exodus, etc." Since the revelations in the future redemption will be far more exalted than those of the exodus from Egypt, why does it say, "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders," implying that the greatness of the future redemption is that even then, "I will show you wonders" just as there were in the exodus from Egypt? Furthermore, the precision in the verse "As in the days of your exodus, etc." is seemingly even greater than the fact that the exodus from Egypt will be remembered in the future. For the remembrance of the exodus from Egypt in the future will be only secondary [and the *maamar* emphasizes that due to the greatness of the future redemption relative to the exodus from Egypt, one would (seemingly) not need to mention the exodus from Egypt even in a secondary way]. In contrast, the verse "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, etc." (using the *kaf* of comparison) implies that the primary matter of "wonders" occurred in the exodus from Egypt, and the verse innovates that even in the future redemption there will be wonders like those that occurred in the exodus from Egypt. Even though it is explained in many places that the meaning of "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders" is that the wonders of the future redemption will be wonders even in comparison to the wonders of the days of the exodus from Egypt [as our Sages say on the verse "I will show you wonders": "I will show you what I did not show the forefathers, for the miracles and wonders I am destined to perform for the children are greater than the wonders I performed for the forefathers," and it is similarly stated, "To Him who performs great wonders alone, for His kindness is everlasting"], nevertheless, since the verse says that the wonders of the future redemption will be *as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt*, it implies that the wonders of the future redemption will possess two qualities: they will be extremely great wonders, wonders even in comparison to the wonders of the exodus from Egypt; and a further quality, that the wonders then will be "as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt." The primary innovation and quality is (not that the wonders then will be extremely great, but) that the wonders then will be "as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt."

3) The matter is that there are (generally) two types of miracles: miracles that are above nature [like the miracles of the exodus from Egypt, turning water to blood, etc., up to the miracle of the splitting of the Red Sea, where the sea turned into dry land], and miracles clothed in the ways of nature. The latter have three general modes: 
The garments of nature are such that it is evident they are merely a garment for the miracle clothed within them. This is the case with the miracles of Chanukah and Purim, and similarly all miracles for which one recites the blessing for miracles (a wall fell and one was saved, and the like), where despite being clothed in the ways of nature, the miracle within them is clearly evident. 
Below this are cases where the garments of nature conceal the miracle within them, yet it is still evident that something unusual occurred, such as the four who must offer thanks; for these miracles one does not recite the blessing for miracles, but one brings a thanksgiving offering and recites the *Birchas HaGomel*. 
Below this are cases where the garments of nature completely conceal and hide the miracle, to the point that it is not at all evident that something unusual occurred, as it is written, "To Him who performs great wonders alone," and our Sages said that even the person who experienced the miracle does not recognize his miracle.

We must understand why the *maamar* brings the aforementioned teaching of our Sages on the verse "I will show you wonders" in conjunction with the verse "To Him who performs great wonders alone." After all, in that very teaching, our Sages say that the wonders of the future redemption will be greater than those of the exodus from Egypt, yet the *maamar* cites the verse "To Him who performs great wonders alone," which refers to miracles that are completely hidden, to the point that the person who experienced them does not recognize them. The Tzemach Tzedek explains that the root of miracles clothed in nature is from a more exalted place than the root of miracles that are above nature. The more a miracle is clothed in nature, the more it indicates that its root is from a more exalted place. This is "To Him who performs great wonders alone"—the root of miracles that are completely clothed in nature, such that the person who experienced the miracle does not recognize it, is from the level of "Alone" (*Levado*). This is why the *maamar* brings the aforementioned teaching of our Sages regarding the wonders of the future redemption in conjunction with the verse "To Him who performs great wonders alone"—the wonders of the future redemption will be drawn from the level of "Alone."

Furthermore, based on this, we can also explain why the verse specifies *I will show you* (*Ar'enu*) wonders. The primary innovation is not that there will be wonders, but that Hashem will *show* the wonders. For wonders exist even now, and there even exist great wonders drawn from the level of "Alone." The innovation of the future redemption is that then there will be *Ar'enu*—"I will show you"—wonders. Currently, the miracles drawn from the level of "Alone" are such that only He knows it is a miracle, whereas in the future redemption there will be *Ar'enu* wonders, where the miracle within them will be revealed.

4) The two types of miracles (those above nature and those clothed in nature) each possess a quality. Since in the future all matters will be in absolute perfection, we must say that "I will show you wonders" refers not only to miracles clothed in nature (that in the future the miracle within them will be revealed, as discussed in section 3), but also to miracles that are not clothed in nature. This is also understood from the fact that it says the wonders of the future redemption will be *as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt*, for the miracles of the exodus from Egypt were above nature. Furthermore, miracles are called "wonders" (*nifla'os*) simply because they are a wonder (*pela*) that has no place in nature. From this it is understood that when it says "I will show you wonders," and the *Midrash* states that the wonders of the future redemption will be greater than those of the exodus from Egypt [to the point that they will be *wonders* even in comparison to the wonders of the exodus from Egypt]—the intent is that the "wonder" (above nature) in the miracles of the future redemption will be a wonder even in comparison to the "wonder" of the miracles of the exodus from Egypt. We must understand what is meant by *I will show you* wonders; seemingly, according to the interpretation that "I will show you wonders" refers to miracles above nature (especially since the "above nature" of the future wonders will be even greater than the "above nature" of the exodus miracles), what is the innovation in *showing* the wonders?

5) This can be understood by prefacing what was brought above (section 3), that the root of miracles where the person who experienced them does not recognize them is from the level of "Alone." We must understand how it is possible for the garments of nature to conceal and hide a miracle drawn from the level of "Alone." The matter is that the quality of miracles clothed in nature over miracles above nature is as the Mitteler Rebbe explains in *Shaar HaEmunah*: through the miracle being clothed in nature, it comes into revelation. For miracles not clothed in nature, one knows only their existence, not their essence. Like the miracle of the resurrection of the dead: although one sees in practice that initially he was dead and now he is alive, one does not know or grasp *how* he was resurrected. This is the case for all miracles above nature. Therefore, these miracles are called "wonders," for a "wonder" is something that cannot be grasped by the intellect [to the point that when a person sees a wonder, he is astonished, and astonishment is the nullification of the intellect]. Through the miracle being clothed in nature, it comes into revelation, and one grasps its essence. Like the miracle of Purim, where one knows and grasps *how* the decree was nullified (it was through Esther, etc.). Similarly, the four who must offer thanks, where one knows and grasps how the sick person was healed (it was through medicinal herbs), and the like.

It can be said that this matter [that in miracles clothed in nature, the essence of the miracle is in revelation] applies primarily to miracles where the person who experienced them does not recognize the miracle. Miracles where it is clearly evident that the garments of nature are merely a garment for the miracle, and even miracles where it is not clearly evident that it is a miracle but it is evident that something unusual occurred (like the four who must offer thanks), are in the category of a "wonder" that is not grasped by the intellect [which is why one must *offer thanks* for them, for thanksgiving is the nullification of the intellect]. Miracles where the person who experienced them does not recognize the miracle are clothed in nature to such an extent that they are grasped by the intellect. Based on this, it can be said that the fact that miracles drawn from the level of "Alone" are such that the person who experienced them does not recognize the miracle is because the matters drawn from the level of "Alone" are in revelation everywhere (for it is impossible for there to be anything that could conceal them). Therefore, they are clothed in nature in such a way that the person who experienced the miracle does not recognize it; they contain no "wonder" at all (not even like the wonder of the four who must offer thanks) and are grasped by the intellect.

6) The difference between the two types of miracles in their root is (as explained in *Shaar HaEmunah* there) that miracles clothed in nature have their root in the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "below, below, to the point of no end"—the matter of the expansion and revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, which expands and is revealed down to the lowest depths. Therefore, they come into revelation, to the point that they are grasped even by human intellect. Miracles above nature have their root in the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "above, above, to the point of no end"—the matter of the concealment and ascent of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, which ascends and is concealed in concealment after concealment and elevation after elevation, higher and higher. Therefore, even when they are drawn below, they remain above revelation—"wonders." This is "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders"—that in the future redemption, even the wonders whose root is from the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "above, above, to the point of no end" will be in revelation, and the revelation will be in the manner of sight, *Ar'enu* ("I will show you").

It can be said that in the *Ar'enu* wonders that will be in the future, there are two matters: that even the miracles of the future that will not be clothed in nature [like the splitting of the sea and the river—"Hashem will dry up the tongue of the Egyptian sea and wave His hand over the river, etc."] will be in revelation, similar to the miracle of the splitting of the Red Sea that occurred in the exodus from Egypt. Although it was above nature, they saw in revelation (not only the splitting of the sea, but also) the revelation through which the sea split, as everyone pointed with their finger and said, "This is my G-d and I will glorify Him". The innovation in the *Ar'enu* wonders of the future relative to the revelation of the splitting of the Red Sea is that the wonders of the future will be wonders even in comparison to the wonders of the exodus from Egypt—that is, the wonders of the future will be drawn from a more exalted place than the wonders of the exodus from Egypt [and therefore they will also be, simply put, greater wonders], and yet they will be in revelation and in the manner of sight. Also, the revelation at the splitting of the Red Sea was only for a moment, whereas the revelation of the future (although it will be a more exalted revelation than that of the splitting of the Red Sea) will be in a permanent and eternal manner. Another matter in *Ar'enu* wonders is that because the wonders will be revealed to Israel in the future, their drawing down into the world will also be in a manner of revelation, and therefore they will then be clothed in nature. And yet they will be wonders, because their being clothed in nature is not because they are in the category of revelation [like the miracles currently clothed in nature, whose root is from the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "below, below"—the matter of the expansion and revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof*], but rather, even though their root is from the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "above, above, to the point of no end," a concealment that is above revelation [and furthermore, a concealment that is above even the *category* of revelation, as explained in the *Hemshech* of the *Hillula*, that the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "above, above, etc." means that even in His essence He is not in the category of revelation], they will nevertheless be clothed in nature, because in the future even the concealment (that is above revelation) will be in revelation.

7) It can be said that in the clothing of the wonders in nature that will occur in the future, there are two matters. The fact that wonders above nature will be clothed in nature is because then even the concealment that is above revelation will be drawn into revelation. The fact that nature will not conceal the wonders is because then there will be an elevation of nature, such that nature itself will be a vessel for the wonders that are above nature. Based on this, we can explain the connection between the two interpretations of *Ar'enu* wonders—the interpretation of the Mitteler Rebbe that even wonders above nature will then be seen in revelation (unlike now, where for wonders above nature one knows only their existence), and the interpretation of the Tzemach Tzedek that even in miracles clothed in nature (great wonders drawn from the level of "Alone") the miracle within them will be seen in revelation (as discussed in section 3)—for the content of both interpretations is that in the future, nature will be a vessel for what is above nature, which is why nature will not conceal the miracles clothed within it. However, according to the Tzemach Tzedek, the matter of *Ar'enu* wonders is that nature will not conceal the miracles belonging to nature (that is, the miracles drawn from the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "below, below, etc."—the matter of the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof*). For nature as it is now conceals even these miracles. The root of miracles clothed in nature is in the *revelation* of the *Ohr Ein Sof*, which generally is the Name *Havayah* that is above nature, and the root of nature is from the Name *Elokim* (*Elokim* in *gematria* is *HaTeva*—nature), which conceals and hides the Name *Havayah*. In the future, the Name *Elokim* will be a vessel for the revelation of *Havayah*. According to the Mitteler Rebbe, the matter of *Ar'enu* wonders is that nature, which is drawn from the Name *Elokim*, will be a vessel (also) for the wonders above nature, which are drawn from the fact that the *Ohr Ein Sof* is "above, above, etc."—a concealment that is above revelation (above *Havayah*).

8) Based on what was brought above (from the *maamar*) that in the redemption from Egypt the conduit for the future redemption was also opened, it can be said that in the redemption from Egypt, the empowerment for the matter of *Ar'enu* wonders that will be in the future was also drawn down. The matter is that the fact that in the future nature will be a vessel for what is above nature [the matter of *Ar'enu* wonders] is through the *birurim* (refinements), for through this the world is refined and elevated and becomes a vessel for what is above nature. The beginning of the matter of *birurim* was at the exodus from Egypt. By the fact that "we were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt" [to Pharaoh—a harsh king; in Egypt—a harsh land, from which no slave can escape] and nevertheless they stood the test, through this they refined the sparks that were in Egypt. And to the point that "they emptied out Egypt", making it like a net with no fish and a trap with no grain, and through this, the power was given for the work of *birurim* in all the days after the exodus from Egypt (the plural "days"), including the days during the time of exile.

It can be said that one of the reasons why we remember the exodus from Egypt every day is because through this, the empowerment drawn from the exodus from Egypt for the work of *birurim* is increased every day. And through refining more and more sparks each and every day, we draw closer to the coming of Moshiach, for through every spark that is refined, the world becomes more refined, until at the completion of the *birurim*, the world will be a vessel for the revelation of *Elokus* that is above worlds, which generally means that the nature of the world will be a vessel for what is above nature—*Ar'enu* wonders. Therefore, we will remember the exodus from Egypt even in the days of Moshiach, because the fact that the world will then be a vessel for the revelation of *Elokus* that is above worlds is through the redemption from Egypt. This is "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders"—the fact that it says that *Ar'enu* wonders (in the future) will be *as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt* is because the empowerment for the matter of *Ar'enu* wonders that will be in the future is from the exodus from Egypt. And it specifies "as in the days of your exodus," the plural "days," because in the exodus from Egypt there was only the empowerment for the matter of *Ar'enu* wonders, and for the matter of *Ar'enu* wonders to be drawn into practice, it is through the work of *birurim* in the days between the exodus from Egypt and the future redemption, which are the "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt" (as discussed in section 1 from the *maamar*), the plural "days."

9) It can be said that one of the differences between the verse "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders" and the verse "So that you may remember the day of your exodus from the land of Egypt all the days of your life," from which we learn that we remember the exodus from Egypt even in the days of Moshiach, is that the verse "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders" speaks of the future redemption and says "as in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt" because the empowerment for the *Ar'enu* wonders in the future redemption is from the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt. The verse "So that you may remember, etc." is stated in the *parsha* of "Observe the month of spring and perform the Pesach, etc.", for Pesach is the matter of the exodus from Egypt, and it says "So that you may remember the day of your exodus from the land of Egypt all the days of your life," to include the days of Moshiach, meaning that the intent *in the redemption from Egypt* is that it should be (remembered and) drawn down also in the days that follow, in the days of Moshiach. Based on this, we can explain what is said in the *maamar* that to understand the matter of the plural "days," one must preface the matter of remembering the exodus from Egypt in the future, for the fact that we remember the exodus from Egypt in the days of Moshiach is because this is *the essence of the exodus from Egypt*. Based on this, it is better understood why all the days from the time of the exodus from Egypt until the future redemption [through which the power that was in the exodus from Egypt (for the future redemption) is drawn from potential into practice] are "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," the plural "days."

Based on the above, that the essence of the exodus from Egypt is to bring about the future redemption, it is understood that by remembering the exodus from Egypt every day, and especially by telling the story of the exodus from Egypt on the night of Pesach [at a time when *matzah* and *maror* are placed before you. And at the time when the *Beis HaMikdash* existed—at a time when the Pesach offering, *matzah*, and *maror* were placed before you], we hasten even more the fulfillment of the prophecy "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders," in the future redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, who will come and redeem us with the true and complete redemption, speedily in our days.

## Acharon Shel Pesach 5739

### Acharon Shel Pesach 5739

"As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders." In his maamar, the Rebbe Rayatz notes the precision of the Zohar regarding the use of the plural *kimiy* (as in the days of), whereas the exodus itself took place on a single day ("they left in one moment"). Similarly, regarding the obligation to remember the exodus, the Torah states, "So that you may remember the *day* of your exodus from the land of Egypt" (using the singular *yom*). Why, then, is the plural used here? He explains that all the days from the time of the exodus until the future redemption are "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt," and therefore it says *kimiy* in the plural. It can be said that the statement in the maamar—that all the days from the exodus until the future redemption are days of your exodus—is because *Mitzrayim* (Egypt) comes from the word *meitzar* (constriction) and *gevul* (boundary). The concept of the exodus from Egypt is the departure from all constrictions and boundaries, even from the most sublime constrictions and limitations. This is why all these days are "days of your exodus from the land of Egypt": because every single day, one departs from increasingly sublime constrictions and boundaries, until one arrives at the true and complete redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, at which time the departure will be from all constrictions and limitations, even the most sublime ones. For regarding Mashiach, it is written, "The breaker has gone up before them"—the breaking of all barriers, measurements, and limitations.

2) The maamar continues that "I will show you wonders" refers to the revelation of the future redemption, implying that the future redemption will also be on this level, only in a more sublime manner. It can be said that the reason the maamar continues by stating that "I will show you wonders" refers to the revelation of the future redemption is to explain (more fully) why this verse says *kimiy* (plural), even though the obligation to remember the exodus uses the singular *yom*. Since it is speaking of the future redemption ("I will show you wonders"), when the perfection of the exodus will occur—the departure from all constrictions and limitations (even the most sublime)—and since arriving at this revelation is achieved through the *avodah* of the exodus that occurs every day (where one departs from increasingly sublime constrictions), therefore it says *kimiy* in the plural. For the concept of the exodus that leads to the future redemption is both the departure from Egypt the first time (on one day) [which also relates to the future redemption, as stated in the maamar that the exodus from Egypt inaugurated the entire concept of redemption and opened the channel for all subsequent redemptions, including the future redemption], and the departure from the constrictions and limitations that follow the initial exodus—where every day one departs from more sublime constrictions—until, through all this, one reaches the future redemption, at which time the exodus will be in absolute perfection: the departure *from all* constrictions and limitations.

Thus, the fact that the verse "So that you may remember the *day* of your exodus from the land of Egypt all the days of your life"—from which we learn the obligation to remember the exodus every day (day and night)—uses the singular *yom* is because the concept of the exodus every day is (primarily) the departure from the constrictions of the *sitra achra*, and in a general sense, the departure from the constrictions and limitations that prevent and hinder the study of Torah and the fulfillment of *mitzvos*. This relates to the "day of your exodus," for at the exodus, by virtue of the fact that the Jewish people became servants of Hashem (and not servants of Pharaoh), the power was granted to every Jew that, regarding Torah and *mitzvos*, they would be free from all worldly matters, including the matters of *galus*, because "a document [of debt] precedes [the loan]." But in the verse "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders," it says *kimiy* in the plural, because in order to reach the revelation of the future redemption—"I will show you wonders"—since the revelations of the future redemption will be wonders even in comparison to the revelation of the exodus, there must also be a departure from the constrictions and limitations of *kedushah* itself. And in this very matter—every single day—there is a departure from more sublime constrictions, and therefore it says *kimiy* in the plural.

3) From the above (that the *avodah* of the exodus every day is in an increasingly sublime manner), it is understood that during the time of *galus*, the departure from constrictions and limitations is from more sublime constrictions than the departure from the constrictions that existed during the time of the Beis Hamikdash. And within the time of *galus* itself, in the generations of *ikvesa d'meshicha*, and especially in the final generation, the departure from constrictions becomes even more sublime. For in addition to the fact that every day we refine more and more sparks, thereby drawing closer to the coming of Mashiach, the very matter of the exodus—the departure from constrictions and limitations—is also in a more sublime manner each day. Through the *avodah* of the exodus in these final days before the coming of Mashiach, where the departure from Egypt in these days is from the most sublime constrictions, they will merit, very soon, the future redemption, at which time there will be a departure from all constrictions and limitations.

The explanation of this matter (that the revelation of the future redemption will be primarily through the *avodah* at the end of the time of *galus*) will be understood by first explaining why, in the future redemption, there will be a departure *from all* constrictions and limitations, even from the most sublime ones. The matter is, as explained in the maamar, that all the worlds (even the most sublime worlds) are called *Mitzrayim*, because they are a state of *meitzar* (constriction) and *he'elem* (concealment). The fact that the worlds are called *Mitzrayim* is for two reasons: because they are in a state of limitation (*Mitzrayim* from *meitzar* and *gevul*), and also because their creation is through the concealment of the light. As is known, the concealment of the *Tzimtzum HaRishon* (through which space was made for the existence of the worlds) is a *Tzimtzum* by way of removal, where the light was completely concealed, similar to the *Mitzrayim* of the *sitra achra* (Egypt in its literal sense), which is concealment and hiding. It can be said that this is what the maamar continues with: that in the world there are three aspects. *Olam* as it is commonly understood, which indicates place and time; *Olam* from the word *he'elem* (concealment), the essence of which is hiding; and *Olam* from the word *elem* (youth/strength). The maamar explains the connection between these three definitions: that the concealment and hiding of the world (time and place) is with great intensity. It can be said that the reason it brings here not only the definition of *Olam* as commonly understood (place and time) but also the definitions that *Olam* is from the word *he'elem* and *elem* is because *Olam* as commonly understood—place and time (the concept of limitation)—is an explanation only for the fact that the worlds are *Mitzrayim* in the sense of *meitzar* and *gevul*. To also explain the constriction and concealment in the worlds (to the point that it is similar to the concealment and hiding of Egypt in its literal sense), it brings the definitions that *Olam* is from the word *he'elem* and *elem*, implying that the concealment and hiding of the world is with great intensity.

4) The maamar continues that the creation of the worlds is from the *Ohr HaKav* (the Light of the Line). From the light before the *Tzimtzum*, it was impossible for the worlds to be created (as they are now), because the worlds are in a state of "above and below" [this is the definition of *Olam* as place; the concept of place in spirituality is above and below], and the light before the *Tzimtzum* did not have a beginning or end, above or below. Furthermore, the general light before the *Tzimtzum* is light included within its Source, and light included within the Luminary is in a state of absolute *bitul* to the point that it is not even called "light" at all; consequently, it cannot serve as a source for the worlds, for in order to be a source for something, the source must be in a state of "existence." It is specifically through the *Ohr HaKav*—which, by being drawn through the *Tzimtzum*, is in a state of "existence of light" and possesses above and below—that the creation of the worlds is brought about. It can be said that these two aspects of the *Kav* are similar to the two aforementioned aspects of *Olam*. The "above and below" of the *Kav* is similar to the *gevul* (boundary) of *Olam*, and the fact that the *Kav* is an "existence of light" (not in a state of absolute *bitul*) is similar to the *he'elem* and *hester* (concealment and hiding) of *Olam* (the cause of the "being" within it). Based on the above—that the worlds are called *Mitzrayim* because they are a state of *meitzar* and *he'elem* (these two aspects)—it can be said that the *Kav* is also included in the category of *Mitzrayim*, and the true essence of the exodus from Egypt—the departure from all constrictions and limitations—is through the revelation of the *Atzmus* of the *Ein Sof* that is before the *Tzimtzum*. This is why, in the future redemption, there will be the perfection of the exodus (the departure *from all* constrictions and limitations), because then there will be the revelation of the light before the *Tzimtzum*, and in this itself—the revelation of the light of the *Bli Gvul* (Infinite) that is above the light that relates to the worlds, up to the revelation of the *Atzmus*.

5) The fact that in the future, there will be a departure from all constrictions and limitations applies to all matters. All matters in the future will be incomparably different from how they are now. This applies to the world, which will then be "new heavens and a new earth" (a new world); to the Torah [through which the world was created, "He looked into the Torah and created the world"], for in the future there will be a "new Torah that will go forth from Me"; and to the drawing down and revelation of *Elokus*, for the revelation of *Elokus* in the future will be a new revelation that is incomparably more sublime than the revelation of now. All current revelations are from the externality of *Atik*, while in the future, the revelation will be of the internality of *Atik*. This is why the future redemption will be primarily through the *avodah* at the end of the time of *galus*, because since all matters of the future will be new matters, the *avodah* through which these matters are drawn down must also be a new *avodah*, as follows.

The matter is that during the time of the Beis Hamikdash, when there was a revelation of *Elokus*—"ten miracles were performed for our ancestors in the Beis Hamikdash"—these miracles were visible to the physical eye, to the extent that many of them were visible even to the common people. The *avodah* of fulfilling Torah and *mitzvos* was according to reason and intellect. Even though there was the commandment, "And you shall love... with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your might," nevertheless, since they saw the revelation of *Elokus* tangibly, even the *mesiras nefesh* that existed then had the measurement and limitation of reason and intellect. The same was true during the time of *galus* when the darkness was not as intense. This is not the case in the generation of *ikvesa d'meshicha*, where every day the darkness intensifies, and there are many trials—especially the trial of "not being ashamed before those who mock," and all the more so when the mockers are base and empty people, which is very difficult to endure. Standing firm in these trials is an *avodah* of *mesiras nefesh* that is above measurement and limitation. This is why the *avodah* in the generation of *ikvesa d'meshicha*—and in this generation itself, at the end of the time of *galus*—is more sublime (in this regard) than the *avodah* that existed until now, and in an incomparable way, to the point that it is a *new* *avodah*. For the *avodah* of previous generations had some connection to reason and intellect (the measurement and limitation of the revealed powers), whereas the *avodah* at the end of the time of *galus* is the *avodah* of *mesiras nefesh* that comes from the internality of the soul.

Through the *avodah* during the time of *galus* in general, and especially through the *avodah* at the end of *galus*—where the *avodah* is from the internality of the soul—we draw down the internality of Above, up to the internality of *Atik*. Through this, there will be a renewal also in the Torah ("a new Torah will go forth from Me"), in which the Giver of the Torah will be drawn down in a new way. [It can be said that this matter is also accomplished through the *avodah* at the end of *galus*, for when the *avodah* is with *mesiras nefesh*, then the study of Torah is *lishmah*.] Through this, there is also a renewal in Creation ("He looked into the Torah and created the world"), such that there will be new heavens and a new earth.

May it be the Will that all this happens very soon, that through our deeds and our *avodah* now, we will go very soon to greet Mashiach Tzidkeinu, at which time it will be fulfilled in actual reality: "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders," and we will be redeemed immediately.

## Acharon Shel Pesach 5746

### Acharon Shel Pesach 5746

"And My servant David shall be king over them, and they all shall have one shepherd," etc.. It is well known what the Rebbe, our leader of our generation, said regarding this verse, explaining that it refers to Melech HaMoshiach, who is called by the name David—David Malka Meshicha. The essence of the matter is that in the time to come, Moshiach (David) will be the one shepherd for all, and they will no longer be divided into two kingdoms. For in Israel there were two kingdoms: the kingdom of the House of David and the kingdom of the House of Joseph (Ephraim). The kings of the House of David were from the tribe of Judah, and the kings of the House of Joseph were from the tribe of Joseph. But in the time to come, there will no longer be two kingdoms; rather, there will be one shepherd for all, and this shepherd will be David, from the tribe of Judah.

We must understand: since there is a specific promise for the time to come that there will be one shepherd for all and they will no longer be divided into two kingdoms, it is implied that even then there is a place for the possibility of two kingdoms. What is the logic behind the existence of two kingdoms in the time to come? Furthermore, we must understand what the certainty of the superiority is in the fact that David (from the tribe of Judah) will be king over them and the one shepherd for all. Seemingly, we find a superiority in Joseph over Judah. For in the case of Joseph and Judah themselves, Joseph was king over all his brothers, including Judah, as is explicit in the dreams of Joseph. Moreover, there is a superiority in the kingdom of Joseph, which was over ten tribes, whereas the kingdom of Judah was over only two tribes. We must also understand the content of these matters in the *avodah* of man at this time, especially in light of the well-known principle that all the revelations of the time to come depend on our deeds and our *avodah* throughout the duration of the exile, as the Sages said: "Today is for doing them, and tomorrow is for receiving their reward."

2) The matter is as follows: The difference between Joseph and Judah can be understood from the names (Joseph and Judah) themselves. Judah is from the word *hoda'ah* (acknowledgment/submission), and Joseph is from the word *hosafah* (addition), as is explicit in the verse. In the *kochos hanefesh*, this is the difference between the power of *asiyah* (action) in man and the power of *sechel* (intellect) in him. The power associated with *hoda'ah* [in mankind in general, and certainly in the Jewish people, who are a "part of Elokus from above, truly"] is the power of action. For in order for there to be an act within the power of *asiyah*, there must be *hoda'ah* and *iskafya* (coercion). The concept of *hoda'ah* in its simple sense is that one acknowledges a certain thing even though it is not understood by his intellect, or furthermore, that it is the opposite of his intellect. This concept is within the power of *asiyah* in practice, because when a person performs an action, he lowers his will and his intellect, etc., until he arrives at this action. This descent (in every person, and especially in a Jew, as mentioned above) is a descent to the "deep pit," and therefore he must have *hoda'ah* and *iskafya*, etc. (similar to the descent of the *neshamah* below from the "high roof" to the "deep pit," where this descent is by way of coercion, as the saying goes: "Against your will you live").

However, the concept of *hosafah* (addition) is the opposite of this. For *hosafah* indicates that the matter is not only accepted (settled) in his intellect, but furthermore, that he adds through this in *chochma* and *sechel*, especially when there is addition upon addition. This is the essence of true *chochma* and *sechel*. In the powers of man, this is the power of *binah* within him, which is called "the broad rivers", which is in a manner of "increasing and continuing to increase," giving birth to further matters of *sechel* and explanation; this is the true intent of *pilpul* and *shakla v'tarya*, etc. It is understood that this is the opposite of the concept of *iskafya* and *hoda'ah* (against one's will), because in mankind in general, and especially in a Jew, the intellect is something that is accepted (settled), to the point that he adds to it, etc.

3) These two matters, in the general *avodah* of man, are the two matters (two modes of *avodah*) of Torah study and the performance of *mitzvos*. The essence of Torah is that "the Torah and the *Kudsha Brich Hu* are entirely one", and as it is brought in *Tanya* that through Torah study, the person who studies unites with the Giver of the Torah in a wondrous union, the likes of which does not exist and is not comparable to anything. In every world where one studies Torah, through the study, one reaches the Torah as it is in its truth and its inwardness in this world. It is understood that since through Torah study one reaches this elevation, the person studying has *taanug* (pleasure) in the matter.

However, the concept of the performance of *mitzvos* is specifically in the power of *asiyah*, for the performance of *mitzvos* is with physical things, things that are outside the person himself ("it is not him"). For this reason, there must be *asiyah* from the word *iskafya* (and *hoda'ah*), that he descends from his level, from the "high roof" to the "deep pit"—meaning from the power of *sechel* and *mochin* within him to the power of *asiyah* in the person himself, than which there is nothing lower. As is brought in several *drushim*, the power of *asiyah* exists also in the animal (the category of *chai*), and in this power, the category of *medaber* (man) is equal to the category of *chai*. This descent is through *iskafya* and *hoda'ah*. This is also why in the *mitzvah* of Torah study, according to Torah there must be vitality and *taanug* in Torah study, while the performance of *mitzvos* is primarily through *kabbalas ol* (acceptance of the yoke) specifically, and *ol* primarily pertains to the animal. These are the two matters of Joseph (Torah) and Judah (*mitzvos*).

4) In these two modes of *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos* (study and action), there is a *shakla v'tarya* in the *Gemara* as to which of them is greater. As is brought in the *maamar* there, they disagreed on this: one said study is greater, and one said action is greater. From the language of the *Gemara*, it is understood that when they compared the merits of study and action in and of themselves, they did not decide which of them was greater. For the merit of study is the matter of *taanug* within it (as mentioned above), and there is a merit in action that is not in study: the matter of *iskafya*, which is a novelty—that one does something that is the opposite of his nature and his pleasure. That is, even without the matter of *kabbalas ol malchus shamayim* (which is from above), there is a novelty in the very fact that a person can do something that is the opposite of his nature and his pleasure. Finally, they concluded that "study is greater because study leads to action." This is not because study (*taanug*) in itself is higher than action (*iskafya*), but because study also contains the merit of action, in that it leads to action. For in order to know the action that one must perform, there must be study beforehand. It should be added that in study itself, there is also the merit that is in action, which is *iskafya*. For after one studies Torah with the utmost perfection from the perspective of his *taanug*, his "broad rivers," and his joy, he then reaches a higher level: that he studies from a perspective of *iskafya* and *hoda'ah*, nullifying his entire existence and studying out of *kabbalas ol*.

5) However, all this is only at this time, but in the time to come, "action will be greater". The matter is that in the time to come, it is written, "And I will remove the spirit of impurity from the earth," and therefore the *avodah* then will be only in "ascents from strength to strength" in goodness itself and in *kedushah* itself. The perfection of the elevation is that the *avodah* will be even higher than the level of *taanug*, namely, the *avodah* of Hashem out of *kabbalas ol*, to fulfill the will of the *Ribono shel Olam*. Furthermore, because at this time we are still in the straits and boundaries of *kedushah*, and there are definitions of merits, etc., and we say that there is a merit in action and there is a merit in study. But in the time to come, when there will be an exodus from all straits and boundaries, it is not appropriate to speak of merits, but only of the true *avodah*: that one serves Hashem with *kabbalas ol* without any ulterior motives, not even motives of *kedushah*. The explanation for this in *nigleh* can be based on what the *Rambam* writes, that the perfection of man's *avodah* is the *avodah* from love, and this is the merit of Avraham Avinu, whom the *Ribono shel Olam* called "My lover". This is *avodah* without any ulterior motives, as the *Rambam* writes there that the *avodah* must be even without the motive that I will merit the life of the world to come. This perfection will be in the time to come (in the days of Moshiach). This is why in the time to come, action is greater—the merit of *iskafya* and *kabbalas ol* that is higher than all the powers of man. It can be said that this is hinted at in what the *Rambam* writes, that the *avodah* must be even not in order to merit the life of the world to come, for the matter of the world to come is the matter of Torah (as explained in many places), and the *avodah* in perfection is higher than this.

6) With this, we can understand what is written, "And My servant David shall be king over them, and they all shall have one shepherd," etc., "and they shall no longer be divided into two kingdoms." For even in the time to come, there will be the two kingdoms of Judah and Joseph—that is, the two modes of *avodah* of Torah and *mitzvos*. On the contrary, then there will be the ultimate perfection of Torah study, for then "their work will be done by others", and it will be possible for the occupation of Torah to be in a manner of "their Torah is their profession". The novelty is that nevertheless, "My servant David shall be king over them"—action is greater—that all the *avodos* then (including Torah study) will be in a manner of action and *iskafya*, meaning higher than all straits and boundaries. This is also the precision of "David My servant" specifically: that he will be in the state of a servant, a "faithful servant", the ultimate perfection, the state of *yechidah* to unite You, where there is only the matter of the "Unique One" without any ulterior motives. This continues, as mentioned, even in the Torah study of the future, as mentioned above, that then Torah study will be in a manner of "their Torah is their profession," etc. And until they are no longer divided into two kingdoms (and two camps), as written before this, "Bring them one to the other to you for one stick, and they shall become one in your hand."

7) We stated above that all the revelations of the time to come depend on our deeds and our *avodah*. In this way, we can also understand this matter of "And My servant David shall be king over them," that this is accomplished through the preparation in the *avodah* of man at this time. It can be said that this is what the *Rambam* writes there, that the beginning of *avodah* is "not for its own sake," and from "not for its own sake" one comes to *avodah* for the sake of the life of the world to come, until finally one comes to "for its own sake," to the *avodah* like Avraham Avinu, whom the *Ribono shel Olam* called "My lover." From the fact that this matter is decided as *halacha* in the *Rambam*, it is understood that this matter is required of every single Jew, and one asks according to their strength, for the powers exist in every single Jew to reach this *avodah*. Perhaps it can be said by way of hint that the fact that the above is a preparation for the revelations of the future is hinted at in the fact that the *Rambam* writes this matter in the tenth chapter of *Hilchos Teshuvah*, for it is known that the matter of *teshuvah* is the novelty of the time to come, as the saying goes, "Moshiach will return the righteous in *teshuvah*." From this it is understood that through this *avodah* of "for its own sake" in the ultimate perfection (ten), one comes to the revelation of the time to come, "And My servant David shall be king over them."

8) May it be His will that this shall be speedily in our days, truly, that the prophecy will be fulfilled, "For the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea." This means not only that they will attain the knowledge of their Creator according to the power of man, and in a manner of a wondrous union the likes of which does not exist, etc., but more than this, that they will reach the state of "as the waters cover the sea", the state of a "faithful servant" who is not an existence unto himself at all. And the prophecy will be fulfilled, "And Hashem shall be King over all the earth," and especially in Israel, His close people, where each one of them is like an only child to the *Ribono shel Olam*, connected with the state of the "Unique One" from above, *yechidah* to unite You. And the continuation of the verse will also be fulfilled, "On that day Hashem shall be One and His Name One," as I am written, so I am read, that He will be called (in revelation) by the *Shem HaEtzem*, the *Shem HaMeforash*, and the *Shem HaMeyuchad*, and there will be a revelation of the state of the simple unity from above, for the simplicity from above is connected with the simplicity of the simple man below, in the true and complete redemption, speedily in our days, truly, through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

## Acharon Shel Pesach 5749

"And Hashem shall utterly destroy the tongue of the Egyptian sea; and with His mighty wind shall He shake His hand over the river..." The Rebbe Maharash explains this at length in the *Hemshech* titled *Vehecherim*. He explains that not only will the future redemption involve the "utterly destroying of the tongue of the Egyptian sea"—similar to the splitting of the sea at the Exodus—but there will also be the "shaking of His hand over the river." The splitting of the river is a higher level than the splitting of the sea, as explained there at length. The primary innovation of the future redemption is that it will include the splitting of the river. As it is written, "He turned the sea into dry land; they passed through the river on foot." "He turned the sea into dry land" is in the past tense, referring to the splitting of the sea that occurred at the redemption of the Exodus; "they passed through the river on foot" is in the future tense, referring to the splitting of the river that will occur in the future redemption. This "passing through the river on foot" is the primary innovation of the future redemption.

2) It can be said that the fact that "He shall utterly destroy... the Egyptian sea" is immediately followed by "and He shall shake His hand over the river" is intended to teach that from the splitting of the sea and its associated concepts—which were present in the redemption of the Exodus—it is possible to arrive immediately (without any interruption) at the "shaking of His hand over the river" that will occur in the future. This is especially true on Acharon Shel Pesach, when we read the *Haftorah* concerning the future redemption and partake in the *Seudas Moshiach*. This follows in direct continuation of the seventh day of Pesach, the splitting of the sea, whereby the connection between the splitting of the sea and the revelation of the future redemption is strengthened even further. Since this proximity is part of the Torah, which is an inheritance for every Jew, as it is written, "The Torah that Moshe commanded us is an inheritance for the congregation of Yaakov," every Jew—men, women, and children—possesses the power to move from the splitting of the sea immediately and literally to the future redemption, without a delay of even the blink of an eye. That every Jew has the power to arrive immediately at the revelation of the future redemption is also emphasized in the *Haftorah* of Acharon Shel Pesach: "And the spirit of Hashem shall rest upon him..." This refers to Moshiach ("And a shoot shall come forth from the stem of Yishai..."). Nevertheless, it was ordained that every Jew should recite on the *Yom Tov* (in the prayer said when opening the *Aron Kodesh*), "May the verse that is written be fulfilled in us: 'And the spirit of Hashem shall rest upon him...'" This means that in every individual Jew, there will be this revelation, and in the manner of "the spirit of Hashem shall rest upon him," with all the details mentioned in these verses, including "and He shall shake His hand over the river."

3) This matter depends solely upon our actions and our *avodah*. Through the performance of a *mitzvah* as it ought to be done, a redemption is effected—the personal redemption of the one performing the *mitzvah*—placing him in a state of true freedom, as is understood from what is explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh* regarding the interpretation of the verse, "He has redeemed my soul in peace."

It should be added that the personal redemption ("He has redeemed my soul in peace") achieved through the performance of a *mitzvah* as it ought to be done is not limited to the time of the *mitzvah's* performance; rather, it continues afterward. This is similar to what is explained in *Tanya* regarding the *yichud* (unification) effected by the performance of a *mitzvah*, which is eternal on high. This *yichud* on high also impacts the person below. Similarly, the "redeeming of my soul in peace" effected by the performance of a *mitzvah* is an eternal matter both on high and below [similar to the saying of our Sages, "Whoever engages in Torah brings peace to the heavenly entourage and to the earthly entourage"]. The personal redemption achieved through the performance of the *mitzvah* continues and remains active throughout all the days of one's life, in one's daily life. From this, one also arrives at the general redemption, "And Hashem shall be King" over the entire existence, both on high and below.

4) Furthermore, beyond the fact that all matters of Torah and *mitzvos* (our actions and *avodah*) draw near and bring the future redemption, there is a special *segulah* in those matters that are more openly associated with Moshiach. Among these is the eating of the *Seudas Moshiach* on Acharon Shel Pesach and the drinking of the four cups at this meal. Just as the four cups on the first nights of Pesach correspond to the four expressions of redemption (the redemption of the Exodus), so too are the four cups of the *Seudas Moshiach* on Acharon Shel Pesach associated with the future redemption. One might explain this based on the commentaries regarding the connection of the future redemption to the four cups—the four expressions of redemption for the future, for the future redemption will be "from the four corners of the earth." Perhaps it can be added that the fact that the *Seudas Moshiach* has the same number of cups as the first nights of Pesach is to hint that every Jew has the power to move immediately from the redemption of the Exodus (the four cups of the first nights of Pesach) to the future redemption (the four cups of Acharon Shel Pesach)—a redemption in a simple, literal sense, below ten *tefachim*. Especially since our actions and *avodah* now are like a dwarf on the shoulders of a giant, because they come after and in continuation of the actions and *avodah* of our Rebbes, the *Nesi'im*: the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, the Tzemach Tzedek, the Rebbe Maharash, the Rebbe Rashab, and the Rebbe Rayatz. Therefore, it is certainly and absolutely within the power of our actions and *avodah*, including the eating of the *Seudas Moshiach* and the drinking of the four cups on Acharon Shel Pesach, to bring the redemption immediately, literally in the blink of an eye.

5) May it be Hashem's will that all of this be in actual revelation, in a manner where "each one points with his finger and says, 'This!'"—similar to the *Seudas Moshiach* where one points with the finger at the *matzah* and the wine, and internally, similar to the *matzah* and the cups of the *Seudas Moshiach* which become blood and flesh of one's own flesh—with joy and a glad heart, and immediately, literally, without a delay of even the blink of an eye.

## Shabbos Parshas Acharei, Mevarchim HaChodesh Iyar, 5733

All of Israel has a portion in the World to Come. The meaning of "World to Come" in this Mishnah is the Era of Resurrection, as is proven by the continuation of the Mishnah: "And these are the ones who do not have a portion in the World to Come: one who says that the resurrection of the dead is not from the Torah." The reason for this is as stated in the Gemara: he denied the resurrection of the dead, therefore he will not have a portion in the resurrection of the dead—measure for measure. Now, "all of Israel has a portion in the World to Come." Regarding Gan Eden, it is written, "Who shall ascend the mountain of Hashem... he who has clean hands and a pure heart," implying that there are various conditions required to ascend to Gan Eden, even to the lower Gan Eden. This is not the case regarding the Era of Resurrection, for all of Israel has a portion in the World to Come. One must understand: the revelation that will exist in the Era of Resurrection is far loftier than the revelation of Gan Eden—loftier even than the revelation in the Upper Gan Eden, and even loftier than the highest level in Gan Eden [and, needless to say, loftier than the matter of resurrection itself, for all of Israel will rise to life, including souls that have been in Gan Eden for thousands of years, and who ascend to a loftier level three times every day; yet, in the Time to Come, they will be clothed in bodies, because the revelation of the Era of Resurrection is far loftier than the revelation of Gan Eden, even than the highest level of Gan Eden]. And yet, to merit the revelation of Gan Eden, there must be various conditions, whereas the revelation of the Era of Resurrection will be for all of Israel.

2) This can be understood by first explaining the matter of why the revelation of the Era of Resurrection is far loftier than the revelation of Gan Eden. Seemingly, since Gan Eden is the world of souls (souls without bodies), and in the Era of Resurrection there will be souls in bodies, what is the explanation for why there will be a loftier revelation in the Era of Resurrection, when the soul is clothed in a physical body, than in Gan Eden? Even though in the Time to Come the body will be in a state of ultimate refinement—like the body of Adam HaRishon (the handiwork of the Holy One, blessed be He), which dimmed the orb of the sun, and even more so, for in the Time to Come the body will be in a state of ultimate perfection even greater than that of Adam HaRishon—this is seemingly an explanation only for why, in the Time to Come, souls clothed in bodies will be vessels capable of receiving a revelation loftier than the revelation currently revealed to souls without bodies. But this does not explain why the revelation of the Time to Come will be specifically to souls in bodies.
