Most Tannaim were primarily occupied with the *nigleh* of the Torah, as "masters of Kabbalah" were few, and Rabbi Yehuda’s entire study was *Nezikin* (Damages), for this part of the Torah is clothed in the most lowly matters of this physical world (matters of damages, false claims, etc.), in order to refine the sparks within these matters as well. Those whose primary occupation was the *nistar* of the Torah, *Maaseh Merkavah*, did not engage in the *nigleh* as much. For example, Rabbi Nechunya ben HaKaneh, who authored *Sefer HaKaneh*; very little is cited from him in the *nigleh* of the Torah. Rashbi, however, in addition to his occupation with the inner dimension of the Torah being on a much higher level, drew the light of the inner dimension into the *nigleh* as well, as is known regarding the matter of "I have struck and I will heal," that through Rashbi, the partition between the *galya* (revealed) of the Torah and the *satim* (hidden) of the Torah was healed and nullified, to the point that the revealed and the hidden are one.

c) Based on this, it is understood that the superiority of Rashbi over all the Tannaim is an incomparable superiority, and therefore, to arouse the influence from him, they needed to praise him. The superiority of *yechidah* over *nefesh-ruach-neshamah* is an incomparable superiority, as is understood from the fact that it is called *yechidah* (singular), for the title "singular" indicates that there is no second to it. Even though *yechidah* is also a created (spark), and all five levels (including *yechidah*) belong to the *nefesh* that is clothed in the body—in the manner of the statement, "Five names are called to it (the soul in the body)"—nevertheless, the essence of *yechidah* is that it receives from the level of the "Singular One," a created spark whose essence is that the spark of the Creator is clothed within it. From this, it is understood *a fortiori* regarding the general *yechidah* (of the entirety of the *hishtalshelus*, and certainly) of the Torah, which is incomparably higher even than the "soul of the Torah," and certainly than the "body of the Torah"—the *halachos* of the Torah, which are called the "bodies of the Torah." Therefore, in order to arouse the influence of Rashbi (the level of *eitan*, *yechidah*), and especially the level of the inner dimension within it (the concealment that is for its own sake), so that it be drawn into the study of the Torah (*nigleh* of the Torah, even *Nezikin*) of the Tannaim—whose study was in the manner of clothing to perform *birurim*—they needed to praise him, for to arouse incomparable influence, one must precede it with praises, as mentioned above.

d) But one must understand: since Rashbi was incomparably separated from the other Tannaim, how could their praises affect him, to the point of arousing and innovating influence for them? And it is even more difficult to understand from the matter of the praises offered to the Creator to arouse the will to bestow influence upon created beings; the arousal of the will is in the *Atzmus* (Essence) of *Ohr Ein Sof* which is above *hishtalshelus* (as is understood from what is explained in many places that through praises one arouses the will to be drawn down and clothed in the *Sefiros*). How is it possible that our praises take place in the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*? The matter is known: the fact that the elevation of *MAN* (feminine waters) by created beings (including the matter of praises) arouses the influence is because it has ascended in His will, blessed be He, that the elevation of *MAN* by created beings should take place before Him, blessed be He. Furthermore, through the elevation of *MAN* by created beings, there is, as it were, a change in the light, so that it is drawn down below to a place that is entirely incomparable, even though, in its own right, it is separated from it. The explanation for this is that since the Holy One, blessed be He, desired to have a dwelling place in the lower worlds, and the matter of "desire" is above reason—as the Alter Rebbe said, "For a desire, there is no question"—and since the desire and *taanug* (delight) from above is for a dwelling place in the lower worlds, in the lowest of the low, therefore the elevation of *MAN* by created beings acts upon the light. Seemingly, one should add that this is also why the majority of the praises (in the arrangement of the praise of the Place in *Pesukei D’Zimra* before prayer) are regarding His influence upon created beings, because [in addition to the fact that the praise must be of the same nature as the influence one wishes to arouse, and to arouse influence below, the praises must be regarding this matter] by praising before Him, blessed be He, His influence upon the lower created beings, one thereby arouses and reveals the delight in a dwelling place in the lower worlds.

Likewise, it is the same regarding the praises of Rashbi. The fact that through the praises they aroused his influence (the level of *eitan*, *yechidah*) to be drawn into the study of the Torah of the Tannaim—whose study was to perform *birurim*—is due to the advantage inherent in the matter of *birurim* (over the matter of revelations), for the will and delight from above are specifically in the matter of *birurim*. This is what is meant by "And the *eitanim* (anagram of *Tannaim*) are the foundations of the earth," *eretz* (earth) being from the word *ratzon* (will); and more than that, *eretz* also relates to *teshuka* (yearning), as it is written, "You have visited the earth and yearned for it," for the will, yearning, and delight from above are specifically in the "earth," in the refinement of physical matters, and especially in the refinement of the matters of *Nezikin*, which is accomplished by the *eitanim* who are the foundations of the earth. And through the drawing down of the light of the inner dimension of the Torah, which is above the matter of *birurim*—the Torah of Rashbi—to the *eitanim* who are the foundations of the earth (whereby they are also drawn additional strength in the matter of *birurim*), there is created an advantage in the light, for the advantage of light is from (the refinement of) darkness. Furthermore, through the drawing down of the influence of Rashbi (light that is above the matter of *birurim*) to the *eitanim* who are the foundations of the earth (the matter of *birurim*), there are two advantages: the advantage of *ohr yashar* (direct light) and the advantage of *ohr chozer* (returning light), analogous to "bringing the righteous to repentance," the advantage of the righteous and the advantage of the *baalei teshuvah* (penitents).

e) One can connect this with the statement in the Zohar: "Once, the world needed rain. Rabbi Yissa, Rabbi Chizkiyah, and the other colleagues came before Rashbi, and through his expounding to them the verse, 'Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brothers to dwell together,' rain descended." It is explained in the discourses of the Tzemach Tzedek and the Rebbe Maharash that even though Rashbi drew down the descent of rain through the utterance of Torah in the manner of "from above to below," nevertheless, he also required the elevation of *MAN* by the colleagues and the praises they offered him, through which they acted upon Rashbi with an advantage, analogous to "from the students more than all of them." It can be said that the connection of this matter to the descent of rain is because regarding rain it is written, "For He had not caused it to rain... and there was no man to work the earth," for the descent of rain is through the work of man, and especially through the work of *teshuvah*, as our Sages said regarding the verse, "My doctrine shall drop as the rain... the creatures broke their necks, and immediately rain descended." And in the descent of rain through the utterance of Torah by Rashbi (from above to below) and the elevation of *MAN* by his students (from below to above), there were two advantages: the advantage of the righteous and the advantage of the *baalei teshuvah*.

Seemingly, one should add that the drawing down of the influence of Rashbi (the level of *eitan*, *yechidah*) to the *eitanim* who are the foundations of the earth for the sake of the matter of *birurim* is analogous to what is explained elsewhere regarding the *yechidah* in the soul: the fact that the *yechidah* spreads into the "four cubits" of a person—which is why "a person’s four cubits acquire for him in every place," even without his knowledge—is specifically to acquire an object placed within them, the acquisition of a physical thing (which is outside the person) into the person’s domain, in order to refine and purify it by using it for the sake of Heaven, for the refinement of physical matters specifically reaches the essence. Likewise, it is the same regarding the *yechidah* of the Torah (the Torah of Rashbi), which is aroused and drawn down for the sake of the matter of *birurim*.

f) Based on all the above, one can also understand that the praises with which they praised Rashbi (which are brought in the maamar) in order to arouse the influence—"Rabbi Yehuda would call him 'Shabbos'" and "Who is 'the face of the Master, Havayah'? This is Rashbi"—are because in these praises is hinted the drawing down of the Supernal (which is incomparably separated) to below, and furthermore, that the drawing down is through the work of the lower being. For regarding Shabbos it is written, "For it is holy," *kodesh* (holy) is a term in itself, meaning that Shabbos is separated and incomparable to the six days of work, and nevertheless, influence is drawn from the holiness of Shabbos into the days before it and after it—from the fourth day onwards it is "before Shabbos," and until the fourth day it is "after Shabbos"—in addition to the fact that "from Shabbos all the days are blessed." It is the same regarding man: even though Shabbos is "holy and established," and since the holiness of Shabbos is a most lofty holiness, *kodesh* in itself (as mentioned above), there is no room for human grasp in it, nevertheless, the power was given to Israel to add to the holiness of Shabbos, "Remember the day of Shabbos to sanctify it," to the point that Israel also adds to the delight of Shabbos, in the language of our Sages, "Whoever delights the Shabbos."

Likewise, it is the same regarding the matter of "Who is 'the face of the Master, Havayah'?" For *Havayah* is the Name of the Essence, the Explicit Name and the Unique Name, whereas the fact that He, blessed be He, is called by the title *Adon* (Master) is as our Sages said, "It is fitting for You to be called *Adon*, for You are Master over all Your creatures," for in addition to the fact that the name *Adon* is in the *Sefirah* of *Malchus*, and in *Malchus* itself—as it descends into *Beriah* to bring the worlds into being and to be master over them—the language of our Sages, "You are Master over all Your creatures" (specifically "Your creatures"), implies that the name *Adon* is over these created beings which are merely "creatures" [analogous to a master in the simple sense, where the title "master" is specifically over slaves]. The matter of "the face of the Master, Havayah" is the unification of *Havayah* (the Name of the Essence) with the level of *Adon* (Master over all Your creatures). This is also why in the commandment of *Reiyah* (appearing before Hashem) it is written, "All your males shall appear before the face of the Master, Havayah." Our Sages said, "Just as he comes to appear, so he comes to see," for in addition to the fact that at the time of the pilgrimage they would see Elokus tangibly (for seeing is above comprehension) and the seeing was also in the level of the inner dimension of Elokus, as it is written, "He shall see... the face of...", furthermore, the person’s seeing of Elokus (from below to above) was in the same manner as the seeing from above: "Just as he comes to appear, so he comes to see," "They shall see His face directly," that the seeing from below and the seeing from above are direct, in equality. Therefore it is said regarding this, "He shall see... the face of the Master, Havayah," for in order for there to be the unification of the seeing from below with the seeing from above, it is through the drawing down of the level of the "Master, Havayah," the unification of *Adon* with *Havayah*.

One should add that the commandment of *Reiyah* also includes the bringing of a sacrifice (*Olas Reiyah*), as it is written, "And they shall not appear before Me empty-handed." In this, the greatness of the superiority of the work of *birurim* is seen: that through the bringing of a physical sacrifice [and according to one opinion, the sacrifice must be not from the fowl but from the cattle], specifically through this, "they shall see My face," a revelation of the inner dimension of Elokus in the manner of seeing, and even to the level of "they shall see His face directly."

g) Based on all the above, one can also understand the connection of the passing of Rashbi with the counting of the *Omer*, to the point that the day of his passing is called (according to the custom of Israel, which is Torah) by the name "Lag B’Omer." The matter is that the work of the counting of the *Omer* is the refinement of the *midos* (attributes) of the animal soul, and nevertheless (and on the contrary, because of this), specifically in this commandment we say, "And through this, abundant influence will be bestowed upon all the worlds," for the work of *birurim* specifically reaches very high, and draws down from there influence—not light which is merely an illumination, but influence which is the essence of the thing—and furthermore, "abundant influence," and because it is abundant influence, therefore (because of the abundance within it) it is drawn and bestowed upon all the worlds, down to this lowest world, and within this world itself, to the lowest place, than which there is none lower. It can be said that this is the connection of the days of the *Sefirah* [and within them, the day of Lag B’Omer, the end and completion of the work of the counting of the *Omer* in the essential *midos*] to the passing of Rashbi, for the work of Rashbi was to heal and nullify the partitions between all matters, and to draw down from the highest heights to the lowest depths, from the level of *eitan* into the level of *eretz*. And this matter stands in revelation on the day of his passing on Lag B’Omer, as explained in the *Iggeret HaKodesh* of the Alter Rebbe, that on the day of passing, all the work of man that he performed during the days of his life stands in revelation as it is in its root and source, as explained at length in the discourses of Lag B’Omer in the *Siddur* of the Mitteler Rebbe.

This matter is drawn down to the lowest depths, even to simple joy. And from this is drawn down the greatest joy, even to "joy that breaks through boundaries," the joy of the world, which breaks through the boundaries of *galus* (exile) and brings the true and complete *Geulah* through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, for Moshiach comes to "bring the righteous to repentance." And this is also connected with the matter of "spreading the wellsprings": "When will the Master come? When your wellsprings spread outward," speedily in our days, truly, and in our time.

## B’ezras Hashem. Shabbos Parshas Emor, 19 Iyar, 5734

Rabbi Shimon began and said, "I am for my Beloved, and His desire is upon me". "All the days that I have been bound in this world, I have been bound in one bond to the Holy One, blessed be He; and because of this, now, 'His desire is upon me'". And in the continuation of that passage, "I would look every day at this verse that is written, 'In Hashem my soul shall boast,' etc. And now, the entire verse is fulfilled, etc., for my soul is bound in Him, it is aflame in Him, it is attached to Him, etc." This means that throughout all the days of his life ("all the days that I have been bound in this world"), Rashbi was connected to the Holy One, blessed be He (*etkatarna* is a term for connection, as in "And you shall bind as a sign upon your hand," etc., which is translated as "And you shall bind upon your hand a scarlet thread," etc.), for connection is specifically through two things. And "now"—on the day of his passing—he reached a more elevated level, becoming one with the Holy One, blessed be He; in Him he is *attached*.

And although Rashbi reached the level of "in Him he is attached" (only) on the day of his passing, since *all his days* he strove to reach this level ("I would look *every day* at this verse"), it is understood that in a general sense, this was relevant to him and he reached it even during his lifetime, except that the perfection of this ("the entire verse") was fulfilled in him "now," on the day of his passing. This is also understood from what is explained in the *Hemshech* of 5666, that the fact that Rashbi’s Torah study was in the manner of "his Torah is his craft"—meaning his Torah study was in the manner of drawing down *mayin duchrin* (unlike other Tannaim, whose study was in the manner of elevating *mayin nukvin*)—is the concept of ("in Him he is attached") "it is aflame in Him." And this fact, that Rashbi’s Torah was his craft, existed throughout all the days of his life. It should be added that the fact that in many discourses the concept of "my soul is bound in Him," etc., is brought in one sequence with "I have been bound in one bond," is because even in the matter of "I have been bound in one bond," there is, in its inwardness, "in Him he is attached," etc. However, throughout all the days of his life, this ("in Him he is attached," etc.) was not in a state of revelation. On the day of his passing, when the inwardness (the root and source) of all the matters of a person’s *avodah* that he performed throughout his life is revealed, then the inwardness of "I have been bound in one bond" is revealed, which is (not only connection, but also) "in Him he is *attached*."

2) This will be understood by prefacing the well-known principle that *hiskashrus* (connection) is higher than *dveikus* (attachment). *Dveikus* is in the externality, as in "As the belt clings to the waist of a man", where the *dveikus* of the belt to the man is only to his externality. *Hiskashrus* is in the inwardness, as in "And his soul is bound to his soul", which reaches into his inwardness. This is the difference between *korbanos* and *ketores*: the *avodah* of *korbanos* is from the perspective that Yisroel are *dveikim* (attached) to the Holy One, blessed be He [as is found in the Midrash and in the Zohar], and therefore they would offer the *korbanos* on the outer altar, which in the human soul is the externality of the heart. *Ketores* is a term for *kesher* (bond/connection) [for every *kesher* in the verse is translated as *katar*], and therefore the *ketores* is on the inner altar, which in the human soul is the inwardness of the heart. The difference between the *avodah* of the externality of the heart and the *avodah* of the inwardness of the heart is that the *avodah* of the externality of the heart is according to *taam v'daas* (reason and intellect), for in love that is according to reason and intellect, changes are possible. The *avodah* of the inwardness of the heart is above reason and intellect, where change is not applicable. This is why *korbanos* is a term for *kirev* (drawing near) and *ketores* is a term for *hiskashrus* (connection); for *kirev* indicates that it is a separate existence, only that it is close to *Elokus*, but it is possible for it to become distant, *chas v'shalom*. *Hiskashrus* (*ketores*) is an enduring bond, for the true nature of an enduring bond is that change is not applicable to it.

3) This difference between *dveikus* and *hiskashrus* concerns the *avodah* from below to above. For *korbanos* and *ketores* are both the pillar of *avodah*, which is an elevation from below to above. The difference between *dveikus* and *hiskashrus* regarding the *hemshech* (drawing down) from above to below (Torah and *gemilus chasadim*) is as it is in the Zohar: "How beloved are Yisroel to the Holy One, blessed be He, etc., and He desired to be *dveik* (attached) to them and to be *miskasher* (connected) with them." The Mitteler Rebbe explains that "to be *dveik* to them" is through the *mitzvos*, for the *hemshech* through the *mitzvos* is in the category of *makif* (encompassing), as it is written, "And His right hand embraces me," for the light drawn down through the *mitzvos* embraces the person who performs the *mitzvos*. An embrace is from the outside, and therefore it says "to be *dveik* to them," for *dveikus* is an external connection. "And to be *miskasher* with them" is through the Torah, for the *hemshech* through the Torah is in the inwardness, as it is written, "And Your Torah is within my inward parts." Therefore it says "to be *miskasher* with them," for *hiskashrus* is an internal connection. The connection of the Holy One, blessed be He, with Yisroel through the Torah is called *hiskashrus*, as the saying goes, "Three bonds (*kesharim*) are connected one to another: the Holy One, blessed be He, the Torah, and Yisroel."

4) In the *dveikus* of Yisroel to the Holy One, blessed be He [from which they have the power to offer *korbanos*], there are two aspects. In the Midrash it is stated that the reason the Holy One, blessed be He, commanded Yisroel to offer *korbanos* is because "they are attached to Me," as it is written, "For as the belt clings to the waist of a man," etc. In the Zohar it is stated, "Worthy are Yisroel, for the Holy One, blessed be He, called them 'Adam,' etc., 'Adam, when he offers from among you,' etc.. Why did He call them 'Adam'? Because it is written, 'And you who are attached (*dveikim*) to Hashem your G-d,' etc." It is explained in the *Hemshech* of 5666 that the Midrash speaks of the *dveikus* of *keilim* (vessels), for the *keilim* are in the category of existence, and their union (*dveikus*) is not an essential union; therefore it brings the verse "As the belt clings," etc., for the belt is an existence unto itself, only that it is attached to the man. The Zohar speaks of the *dveikus* of *oros* (lights), for the *dveikus* and union of the *oros* is an essential union; therefore it brings the verse "And you who are attached to Hashem your G-d," a true *dveikus*. It can be said that the reason the *dveikus* of *oros* is also called by the name *dveikus*—which is in the externality (as above)—is because even the light is an extension and revelation outside of the essence.

According to the well-known principle that Torah and *mitzvos* are (analogous to) *oros* and *keilim*, it can be said that the connection through Torah study (as the Torah is in the category of extension and revelation) is also included in the concept of *dveikus*. The concept of *hiskashrus* through Torah study [that Yisroel *are connected* to the Torah, and the Torah to the Holy One, blessed be He] is from the perspective of the essence of the Torah, which is above extension and revelation. This will be understood according to the well-known principle that in the *hiskashrus* of Yisroel, the Torah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, there are two modes. From the perspective of the Torah as it is drawn down in *Seder Hishtalshelus*—since the order of *Hishtalshelus* is that the *externality* of the higher becomes the *inwardness* of the lower—the mode of the *hiskashrus* of Yisroel to the Torah is that the *satim* (inwardness) of Yisroel connects to the *galya* (externality) of the Torah. Likewise, it is in the *hiskashrus* of the Torah to the Holy One, blessed be He, where the *satim* of the Torah connects to the *galya* of the Holy One, blessed be He. In this mode, the connection of the *galya* of Yisroel with the *satim* of the Holy One, blessed be He, is through several intermediaries (the *satim* of Yisroel, the *galya* of the Torah, the *satim* of the Torah, and the *galya* of the Holy One, blessed be He). But from the perspective of the essence of the Torah, as it is above *Hishtalshelus*, the *hiskashrus* of Yisroel to the Holy One, blessed be He, is such that even the *galya* of Yisroel connects to the *satim* of the Holy One, blessed be He, without intermediaries. And even when one studies the *galya* of the Torah with his understanding and comprehension, he is connected (even the *galya* of his soul, down to his understanding and comprehension) also with the *satim* of the Torah, and through it—with the *satim* of the Holy One, blessed be He.

It can be said that the *hiskashrus* of Yisroel and the Torah that is from the perspective of the level of the Torah as it is drawn down in *Hishtalshelus* is included in the concept of *dveikus*, which is in the externality. For the *hiskashrus* of Yisroel to the Torah (from this level) is in the externality (*galya*) of the Torah; and furthermore, even the inwardness in this level is as it has been drawn down in *Hishtalshelus* to be an intermediary. The expression "three *bonds* (*kesharim*) are *connected* one to another" is (primarily) regarding the *hiskashrus* that is from the perspective of the essence of the Torah, which is above *Hishtalshelus*, for this *hiskashrus* is in the *satim* of the Torah and the *satim* of the Holy One, blessed be He—the inwardness.

5) Even in the *hiskashrus* that is from the perspective of the essence of the Torah, Yisroel and the Torah, as well as the Torah and the Holy One, blessed be He, are like two things that are connected to one another. This is not the case with the fact that Yisroel, the Torah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, are *all one*; this is higher than the *hiskashrus* of Yisroel, the Torah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, as it is from the perspective of the essence of the Torah that is above *Hishtalshelus*. This will be understood by prefacing the precision in what they said, "Three bonds are connected one to another," for seemingly there are only two bonds: that Yisroel are connected to the Torah, and the Torah to the Holy One, blessed be He. One of the explanations for this is that the third bond is the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He, not through the mediation of the Torah, because the root of Yisroel is in the *Atzmus* (Essence), which is above the root of the Torah. The order of these three bonds is like a ring (a circle) that has no beginning or end. At first, Yisroel are connected to the Torah, and the Torah to the Holy One, blessed be He, and afterwards, the bond of the Holy One, blessed be He, and Yisroel. It can be said that after Yisroel are connected to the Torah and through it to the Holy One, blessed be He, the root of Yisroel—which is above the root of the Torah—is revealed, [which is] the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He. And after this bond (of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He) is formed, then at first is the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He, and after the root of Yisroel is revealed in His *Atzmus*, they draw this into the Torah as well, similar to how Dovid would connect the Torah from above to the Holy One, blessed be He. For in addition to the fact that the Torah is the wisdom of the Holy One, blessed be He—and wisdom is incomparable to the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof*—Yisroel draw the *Atzmus* of *Ohr Ein Sof* into it. Since even in the root of the Torah and Yisroel as they are in the *Atzmus*, the root of Yisroel is higher than the root of the Torah, therefore through Yisroel's Torah study, the *Atzmus* itself is drawn into it.

Based on this, it can be said that the fact that Yisroel, the Torah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, are *all one* (that they are not only connected to each other, but are truly one) is (primarily) in Yisroel. Regarding the Torah, since the Torah is for the sake of Yisroel ("Speak to the Children of Yisroel," "Command the Children of Yisroel")—meaning that it is *analogous* to an intermediary, it is like an existence that is connected and bound to the Holy One, blessed be He. This is not the case with Yisroel, who are rooted in the *Atzmus* itself. The fact that Yisroel are rooted in the *Atzmus* does not mean that something else is rooted in it, *chas v'shalom*, but rather that this (the root of Yisroel) is one with the *Atzmus*. And through Yisroel drawing the *Atzmus* into the Torah, the Torah also becomes one with the *Atzmus*; Yisroel, the Torah, and the Holy One, blessed be He, are *all one*, truly one.

6) Based on all the above, it can be explained that the two aspects in Rashbi—"I have been bound in one bond" and "in Him he is attached," etc.—are two modes in his Torah study (the primary matter of Rashbi, "his Torah is his craft"). This will be understood according to the well-known principle that the fact that even through the study of the *nigleh* (revealed) aspect of Torah, the connection of Yisroel to the essence of the Torah and the Holy One, blessed be He, is achieved (as in section 4), is in a state of concealment; the revelation of the essence (of the Torah and the Holy One, blessed be He) is through the study of the *pnimiyus* (inwardness) of the Torah. Since Rashbi’s study was in such a manner that even in his study of the *nigleh*, the *pnimiyus* shone, therefore his connection to the essence of the Torah and the Holy One, blessed be He, even in his study of the *nigleh*, was in a state of revelation. This is what he meant by "All the days that I have been bound in this world, I have been bound in one bond to the Holy One, blessed be He," that even his study of the *nigleh* (which was the majority of his study) [and similarly all his affairs, which were permeated with Torah, since his Torah was his craft] was in the manner of a connection higher than *dveikus*: "I have been bound in *one* bond." Furthermore, through this he also reached the third bond: the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He, which is from the perspective of the essence of the soul as it is connected with the *Atzmus*. This is the concept of "*in one* bond I have been bound," for the connection that is from the perspective of *yechidah* (the essence of the soul) is higher than the division of two lines, as explained elsewhere, that in the level of *yechidah*, both are one thing: *one* bond. For in the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He, through the Torah—since the Torah is like an intermediary and is not truly one with the *Atzmus*—therefore, even the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He, through the Torah (even the bond as it is from the perspective of the essence of the Torah, which is above *Hishtalshelus*) is analogous to the connection of two things that are connected. Consequently, the connection is in a manner that has a desire for *Elokus* (an existence of desire, which is divided into two lines). The *true* concept of "I have been bound in one bond"—one bond—is in the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He (which is higher than their bond through the mediation of the Torah), from the perspective that the root of Yisroel is in the *Atzmus*.

Based on all the above, it can be said that the fact that Rashbi reached the perfection of "in Him he is attached" specifically on the day of his passing is because throughout all the days of his life, his primary matter was Torah study ("his Torah is his craft"). Therefore, even in the matter of "*in one* bond I have been bound" (which is from the perspective of the essence of the soul), the concept of connection (analogous to the connection of two things that are connected) was felt. On the day of his passing, the bond of Yisroel and the Holy One, blessed be He, which is from the perspective of the essence of the soul, shone for him in revelation (and in perfection): "My soul is attached to Him".

7) Although this "I have been bound in one bond," and certainly "in Him he is attached," is an extremely elevated level, nevertheless, through the fact that Rabbi Shimon opened and said, "I have been bound in one bond," etc.—for the meaning of "opened" is that he opened the channel—the power is given to every single Yisroel that there should be in him a small measure and a trace of this matter: that his Torah study should be in a manner where his soul is bound to the Torah with all his inwardness. Even though there are times when he is forced to interrupt his Torah study, nevertheless, at the time of study, it (the Torah study) should be his entire existence, until he is one with the Torah and with the Giver of the Torah, truly one. The empowerment for this in a greater way is through the study of the *pnimiyus* of the Torah, the Torah of Rashbi, for through this there is the power (even more so) to connect and unite with the Torah and the Giver of the Torah. And through the study and dissemination of the Torah of Rashbi—which in these generations has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus—we also bring closer the *Geulah*, as per the promise of Melech HaMoshiach to the Baal Shem Tov, that "when your springs spread outward, the Master will come, this is Melech HaMoshiach," and as it is in the Zohar, that "through this composition of yours (of Rashbi), etc., they will go out through it from exile with mercy."

## With the help of Heaven. Shabbos Parshas Emor, 19 Iyar, 5737

"Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah." The Rebbe Maharash writes in his maamar on this verse that according to what is stated in the Siddur, Shaar Lag B’Omer, the allusion in the Torah to Lag B’Omer is in the verse, "This heap [gal] is a witness." Thus, the verse "Open [gal] my eyes" is also explained in relation to Lag B’Omer. And according to the known principle that all matters in the Torah are precise to the ultimate degree, we must say that the fact that the matter of Lag B’Omer in the verse "Open my eyes" is learned from the matter of Lag B’Omer in the verse "This heap is a witness" is because in order to arrive at the matter of "Open my eyes," one must go through "This heap is a witness."

2) Now, the connection of this verse to Lag B’Omer—the day of the passing of Rashbi (in the simple sense)—is that the essence of Rashbi is that through him the revelation of the *pnimiyus haTorah* (inner dimension of Torah) occurred. [As is explained in many places, the revelation of the *nigleh* (revealed) Torah was through Moshe Rabbeinu, and the revelation of the *pnimiyus haTorah* was through Rashbi.] The primary revelation took place on the day of his passing, as is stated in the Zohar, that on the day of his passing he revealed to his students "holy words" that he had not revealed throughout his entire life (because the matters he revealed then were of such an exalted nature that even Rashbi had previously been afraid to reveal them). And through the writing down of these "holy words" (according to his instruction), they were subsequently revealed to all of Klal Yisrael, to the point that they were revealed in a manner of *yitparnesun*—through understanding and comprehension. This revelation (the revelation to all of Klal Yisrael and in a manner of *yitparnesun*) also took place on the day of Rashbi’s passing (through the writing of the "holy words" at that time, according to his instruction). To add: according to the known principle that on the day of a person’s passing, the inner dimension of all the *avodah* he performed throughout his life is revealed, it is understood that on the day of Rashbi’s passing, the inner dimension of the secrets he had revealed throughout his life was also revealed. And every year, on Lag B’Omer, the revelation that occurred on the first Lag B’Omer is reawakened—the revelation of the *pnimiyus haTorah* (the Torah of Rashbi). This is also understood from what is explained in many places, that Lag B’Omer is the *Matan Torah* (giving of the Torah) of the *pnimiyus haTorah*, similar to the festival of Shavuos, which is the time of *Matan Torah* in general. From this it is understood that just as *Matan Torah* on Shavuos occurs every single year, so too, the *Matan Torah* of the *pnimiyus haTorah* on Lag B’Omer occurs every single year. This is why the matter of Lag B’Omer is alluded to in the verse, "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah," for the "wonders" in the Torah are the *pnimiyus haTorah*, and the matter of "Open [gal]... wonders" is the revelation (*gal* from the word *gilui*) of the wonders of the Torah (*pnimiyus haTorah*) which is drawn down in *G"L* (Lag B’Omer).

3) The maamar explains that there are two levels in the "wonders of the Torah" (*pnimiyus haTorah*). The matter is as stated in the Zohar: in the Torah there is a garment, a body, a soul, and a soul of the soul. The *nigleh* of the Torah is the garment and the body of the Torah [the stories of the Torah are the garment, and the *halachos* of the Torah are the body], and the soul of the Torah is the *pnimiyus haTorah*, which is divided generally into two levels: *razin d’oraysa* (hidden) — the soul; and *razin d’razin* (the hidden of all hidden) — the soul of the soul. The request "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah" (wonders in the plural) is that he should see both levels of the wonders of the Torah: the *razin* and the *razin d’razin*. It explains that the soul is the level of *nefesh-ruach-neshamah*, and the soul of the soul is the level of *chayah*. Perhaps it can be said that the matters of *pnimiyus haTorah* that Rashbi revealed throughout his life are the level of the soul of the Torah, and the matters he revealed on the day of his passing are the level of the soul of the soul. Now, in the *Reshimot* of the Tzemach Tzedek on Tehillim, regarding the verse "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah," he cites the aforementioned Zohar passage that in the Torah there is a garment, a body, a soul, and a soul of the soul, and he explains that the request "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders" is that he should see not only the garment, but also the soul. Further on, he brings the interpretation of the Shelah that "behold wonders" refers to the *kavanah* (intent) of the *mitzvos*. After bringing the interpretation of the Shelah, he continues to explain that "wonders" refers to the *pnimiyus haTorah*, the soul of the Torah. One must understand: seemingly, he should have first finished explaining the interpretation that "wonders" refers to the soul of the Torah, *and only afterwards* brought *another* interpretation that "wonders" refers to the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos*. The fact that he brings this interpretation in the middle of the explanation that "wonders" refers to the soul of the Torah implies that the interpretation that "wonders" refers to the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* is relevant to the explanation and understanding of the interpretation that "behold wonders" means to see the soul of the Torah.

4) This can be understood by prefacing the request "Open my eyes," that he asks Hashem to open his eyes. Seemingly, since the reason that one does not ordinarily see the wonders of the Torah is (not due to a lack of vessels, but) because the *wonders* themselves are covered and hidden [it can be said that the concealment of the level of the soul of the Torah is because it is clothed in the body of the Torah (*nigleh* of the Torah), which conceals the soul; and the concealment of the level of the soul of the soul of the Torah is (not that it is clothed in something that conceals it, but) that by its very nature it is in a state of concealment, a concealment that is above revelation], he should have asked that Hashem reveal the *wonders of the Torah*. Why is the request "Open *my eyes*," that Hashem should open his eyes? The point of the explanation is based on the Midrash, which gives a parable of a king who decreed: "The inhabitants of Rome shall not descend to Syria, and the inhabitants of Syria shall not ascend to Rome." So too, when Hashem created the world, He decreed: "The heavens are the heavens of Hashem, and the earth He gave to the children of man." When He wished to give the Torah, He annulled the decree and said: "The lower realms shall ascend to the upper realms, and the upper realms shall descend to the lower realms; and I am the one who begins," as it is written, "And Hashem descended upon Mount Sinai," and it is written, "And to Moshe He said, 'Ascend to Hashem.'" It is explained elsewhere that the reason He says "The lower realms shall ascend to the upper realms" before "the upper realms shall descend to the lower realms"—even though the descent of the upper realms below occurred first—is because "I am the one who begins," and the ultimate intent of *Matan Torah* is that the lower realms shall ascend to the upper realms [that they should become vessels for the revelation of *Elokus*, and furthermore, that the drawing down of the revelation within them should be from their own perspective and through their own *avodah*]. However, in order for the ascent of the lower realms to the upper realms to be possible, it is through the descent of the upper realms to the lower realms, which is the beginning and the empowerment for the ascent of the lower realms to the upper realms. And it is similar regarding the *Matan Torah* of the *pnimiyus haTorah* on Lag B’Omer: the intent in the annulment of the decree and the partition between the upper and lower realms regarding the *pnimiyus haTorah* is that the revelation of the *pnimiyus haTorah* that is drawn down to the person should be (also) from the perspective of the person. This is "Open *my eyes*, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah"—that Hashem should open the eyes of the person, and the person should see the wonders in the Torah (not only by the wonders being in a state of revelation, but also) from the perspective of the person.

Based on this, we can explain what is stated in the maamar that the interpretation of "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders *from Your Torah*" is that the seeing of the wonders of the Torah (the soul of the Torah and the soul of the soul) should be *from Your Torah*, from the *nigleh* of the Torah. Seemingly, the main thing is that he should see the wonders of the Torah; what is the advantage in seeing them through the matters of the *nigleh* of the Torah? The explanation for this is that all matters in the world and in man have their beginning in the Torah. Therefore, by the seeing of the wonders of the Torah (of the person) being through these matters of the *nigleh* of the Torah—meaning that the body and the garment of the Torah *themselves* reveal the soul and the soul of the soul of the Torah—thereby it is brought about similarly in the person who engages in Torah: that his seeing of the wonders of the Torah is (also) from the perspective of the person.

5) The two aforementioned matters regarding the annulment of the decree and the partition between the upper and lower realms that were innovated at *Matan Torah* are, in general, Torah and *mitzvos*. The matter of the descent of the upper realms to the lower realms that was innovated at *Matan Torah* is primarily in Torah, and the matter of the ascent of the lower realms to the upper realms that was innovated at *Matan Torah* is primarily in *mitzvos*. The matter is that one of the differences between Torah and *mitzvos* is that Torah is the *word of Hashem*, the wisdom of Hashem, which descended below and was clothed in human intellect so that man could understand and comprehend it. The *mitzvos*, however, which are commands *to man*, the intent and the objective in them is (not the command itself, but) the *fulfillment* of the command *that man* performs. This is why the matter of the descent of the upper realms to the lower realms is primarily in Torah: for the Torah, which is the wisdom of Hashem, its place is above, and the fact that the Torah was given below and clothed in human intellect and physical things is the matter of the descent of the upper realms to the lower realms (whereas in the giving of the *mitzvos* below, the matter of the descent of the upper realms to the lower realms is not as emphasized, for on the contrary, the essence of the *mitzvos* (commands) is *that man* should fulfill them). And the matter of the lower realms ascending to the upper realms is primarily in *mitzvos*. For in Torah, since even after it has descended below it remains the *word of Hashem*, therefore, even in the study and engagement in Torah by man, what is primarily felt is the drawing down from above—that the Torah, which is the wisdom of Hashem, is drawn down and clothed in the intellect of man, and not the clarification and refinement of the intellect of man. The matter of the lower realms ascending to the upper realms is emphasized primarily in *mitzvos*. Since the essence of the *mitzvos* (commands) is *that man* should fulfill the *mitzvos* (as above), and the intent in this is to refine the creatures, to clarify, refine, and elevate the powers and limbs of the person who fulfills the *mitzvah*, and also the things in the world with which one fulfills the *mitzvah*, and to make them vessels for *Elokus*, the essence of the *mitzvos* is the ascent of the lower realms to the upper realms. Based on what was explained above (section 4) that the primary intent in the annulment of the decree of *Matan Torah* is that the lower realms should ascend to the upper realms, and likewise regarding the annulment of the decree and the partition regarding the *pnimiyus haTorah* ("Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah"), that the essence is the ascent from below to above, we must say that "behold wonders from Your Torah" also includes the matter of the *mitzvos* as they are from the perspective of the soul of the Torah. Therefore, the Tzemach Tzedek brings the interpretation of the Shelah that "wonders from Your Torah" are the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos*. The matter is that for the fulfillment of the *mitzvos* to be as it should be, it is through the study of Torah, for "study leads to action." It can be said that for the action of the *mitzvah* (the body of the *mitzvah*) to be as it should be, it is through the study of the *nigleh* of the Torah (the body of the Torah), and for the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* (the soul of the *mitzvos*) to be as it should be, it is through the study of the *pnimiyus haTorah* (the soul of the Torah).

6) Just as in the study of Torah (even in the *nigleh* of the Torah, and certainly in the *pnimiyus haTorah*) there is an endless multitude of levels, so too it is in the fulfillment of the *mitzvos*, even in the action of the *mitzvos* and certainly in the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos*, where there is a multitude of levels. Based on this, one can understand what is known, that from the action of the *mitzvos* garments are made for the soul in *Gan Eden HaTachton*, and from the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* garments are made in *Gan Eden HaElyon*. Seemingly, even in *Gan Eden HaTachton* itself, and likewise in *Gan Eden HaElyon* itself, there is a multitude of levels, so how is it possible that in all the levels there are the same garments? The explanation for this is that in the action of the *mitzvos* and also in the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* there is a multitude of levels, as mentioned above.

Now, although the *kavanah* of the *mitzvah* is the soul of the *mitzvah* which belongs generally to the soul of the Torah, nevertheless, many matters of the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* are also in the *nigleh* of the Torah. Both regarding the general *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* (which is the same in all *mitzvos*) to fulfill the will of Hashem—this *kavanah* is also according to the *nigleh* of the Torah (only that through the study of the *pnimiyus haTorah*, the *kavanah* and the desire of the person to fulfill the will of Hashem is in a more exalted manner)—and also the specific *kavanot* in each *mitzvah* in particular: the reason and the *kavanah* of many *mitzvos* are explained also in the *nigleh* of the Torah, even according to the way of *peshat* [to the point that the reason for many *mitzvos* is explicit in the verses], and certainly according to the way of *remez* and *derush*. However, the reason and the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* according to the way of *sod* (*pnimiyus haTorah*) is much more exalted, and thereby depth, etc., is added also to the reason according to the *nigleh*. It can be said that just as regarding the seeing of the wonders of the Torah, the request "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah" is that the seeing of the wonders of the Torah (the soul and the soul of the soul) should be *from Your Torah*, from those matters in the *nigleh* of the Torah, from the body of the Torah and also from the garment of the Torah (as above, section 4), so too it is regarding the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos*: that the wonders of the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* [the *kavanot* of the Arizal, and higher] must be seen from within the *kavanot* according to the way of *peshat*, and furthermore—from within the *kavanot* according to the way of *peshat* that are understood by a five-year-old child [which are like the garment of the Torah, the stories of the Torah, which even small children learn].

7) To add: regarding the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos*, this matter [that the most exalted matters should be known from these matters as they are in their simplicity] is even more relevant than in Torah. For the advantage in this—that the knowledge of the wonders is from the matters that are in their simplicity—is that thereby the knowledge of the wonders of the person will be also from the perspective of *his* person (as above, section 4). And since the essence of the *mitzvos* is to refine the creatures through them (as above, section 5), to effect clarification and refinement in the person who fulfills the *mitzvah* [for the essence of the clarification and refinement is through the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos*, and the more exalted the *kavanah*, the more exalted the clarification and refinement], therefore, in the matter of the *mitzvos*, it is even more relevant that the knowledge of the wonders of the *kavanah* of the *mitzvos* should be from within the *kavanot* that are in their simplicity. Based on this, one can explain what the Tzemach Tzedek brings in the maamar *Shoresh Mitzvas HaTefillah*, the expression: "I pray according to the knowledge of this child." Seemingly, in the maamar *Shoresh Mitzvas HaTefillah*, he explains the matter of prayer [in general, and also several specific matters of prayer] according to Kabbalah, and even more—according to Chassidus. What place is there in this maamar to bring the matter of "I pray according to the knowledge of this child"? It can be said that by this he hints that the *kavanot* of prayer at the most exalted levels must be connected with the matter of prayer as it is according to the knowledge of a child. Both regarding the general matter of prayer—that the knowledge of the matter of prayer at the most exalted levels should be also from the matter of prayer as a child understands it—and regarding the explanation of the matters (the verses, requests, etc.) that one says in prayer, that the most exalted explanations must be in accordance with [and to the point that their knowledge should be from within] the interpretation of the words of prayer as a child understands them. And although this matter is in all matters of the Torah, nevertheless, since the matter of prayer is an elevation from below to above, a clarification and refinement of *the person*, therefore in prayer this matter is more relevant (similar to what was explained above regarding *mitzvos*). Therefore, in the maamar *Shoresh Mitzvas HaTefillah*, the matter of "I pray according to the knowledge of this child" is brought.

8) This is the connection between the two verses in which the matter of Lag B’Omer is alluded to: "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah" and "This heap is a witness." For the heap between Lavan and Yaakov is similar to the decree and the partition between the upper and lower realms that existed before *Matan Torah*. It is explained in the Siddur, Shaar Lag B’Omer, that in the heap between Lavan and Yaakov, there are two matters: the heap between the upper Lavan (who is above Yaakov) and Yaakov, which is the *parsah* (partition) between the worlds of *Ein Sof* that are above *Atzilus* and *Atzilus*; and the heap between Yaakov and the lower Lavan, the *parsah* between *Atzilus* and *Beriah-Yetzirah-Asiyah*. The allusion of Lag B’Omer in this verse is that through Rashbi, the heap and the partition were clarified—the partition between the upper Lavan and Yaakov, and also the partition between Yaakov and the lower Lavan. Meaning, that even in *Beriah-Yetzirah-Asiyah*, and even in the world of *Asiyah*, there will be a revelation of *Atzilus* and also a revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *Atzilus*. And this matter is also the content of the second verse that alludes to Lag B’Omer, "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah," that even in the matters of the *nigleh* of the Torah (*Beriah-Yetzirah-Asiyah*), and even in the garment of the Torah, in the stories of the Torah (*Asiyah*), there will be a revelation of the wonders of the Torah, the level of the soul of the Torah (*Atzilus*) and also the level of the soul of the soul (above *Atzilus*).

9) To add: according to the known principle that all matters in the Torah are precise to the ultimate degree, we must say that the fact that the verse "This heap is a witness" [which speaks of the matter of *the heap and the partition* between Lavan and Yaakov] alludes to Lag B’Omer, whose essence is the *clarification* of the heap and the partition, is because the fact that the sign and testimony for "I will not pass to you... you will not pass to me" was that they made a heap, and not that they made a wall, is to hint that the making of the partition from the outset is in a manner that there is room for "I *will* pass to you and you *will* pass to me." That is, the intent in the making of the partition between the upper and lower realms from the outset is so that afterwards there will be the *connection* of the upper and lower realms. And the fact that at first there had to be a partition between the upper and lower realms is because the intent of "a dwelling place in the lower realms" is that the dwelling place should be in the lowest realm, below which there is no lower realm. Therefore, the creation of the lower realms was through a partition, where not only is the revelation of the upper realms not actually shining in them, but there is a *partition* between them, which is a sign and testimony that "I will *not* pass to you and you will *not* pass to me," so that through the *avodah* of Yisrael there will be the connection of even these lower realms [for which there is a *decree* that they should not connect with the upper realms] with the upper realms. This is why the allusion of Lag B’Omer is in the verse "This heap is a witness," for the fact that Rashbi clarified the heap and the partition is that through him the inner matter of the *Tzimtzum* itself was revealed, that the intent in the *Tzimtzum* [which is its inner matter] is for the sake of the revelation.

This is why the allusion of Lag B’Omer in the verse "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah" is learned from the allusion of Lag B’Omer in the verse "This heap is a witness," for the primary innovation and elevation of "Open my eyes, that I may behold wonders from Your Torah" is that the seeing of the wonders of the Torah should be (not only because *the wonders* will be in a state of revelation, but) through "Open *my eyes*," that Hashem should open the eyes of the person and the person should see the wonders of the Torah from the perspective of the person (as above, section 4). And in order for the lower person to be able to see the wonders of the Torah from his perspective, it is through the matter of Lag B’Omer alluded to in "This heap is a witness"—the revelation of the inner matter of the *Tzimtzum*, that the *Tzimtzum* is for the sake of the revelation.

10) Through the study and dissemination of the *pnimiyus haTorah*, the Torah of Rashbi, and especially as it has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus—a revelation in a manner of *yitparnesun*, understanding and comprehension, to the point that the intellect of the person studying becomes one with the matter he is studying [which is similar to what was explained above regarding the matter of "Open my eyes," that the seeing of the wonders of the Torah is from the perspective of the person himself]—one merits the revelation of the *pnimiyus haTorah* that will be in the future, when the revelation then will be in addition to that which is from above, but rather it will be the essence of the world, as the Rambam says: "The entire world will be occupied only with knowing Hashem alone, etc.," as it is said, "For the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea," in the true and complete Redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, very soon.

## In the month of the third [exodus of the children of Israel from the land of Egypt, on this day they came to the wilderness of Sinai]. The Gemara states: It is written here, "On this day they came to the wilderness of Sinai," and it is written there, "This month shall be for you the head of months." Just as there it refers to Rosh Chodesh, so too here it refers to Rosh Chodesh. The Tosafos raise a difficulty: In Maseches Pesachim, it is stated (regarding the verse, "Hashem spoke to Moshe in the wilderness of Sinai in the second year... in the first month") that we derive "wilderness" from "wilderness." It is written here, "in the wilderness of Sinai," and it is written there, "Hashem spoke to Moshe in the wilderness of Sinai in the Tent of Meeting on the first of the second month." Just as there it refers to Rosh Chodesh, so too here it refers to Rosh Chodesh. Since the verse "in the third month" also contains the word "wilderness," why do we not derive it here from "wilderness" to "wilderness"? It must be understood that, on the face of it, the derivation from "this day" reveals that the meaning of "on this day" is Rosh Chodesh. This is unlike the derivation from "wilderness" to "wilderness," where the lesson is only that the matter mentioned in the verse (they came to the wilderness of Sinai) took place on Rosh Chodesh, which would not explain the meaning of "on this day." [Furthermore, this derivation from "this day" has an advantage over the derivation from "wilderness" to "wilderness," because "this month" means Rosh Chodesh, whereas "wilderness of Sinai" and "the first of the month" (Rosh Chodesh) are two separate concepts]. What, then, is the reasoning of the Tosafos that it should have been derived from "wilderness" to "wilderness" ?

2) This can be understood based on what is explained in *Torah Or*, in the discourse titled *Zeh*. The essence of receiving the Torah is as it is written (at the beginning of the matter of *Matan Torah*), "And Elokim spoke all these words, saying." The meaning of the word "saying" (*le'emor*) in this verse is that at the time of *Matan Torah*, the power was given to every individual among the Jewish people so that their Torah study—["all these words," which refers also to the entire Torah]—would be in the manner of "saying," to repeat the words that Hashem says. As it is written, "May my tongue answer Your word," for the Torah is "Your word," and my tongue answers as one who responds to the speaker. This is the connection between *Matan Torah* and the festival of Shavuos, for on Shavuos, the *Keter* is drawn down [as is known regarding the interpretation of the verse, "You shall count fifty days," that on Shavuos the fiftieth gate of *Binah* is drawn down, and the fiftieth gate is the *Keter*]. The fact that at *Matan Torah* the power was given for the Torah study of a Jew to be in a state of absolute *bitul*—that he is not an independent existence at all and is only like one who answers the caller—is because at *Matan Torah*, the revelation of *Keter* is drawn down.

This is "In the third month... on this day they came to the wilderness of Sinai." On Rosh Chodesh Sivan, there was the preparation for receiving the Torah, for on Rosh Chodesh is the birth of the moon, at which time (at the moment of the birth) it is merely a point, and this indicates its *bitul*, for it has nothing of its own. It is the same with the Jewish people, who resemble and count by the moon ; Rosh Chodesh is the time suited to reveal the power of *bitul* that exists within every Jew. Therefore, on Rosh Chodesh Sivan, there is the preparation for receiving the Torah, so that a person's Torah study will be in *bitul*, like one who answers the caller.

3) The *maamar* continues with further explanation as to why Rosh Chodesh Sivan is the preparation for receiving the Torah. When the Jewish people left Egypt, they began counting *Sefiras HaOmer*, which is a preparation for receiving the Torah. The seven weeks of *Sefiras HaOmer* ("Seven weeks shall you count") correspond to the seven *midos*. On Rosh Chodesh Sivan, which is the third day of the seventh week, there are already the three *midos* of *Chagas* (which are the primary *midos*) of the *Sefirah* of *Malchus*, as if the entire seventh week is already present. Therefore, on Rosh Chodesh Sivan, they came to the wilderness of Sinai, for then the primary preparation for *Matan Torah* was completed.

It can be said that the reason the *maamar* explains two reasons why Rosh Chodesh Sivan is the preparation for receiving the Torah is because there are two aspects to the preparation for receiving the Torah: the perfection of the person, for in order to be worthy of receiving the Torah, one must be in a state of absolute perfection ; and his *bitul*. Since the Torah is infinitely higher than man (all the more so because it is higher than the supernal worlds), therefore, when a person is an "existence," his existence is (like) the opposite of the Torah. It is through his *bitul* that a person receives the Torah. Therefore, the *maamar* explains that the reason they came to the wilderness of Sinai on Rosh Chodesh Sivan and were prepared to receive the Torah is that on Rosh Chodesh Sivan, both aspects are present: the fact that Rosh Chodesh is the birth of the moon, at which time it is merely a point—*bitul*; and the fact that on Rosh Chodesh Sivan, the primary *midos* were completed (also) in the *Sefirah* of *Malchus*—the perfection of the person. There are two aspects to the perfection of the person achieved through *Sefiras HaOmer*: *Sefiras HaOmer* is the matter of refining and purifying the *midos*, and through *Sefiras HaOmer*, the forty-nine gates of *Binah* are drawn down.

4) This is "On this day they came to the wilderness of Sinai." *Torah Or* explains that "wilderness" (*midbar*) is from the word *dibbur* (speech), and it is called *midbar* with the addition of a *mem* (for *mem* is one of the letters of *amen-tiv*, which indicate the diminution of a matter) to indicate that the speech is in *bitul*. [This matter also applies to the wilderness in its literal sense, "a land not sown", for when speech is based on one's own intellect and will, the intellect and will are the "sowing" through which the speech grows. The matter of the wilderness as "a land not sown" means that the Torah speech one speaks is the word of Hashem and is not sown from one's own intellect and will]. And "Sinai" is where hatred descended to the nations of the world, and it is that the Torah is called *tushiyah* because it weakens the power of the *Sitra Achra*. It can be said that the reason they came to the wilderness on "this day" is because on this day (Rosh Chodesh), the moon [and likewise the Jewish people who count by the moon] is merely a point. And the fact that on "this day" they came to Sinai, where hatred descended to the *Yetzer Hara*, is because on this day (Rosh Chodesh Sivan), the refinement of the primary *midos* of the animal soul was completed.

5) It must be understood that according to the above, the fact that on Rosh Chodesh Sivan the primary *midos* were completed is an explanation of the perfection of the person, while in the discourse in *Torah Or*, this is linked to the fact that Rosh Chodesh is the birth of the moon—the matter of *bitul*. The explanation for this is that the matter of the birth of the moon is (not its *bitul*, but on the contrary) that its existence is born through the light of the sun being drawn into it. The fact that at the time of the birth it is only a point, which indicates its *bitul* (as above), is that the *existence* of the moon—its light—is in a state of *bitul*, for it is felt that the light within it is not its own, and it only receives from the sun. It is the same with the Jewish people who count by the moon: the matter of the *bitul* of Rosh Chodesh in the *avodah* of man is that his *existence* is in *bitul*. [It can be said that this is what is explained in *Torah Or*, that the reason Rosh Chodesh is called "this day" (simply) is because "this day" is love that is in the state of *bitul* [for "day" is light, as it is written, "And Elokim called the light Day," and love is light, and this is *Yichuda Ila'ah* (which is why Moshe Rabbeinu prophesied with "this"), and "this day" is love that is in the state of *bitul* of *Yichuda Ila'ah*]. For the reason that love is called by the name "light" is because love is expansion [and therefore fear, which is contraction (the opposite of expansion), is called by the name "darkness"], and the *bitul* of Rosh Chodesh is not in a manner that he is nullified from his existence (like the *bitul* of fear), but rather, that his expansion (existence) is in *bitul*]. Based on this, the connection between the two matters on Rosh Chodesh Sivan (the *bitul* of the moon and the completion of the primary *midos*) can be explained, for the matter of the birth of the moon is that the existence is in *bitul*.

6) Based on this, we can explain what the Tosafos asked, that it should have been derived from "wilderness" to "wilderness," for the fact that on Rosh Chodesh Sivan they came to the level of "wilderness" is a higher *bitul* than the fact that Rosh Chodesh is called "this day." The matter of "this day" is that it is in existence (even to the existence of expansion, "day"), but its existence is in *bitul* ("this"). This is unlike the fact that on Rosh Chodesh Sivan they came to the level of "wilderness" [where his speech is not his own speech at all, and the person's mouth is only a vessel for the word of Hashem speaking through him, similar to the word of Hashem in the mouth of the prophet], which is the *bitul* of existence. Since the primary excellence of Rosh Chodesh Sivan is that through it they came to the *bitul* of "wilderness," there is room to say that the hint in the verse (that this was on Rosh Chodesh Sivan) is in the word "wilderness". The fact that the derivation in the Gemara is from "this day" is because the matter of "this day" on Rosh Chodesh Sivan is higher even than the *bitul* of "wilderness," as follows.

7) This can be understood based on what our Sages said : When the Jewish people preceded "we will do" to "we will hear," the ministering angels came and tied two crowns to each of the Jewish people, one for "we will do" and one for "we will hear." There is a well-known precision regarding this : at the beginning of the statement, "When they preceded...," it implies that the fact that they tied two crowns for them is because they preceded "we will do" to "we will hear," while at the end of the statement it says, "one for 'we will do' and one for 'we will hear'," meaning the two crowns are for "we will do" and "we will hear" themselves. The Admur Maharash explains in his discourse titled *B'Sha'ah SheHikdimu*, which was said on *Parshas Bamidbar* one hundred years ago, that "we will do" is the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, and "we will hear" is the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos*. The matter of preceding "we will do" to "we will hear" is like the statement of our Sages : "Why does the *parsha* of *Shema* precede 'And if you will listen'? So that he may accept upon himself the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven first, and afterwards accept upon himself the yoke of *mitzvos*." He explains there that the first *parsha* is *ratzo* and *mesiras nefesh*, and the second *parsha* is *shov*. Just as in *ratzo* and *shov*, first there must be *ratzo* and afterwards *shov*, so too it is in the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven and the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos*: before the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos*, there must be the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven. Based on this, he explains there what our Sages said, "When they preceded...," even though the two crowns are for "we will do" and "we will hear" themselves [one for "we will do" and one for "we will hear"], because in order for there to be *shov* ("we will hear"), there must be the precedence of *ratzo* ("we will do"). The *maamar* continues there that the fact that they tied two crowns for them (specifically crowns) is because a crown is a *makif*, a surrounding light, and there are two matters in this: the ascent of the *memale* into the *sovev*, and the drawing down of the *sovev* into the *memale*. Seemingly, the intention is that the crown corresponding to "we will do" (*ratzo*) is the ascent of the *memale* into the *sovev*, and the crown corresponding to "we will hear" (*shov*) is the drawing down of the *sovev* into the *memale*.

It can be said that the reason the *maamar* adds that the fact that they tied two crowns for them is because a crown is a *makif* and *sovev*, is because from the language of our Sages, "When they preceded..., they tied two crowns for them," it implies that the precision of "preceded 'we will do' to 'we will hear'" relates to the two crowns corresponding to "we will do" and "we will hear" (and not to "we will do" and "we will hear" themselves). Therefore, it explains that a crown is a *makif* and *sovev*, and the two crowns are two matters in *sovev*, for this is the explanation of why the tying of the two crowns was specifically because they preceded "we will do" to "we will hear" (as follows). The fact that it explains beforehand that the reason one must precede "we will do" to "we will hear" is because "we will do" and "we will hear" are like *ratzo* and *shov*, is an explanation of why the crown corresponding to "we will hear" (*makif* and *sovev*) is specifically when "we will hear" is after the precedence of "we will do." For *makif* and *sovev* are above connection to the worlds, and since "we will hear" is *shov* and drawing down below, therefore, the fact that they tied a crown (*makif* and *sovev* that is above the world) corresponding to "we will hear" (drawing down below) is specifically because "we will hear" was after the precedence of "we will do", *ratzo* and ascent above.

8) It requires explanation: is not the general matter of acceptance of the yoke, including the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos* ("we will hear"), *bitul*? For the matter of *bitul* is that the person *exits* his existence, and the tying of the crown corresponding to "we will hear" is specifically when "we will hear" is after the precedence of "we will do." The explanation for this can be said based on what is known that the *makif* of *Keter* is the true *makif* that is above the *makif* that belongs to the *pnim*. This is the difference between *Keter* and *Galgalta*: *Galgalta* is a *makif* that belongs to the *pnim*, similar to the skull in a person which surrounds the brain but belongs to it, for the skull is of his body just like the brain. The *makif* of *Keter* is a *makif* that is above connection to the *pnim*, similar to a crown in the literal sense, which is a diadem on the brain and the skull and has no connection to them (for the crown is of precious stones and not of the person's body). And even though *Galgalta* is also called by the name *Keter*, this is in a general sense, but the essence of *Keter* is the *makif* that does not belong to the *pnim* (which is above the *makif* of *Galgalta*). This is similar to the will in a person, for will is a *makif* over all the powers and limbs, and it has two levels. The will to do some thing through his powers—this will, while it rules over all the powers because it is a *makif* over them, nevertheless, the very fact that he wants the action of the powers is proof that it has a connection to them—this is the *makif* of *Galgalta*. And it is a descent regarding the will itself as it is above the will to do—the *makif* of *Keter*.

Now, the fact that on the festival of Shavuos there is the drawing down of *Keter* is the true *Keter* that is above *Galgalta*. It is the same regarding the two crowns corresponding to "we will do" and "we will hear" that were drawn down on the eve of the festival of Shavuos, for they are *makifim* that are above connection to the *pnim*. Based on this, we can explain that the tying of the crown corresponding to "we will hear" was specifically through the fact that they preceded "we will do" to "we will hear," because the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos* is *bitul* to the will, and since the *mitzvos* are commands *to the person*, the will of the *mitzvos* is similar to the will (of the person) who wants to do through his powers—the *makif* of *Galgalta*. And the drawing down of the *makif* of *Keter* (a *makif* that is above connection to the *pnim*) is through the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, *bitul* to the Master of the Will. And through the fact that they preceded "we will do" to "we will hear," they tied two crowns for them, one for "we will do" and one for "we will hear," because through the precedence of "we will do" to "we will hear," even the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos* is in a manner where it is felt in revelation that it comes from the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, *bitul* to the Master of the Will.

The explanation of the elevation that is achieved in the *bitul* of "we will hear" through the precedence of the *bitul* of "we will do" can be understood by prefacing that just as above, the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos* is to the will (revelation) and the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven is to the Master of the Will, so too it is regarding the person: the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos* is in the revelations of the person, that he will do everything he is commanded, and more than that, that this (to do what he is commanded) will be his will; and the *bitul* of the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven is in the person himself, that his entire existence is that he is a servant of the King, the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He. From this it is understood that the excellence of the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven over the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos* is also in the matter of *bitul*. For in the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos*, there remains with him and in him an existence that is not nullified, and the true *bitul* is in the acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven, that his entire existence is that he is the servant of the King. [And the fact that there must (also) be the acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos* is so that the *bitul* will be (also) regarding the actual fulfillment of the Torah and *mitzvos*]. And when the *bitul* of "we will hear" (acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos*) is after and through the precedence of the *bitul* of "we will do" (acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven), where it is felt *in revelation* that the fact that he accepts upon himself to do everything he is commanded is because he himself is the servant of the King, then, even the *bitul* of "we will hear" is in his entire existence (regarding his revealed powers), and it is not possible at all that he will not fulfill the command.

9) It should be added that just as in *ratzo* and *shov*, the ultimate intention of the *ratzo* is for the *shov* that follows the *ratzo*, so too it is in "we will do" (acceptance of the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven) and "we will hear" (acceptance of the yoke of *mitzvos*), which are like *ratzo* and *shov*, that the ultimate intention of "we will do" is that it should lead to "we will hear." And through the precedence of "we will do" to "we will hear," where "we will do" is a preparation for "we will hear," an elevation is added to "we will do." For the *bitul* of "we will do" (from its own side), while it is the *bitul* of *mesiras nefesh*, which is the highest *bitul*, is the *bitul* of the person, and through the fact that "we will do" is a preparation for "we will hear" to accept upon himself the yoke of *mitzvos* (*shov* and drawing down below), through this the intention of "the Holy One, blessed be He, desired to have a dwelling place in the lower worlds" is completed, the intention of the *Atzmus*. It should be added that the excellence of "we will hear" through the precedence of "we will do" over the matter of "we will do" itself is also in the *bitul*. For the *bitul* of "we will do" (from its own side) is that *he is not* an existence for himself (and he is only the servant of the King), and through the precedence of "we will do" to "we will hear," where the *bitul* of "we will hear" is like the *bitul* of "we will do," the *bitul* is also in the existence of the person.

10) It is the same regarding the two matters on Rosh Chodesh Sivan, the *bitul* of "this day" and the *bitul* of "wilderness", that the excellence of the *bitul* of "this day" over the *bitul* of "wilderness" is in two matters: that the *bitul* of "this day" (that the love is in *bitul*) is also in the existence of the person; and that through this specifically, the intention of a dwelling place in the lower worlds is completed.

It should be added that the fact that the *bitul* of "this day" is drawn down also into the existence of the person [and even after the *bitul* is drawn down into the existence, it is (not *bitul* of the *yesh*, but) *bitul* in existence] is through the drawing down of the *Atzmus*, for it is in the power of the *Atzmus* to connect two opposites (*bitul* and existence).

It can be said that this is also hinted at in the language of the verse, "on this day." For it is explained in *Likutei Torah* that the word "this" (*zeh*), which is a thing that one can point to with a finger and say "this," is only in the Holy One, blessed be He, as it is written, "This is my G-d and I will glorify Him," for "the whole earth is full of His glory". It is explained elsewhere that the true matter of "this" is only in the *Atzmus*. For the meaning of "this" is that the thing one says "this" about is the existence of the thing, and since all existences, including even the highest revelations, their existence is not from themselves and is dependent on the will of the *Atzmus*, it is impossible to say about them in perfection "this thing," and the true matter of "this" is only in the *Atzmus*. This is "on this day," that even the existence of expansion ("day") is in *bitul* ("this"), and this is through the revelation of the power of the *Atzmus*, "this day."
