It is stated in *Likutei Torah* that the "lamps" are the souls, as it is written, "The lamp of Hashem is the soul of man" [and the seven lamps of the Menorah correspond to the seven types of Bnei Yisrael in their *avodah* of Hashem: one serves out of love that flows like water (Chesed), another out of love like sparks of fire (Gevurah), and so on]. The Tzemach Tzedek explains in his *maamar* on the verse "Vayedaber... Beha'aloscha et haneros," delivered one hundred years ago, that the reason souls are called "lamps" is according to the statement of our Sages: "The Holy One, Blessed be He, said to man: 'My lamp is in your hand, and your lamp is in My hand. My lamp is in your hand—this is the Torah, as it is written, "For the mitzvah is a lamp and the Torah is light." And your lamp is in My hand—this is the soul, as it is written, "The lamp of Hashem is the soul of man." If you guard My lamp, I will guard your lamp; and if you extinguish My lamp, I will extinguish your lamp.'" 

Seemingly, the fact that souls are called "lamps" is explicitly stated in the verse ("The lamp of Hashem is the soul of man"), so why does the *maamar* bring the words of the Midrash, "My lamp is in your hand, and your lamp is in My hand"? Furthermore, we must understand that the verse says "The lamp of *Hashem* is the soul of man"—meaning the soul is Hashem’s lamp—whereas the Midrash says the soul is *your* lamp (man’s) and the Torah is *My* lamp (Hashem’s).

2) This can be explained by prefacing what is explained in many places: the concept of *Beha'aloscha et haneros* is that Aharon arouses the souls so that they will have the desire to ascend upward, even to the point of being included in the *Etzem* of *Ohr Ein Sof*. Although this desire is the nature of the soul—as explained in *Tanya* that the reason souls are called "lamps" is because just as it is the nature of a flame to ascend upward and cleave to its root (the general foundation of fire beneath the sphere of the moon), so too is it the nature of the soul to *ratzo* (run) upward to be included in the source from which it was hewn—nevertheless, Aharon must "kindle" the lamps (the souls), because through the soul’s descent below and its enclothement in a body, its natural *ratzo* is concealed. 

As explained at length in the *derushim*, one of the reasons that the souls of *Atzilus* are called "seed of man" and the souls of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah* are called "seed of the beast" is because for the souls of *Atzilus*, even in their descent below, the nature of *ratzo* and ascent remains in a state of revelation; therefore, they are called "man," after the "spirit of man that ascends upward." The souls of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah* (which are the majority of souls), when they descend below and are enclothed in a body, are drawn after the body and the matters of this physical world; therefore, they are called "beast," after the "spirit of the beast that descends downward." The *avodah* of Aharon is to arouse and reveal the nature of *ratzo* and ascent that exists even in the souls of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*—*Beha'aloscha et haneros*—so that their nature of ascent will be in a state of revelation.

It is explained in the *derushim* that the reason the kindling of the lamps (the revelation of the soul’s nature of *ratzo* and ascent) is performed specifically by Aharon is that regarding Aharon it is written, "Like the precious oil upon the head, running down upon the beard, the beard of Aharon, that runs down upon the hem of his garments." The "beard of Aharon" refers to the *halachos* of the Torah. Through the Torah—"Torah is light"—the soul’s nature of ascent (which exists even while it descends below and is enclothed in a body) is revealed and illuminated, *Beha'aloscha et haneros*. Based on this, we can explain the connection of the Sages' statement (cited in the *maamar*), "If you guard My lamp, I will guard your lamp," to *Beha'aloscha et haneros*. The "guarding of your lamp" (ensuring the lamp is not extinguished) means that the soul’s lamp (its nature of *ratzo* and ascent) remains in a state of revelation. And the fact that the guarding of the soul’s lamp is accomplished through guarding the lamp of Torah is analogous to the concept of *Beha'aloscha et haneros* being accomplished through Aharon, who is Torah.

3) The *maamar* continues by noting that we must understand the statement, "If you guard My lamp, I will guard your lamp"—what is their connection, and why are they dependent upon one another? This is explained based on the Midrash: "By whose merit were the heavens and the earth created, and by whose merit do they stand? By the merit of 'These are the names of the children of Israel,' and these stand by the merit of 'These are the testimonies, the statutes, and the laws.'" The intent is that the creation of the world is for the sake of Yisrael, and the perfection of Yisrael is through the Torah. By Yisrael fulfilling the 613 *mitzvos*, they ascend to a level higher than they were on their own. This is why the soul descends below and is enclothed in a physical body: to fulfill *Torah u'mitzvos*, because through this fulfillment, the soul ascends higher than it was before its descent. This is the meaning of "If you guard My lamp, I will guard your lamp"—the guarding of the soul’s lamp is through guarding the lamp of Torah.

Based on what is written in the *maamar* that the concept of "If you guard My lamp, I will guard your lamp" means that through the fulfillment of *Torah u'mitzvos* the soul ascends higher than it was before its descent, we can say that the reason this is linked to *Beha'aloscha et haneros* is that the concept of *Beha'aloscha et haneros* is also the ascent that is effected in the souls, bringing them to a level higher than they were before their descent below.

4) To explain this in greater detail: we must preface that the precision in the *maamar* regarding why they are dependent on each other is because "guarding My lamp" does not merely mean that a person does not transgress the Torah’s commands, *chas v'shalom*, but that he *fulfills* the Torah’s *mitzvos*. Therefore, it emphasizes why they are dependent on each other. The fact that the guarding of the soul’s lamp is dependent on not transgressing the Torah’s commands is because when one transgresses, *chas v'shalom*, a blemish is made in the soul. The precision in the *maamar* is that the guarding of the soul’s lamp is dependent on the *fulfillment* of the Torah. We should add that the precision in the *maamar* relates primarily to the souls of *Atzilus*. Regarding the souls of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah* (the majority of souls), one could say that the reason the guarding of the soul’s lamp is dependent on the lamp of Torah is that since for them the body and the *nefesh habehamis* conceal and hide the soul, the guarding of their soul’s lamp is through the fulfillment of *Torah u'mitzvos* (as mentioned in section 2). The precision and the question in the *maamar* are primarily regarding the souls of *Atzilus*, for since for them the soul is in a state of revelation, why do they need the lamp of Torah?

Based on this, we must understand what is said in the *maamar*—that the guarding of the soul’s lamp is through guarding the lamp of Torah because "These are the names of the children of Israel" stand by the merit of "These are the testimonies, the statutes, and the laws." Seemingly, the fact that Yisrael stand by the merit of the Torah and *mitzvos* is because through their fulfillment they ascend to a higher level (but the level of the souls of Yisrael as they are on their own does not, seemingly, depend on the Torah). The meaning of "If you guard My lamp, I will guard your lamp" is that the guarding of the soul’s lamp (the level of the soul’s lamp as it is on its own) is through guarding the lamp of Torah. This can be explained based on the passage in the *Zohar* cited in *Tanya*: just as the light of a candle does not shine and catch onto the wick without oil, so too the *Shechinah* does not rest upon the body of a person (who is compared to a wick) except through good deeds (which are compared to oil). *Tanya* explains that the reason the *Shechinah* rests upon the body of a person specifically through the fulfillment of *mitzvos* (good deeds)—and it is not enough for his soul, which is a "part of Elokus from above," to be the oil for the wick—is that the soul of man, even when he is a complete *tzaddik* serving Hashem with fear and love in *taanug*, is not *batel* in existence completely; rather, he is an entity unto himself who fears and loves Hashem. But the *mitzvos*, which are the inwardness of His will, may He be blessed, have no vitality that is a separate entity at all; rather, they are unified and included in His will, may He be blessed. Therefore, the resting of the *Shechinah* upon the body of a person is specifically through the fulfillment of *mitzvos*, for the concept of the *Shechinah* resting on something is that that thing is included in the light of Hashem and is *batel* to Him in existence completely, for only then does Hashem, who is One, rest, reveal Himself, and shine within it. Based on this, we can explain that the guarding of the soul’s lamp is through guarding the lamp of Torah, because the concept of the soul’s lamp is its *bitul*, and the perfection of the soul’s *bitul* (*bitul* in existence completely) is through the fulfillment of *mitzvos*. This is why the soul’s lamp is called "your lamp" (man’s) and the lamp of *Torah u'mitzvos* is called "My lamp" (Hashem’s): because the *bitul* of the soul (as it is on its own) is not *bitul* in the ultimate sense—"your lamp," the man’s—while the lamp of *mitzvos*, which is the inwardness of His will, may He be blessed, is "My lamp," Hashem’s. And through man fulfilling the *mitzvos*, this *bitul* is drawn into the soul as well: "I will guard your lamp."

5) It should be noted that the aforementioned *maamar* is related to the festival of *Shavuos*. In addition to the fact that the portion of *Beha'aloscha et haneros* (the beginning of the *maamar*) is read after *Shavuos*, it is noted at the beginning of the *maamar* that it is related to the *d'h* "Vayedaber... Anochi." Based on this, we can say that what is explained in the *maamar*—that through the fulfillment of *Torah u'mitzvos* an ascent is effected in the soul—is analogous to the elevation of Yisrael that was renewed at the Giving of the Torah. The point is that the natural love in every Jew to be attached to Hashem and not to be separated from Him, may He be blessed, in any way—even with *mesiras nefesh*—existed even before the Giving of the Torah, for this love is an inheritance from our Patriarchs. Based on what is written in *Tanya* that the fact that the Patriarchs bequeathed this love to every Jew is related to the fact that the Patriarchs themselves are the *Merkavah*—and the concept of the *Merkavah* is that it is *batel* to the Rider [the fact that the movement of the *Merkavah* (the horses) is according to the will of the Rider is not because they want to have a connection to the Rider (like the aforementioned natural love, which is the desire to be attached), but because of their *bitul* to the Rider]—we can say that in the natural love inherited from the Patriarchs, who are the *Merkavah*, there is also included (in a hidden way) the love that is in the category of *bitul*, where one does not want anything for himself at all, and his entire desire is that there be a revelation of Elokus in the world and that the intent of a "dwelling place" for Him, may He be blessed, in the lower realms be completed. 

Now, it is known that love is the love and desire of the person (the lover), and therefore, even when his love and desire are that the Higher Will be fulfilled, the essence of the love and desire is still an entity. The true essence of *bitul* is in the *avodah* of *kabbalas ol*, which is like a servant who has no desires of his own, and everything he does is due to the yoke of the Master placed upon him, by virtue of which he is *compelled* to fulfill the Master’s will. We can say that since the *bitul* of the *Merkavah* to the Rider is not due to the desire of the *Merkavah* (that it wants to be *batel* to the Rider and be a *Merkavah* to Him) but due to the Rider, therefore, in the inheritance from the Patriarchs, who are the *Merkavah*, the *bitul* of *kabbalas ol* is also included in a hidden way. [It is worth noting the statement of our Sages: "When Yisrael preceded 'we will do' to 'we will hear'... who revealed to My children this secret?" It is explained in Chassidus that the fact that they preceded 'we will do' to 'we will hear' [that in the *bitul* of *kabbalas ol* itself there is an extremely lofty *bitul*, a *bitul* to the Master of the Will] is through the revelation of the hidden love, the "secret." We can say that the connection of the hidden *love* (an entity) to the preceding of 'we will do' to 'we will hear' (*bitul* of *kabbalas ol*) is because the hidden love is an inheritance from the Patriarchs, who are the *Merkavah*]. From this it is understood that the *bitul* that was drawn down to Yisrael at the Giving of the Torah (*after* they preceded 'we will do' to 'we will hear') is a *bitul* even loftier than the *bitul* of the *Merkavah*.

6) The matter is as follows: it is stated in the *Gemara* regarding Rabbi Yochanan ben Zakkai that he did not leave out a "great matter" or a "small matter"—the "great matter" being *Maaseh Merkavah*, and the "small matter" being the *Havayos* of Abaye and Rava. It is explained in the *maamar* that the reason *Maaseh Merkavah* is called a "great matter" is that through the *Merkavah*, the person riding upon the *Merkavah* reaches a place he cannot reach on his own. Likewise, it is in the Higher *Merkavah*, where the *Chayos* carry the Throne and the Man upon the Throne to the level of "not a man." The primary ascent to the level of "not a man" is through the *Merkavah* of the Patriarchs (the Patriarchs are the *Merkavah*), the *Merkavah* of *Atzilus*. For the ascent of the level of "man" to the level of "not a man" through the *Merkavah* of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah* is that the man upon the Throne (the man of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*) ascends to a level that is "not a man" relative to the man of *Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah*. Through the *Merkavah* of the Patriarchs, the *Merkavah* of *Atzilus*, the ascent of the level of the man of *Atzilus* to the level of "not a man" that is above *Atzilus*—*Arich*—is effected. This is why *Maaseh Merkavah* is called a "great matter," for man, *Ze'er Anpin*, is a level of smallness and *Tzimtzum*, and through the *Merkavah* he ascends to the level of *Arich*, "great."

The reason the *Havayos* of Abaye and Rava are called a "small matter" (even though they are the essence of the Oral Torah) is that the *halachos* of the Torah were enclothed in small things. In addition to the fact that the *mitzvos* were enclothed in physical things, there are many detailed laws in every *mitzvah*, and the concept of division (details) is *Tzimtzum* and limitation—smallness. [Based on this, the difference between *Maaseh Merkavah* and the *Havayos* of Abaye and Rava is from one extreme to the other. The concept of *Merkavah* is the departure from the level of limitation (man) to the level of the Infinite (not a man)—a "great matter" (for the true essence of greatness is in that which is not limited). In the *Havayos* of Abaye and Rava, it is the opposite: the enclothement of the Higher Will, which is without limit, into limited things—a "small matter"]. The *maamar* continues with another reason why the *Havayos* of Abaye and Rava are called a "small matter": because the Torah of now is a "small matter" relative to the inwardness of the Torah that will be revealed in the future. Also, because the Torah is a parable and a garment for the *Ohr Ein Sof* enclothed within it. Therefore, the Torah is called by the name "the parable of the Ancient One," for it is a parable and a garment for the level of the "Ancient One of the world." A parable relative to the subject of the parable is a "small matter."

We can say that for the reason that the *Havayos* of Abaye and Rava are called a "small matter" because the Torah was enclothed in small things, the title "small matter" is from the perspective of the Torah’s garments. Therefore, it adds that the Torah of now is a "small matter" relative to the inwardness of the Torah that will be revealed in the future [and also that it is a "small matter" relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* enclothed within it], for according to this reason, the title "small matter" is (also) regarding the Torah itself. [Regarding the fact that it also brings the first reason—that the Torah was enclothed in small things—we can say that this reason emphasizes that the difference between the *Havayos* of Abaye and Rava and *Maaseh Merkavah* is a difference from one extreme to the other, as mentioned above].

7) The *maamar* continues with another explanation for why the Torah is called a "small matter": it is on account of the intensity of the *halacha’s* *bitul* relative to the *Ohr Ein Sof* enclothed within it. Just as a person’s Torah study must be in *bitul*—"Answer with my tongue Your word," like one answering after the caller—so too is it regarding the *halachos* of the Torah themselves, which are in *bitul* to the *Ohr Ein Sof* enclothed within them, as follows. [We can say that for the interpretation of "small" as literal, the title "small matter" is in relation to other matters. For the interpretation of "small" as referring to *bitul* [analogous to "I am small from all the kindnesses"], the smallness (*bitul*) is within itself]. We must understand (according to the interpretation that "small" refers to *bitul*): isn't the *Merkavah* also in *bitul* to the Rider, yet it is called a "great matter"? The point of the explanation here is that the *bitul* called by the name "small matter" ("I am small") is that it is completely *batel* and has no merit of its own. As written in *Iggeres HaKodesh* on the *d'h* "Katonti," one considers himself as mere leftovers and surplus, for which there is no need at all. Since the *Merkavah* is an entity, and it even has an advantage relative to the Rider [as mentioned above, that through the *Merkavah* the Rider can reach a place he cannot reach on his own], it is not a "small matter." [On the contrary, because of the elevation within it, through which it is an ascent to the level of "great" (*Arich*), the *Merkavah* is also called by the name "great matter"]. But the *halachos* of the Torah, which were enclothed in physical things [the first interpretation of "small matter"], since physical things have no value relative to His wisdom and will, may He be blessed, which are enclothed within them, they are in the ultimate state of *bitul*—a "small matter." And through the *bitul* that is in the *halachos* of the Torah themselves, the *bitul* is drawn down also to the person who engages in Torah, so that his Torah study will be in the manner of "Answer with my tongue Your word," like one answering after the caller.

8) This is "And G-d spoke all these words, saying." It is explained in Chassidus that at the Giving of the Torah, the power was given for a person’s Torah study to be in the manner of "saying"—to repeat the words that Hashem says, "Answer with my tongue Your word." Seemingly, even before the Giving of the Torah, Yisrael preceded "we will do" to "we will hear," for the preceding of "we will do" to "we will hear" is the *bitul* of the Master of the Will. What, then, is the innovation in the *bitul* of "saying" that was renewed at the Giving of the Torah relative to the *bitul* of "we will do" and "we will hear"? The explanation is that the *bitul* of preceding "we will do" to "we will hear" has a merit. The preceding of "we will do" to "we will hear" is the acceptance of the yoke of Kingship, and through the people accepting the yoke of His Kingship, He becomes King [analogous to the *bitul* of the *Merkavah*, where through the *bitul* of the *Merkavah* to the Rider, it carries and elevates the Rider]. The *bitul* of "saying" that was renewed at the Giving of the Torah is *bitul* in the ultimate sense.

It is the same regarding the fulfillment of *mitzvos*: at the Giving of the Torah, the power was given for a person’s fulfillment of *mitzvos* to be in the ultimate state of *bitul*—meaning that his fulfillment of *mitzvos* would be (not because he accepted upon himself the yoke of the Kingdom of Heaven and the yoke of *mitzvos*, but) because the command of Hashem decrees upon him to be according to the command. As explained in *Likutei Torah* regarding the Ten Commandments that were said at the Giving of the Torah [and the same applies to all the commands in the Torah], which were said in the language of command and also in the future tense, because the speech itself *decrees* upon the person and the world (and compels them) to be so.

We can say that the advantage of the *bitul* of fulfilling *mitzvos* in this manner relative to the *bitul* of *kabbalas ol* is in two respects. The *bitul* of *kabbalas ol* is because *he* accepted upon himself to be a servant of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He. Furthermore, the *bitul* of a servant to his master [even when he is *born* a servant] is connected with his existence, for his essence is that he is a servant of the master. The *bitul* in the aforementioned manner is (not from the perspective of the person’s essence, but) that it is impossible for there to be an existence in the world that is the opposite of *Hashem’s command*.

9) Now, although the *bitul* of Torah study and the fulfillment of *mitzvos* in the aforementioned manner is *bitul* in the ultimate sense, nevertheless, since "Yisrael and the Holy One, Blessed be He, are one," this *bitul* does not negate their existence; on the contrary, this itself is their existence. This is "If you guard My lamp, I will guard your lamp." Although the concept of "I will guard your lamp" is that man arrives at the ultimate state of *bitul* (which is even higher than the *bitul* of the *Merkavah*), nevertheless, this does not negate his existence, but on the contrary, it *guards* your lamp. This is also what Rashi explains on the verse "Beha'aloscha et haneros"—that the flame should rise by itself. Although the ascent of the souls (*Beha'aloscha et haneros*) is that they arrive at the ultimate state of *bitul*, nevertheless, it is in a manner that belongs to the lamps (souls) themselves: the flame rises by itself. That is to say, the Torah study and the fulfillment of *mitzvos* are in *bitul*, and together with this, also in vitality and joy. And since joy breaks through barriers, therefore, through the joy of Torah and the joy of *mitzvah*, all spiritual and physical matters are drawn down without limitations.
